translation: Alif, Lam, Meem, Sad
commentary:
**الٓمٓصٓ**

This Surah begins with the mysterious letters (الحروف المقطعة): Alif-Lam-Meem-Sad. These are the first such letters we encounter in the Quran after having seen Alif-Lam-Meem twice before. Previously, these disconnected letters appeared followed by praise of the Quran itself - "This is a magnificent book" (ذَٰلِكَ الْكِتَابُ). The pattern establishes that when Allah ﷻ opens with these letters, He draws our attention to the miraculous nature of the Book itself, composed of the very same letters the Arabs use daily, yet inimitable in its eloquence and message.

translation: [This is] a Book revealed to you, [O Muhammad] - so let there not be in your breast distress therefrom - that you may warn thereby and as a reminder to the believers
commentary:
**كِتَـٰبٌ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ فَلَا يَكُن فِى صَدْرِكَ حَرَجٌ مِّنْهُ لِتُنذِرَ بِهِۦ وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ**

*This is a Book sent down to you - so let there be no constriction in your chest from it - that you may warn by it and as a reminder to the believers.*

Allah ﷻ addresses the Messenger ﷺ directly, affirming that this is كِتَابٌ - a complete, comprehensive Book that has been revealed to him. The phrase فَلَا يَكُن فِى صَدْرِكَ حَرَجٌ مِّنْهُ carries profound reassurance. The word حَرَجٌ refers to tightness, constriction, or discomfort in the chest. 

What does it mean that the Prophet ﷺ should not feel this discomfort? The most compelling understanding is that the Messenger ﷺ felt the immense weight of this responsibility. He bore the burden of delivering this message to a resistant people, and perhaps felt he was not doing enough justice to the magnitude of what was being revealed to him. Allah ﷻ comforts him: "Don't be overwhelmed by this responsibility. Your task is clear and defined."

The ayah then clarifies this task: لِتُنذِرَ بِهِۦ - "that you may warn by means of it." This echoes the previous Surah, Al-An'am, where Allah ﷻ said, "This Quran has been revealed to me so that I may warn you by it" (6:19). The message is consistent: your role is to warn, not to force belief. Don't carry the burden of people's rejection as though it reflects a failure on your part.

The ayah concludes with a beautiful description: وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - "and a reminder for the believers." The Quran functions as ذِكْرَىٰ, a reminder. This connects to a fundamental Islamic understanding of human nature. According to our teaching, every soul (روح) was created before being placed in the physical body, and all souls engaged in a primordial covenant with Allah ﷻ (referenced later in this very Surah in ayah 172). That original knowledge exists in our subconscious. Revelation doesn't inform us of something completely foreign; it reminds us of what our souls already knew. 

When people are raised in different religious traditions - as Jews, Christians, or fire-worshippers - they become conditioned by their environment. But the Quran comes to revive that original knowledge, to awaken what was always there. This is why the Quran doesn't just bring information; it brings remembrance.

translation: Follow, [O mankind], what has been revealed to you from your Lord and do not follow other than Him any allies. Little do you remember
commentary:
**ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۗ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ**

*Follow what has been sent down to you from your Lord, and do not follow any protectors besides Him. How little you remember!*

Notice the immediate shift in address. The previous ayah spoke to the Messenger ﷺ in the second person singular (إِلَيْكَ - "to you"). Now Allah ﷻ turns to the audience in the plural: ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم - "Follow what has been sent down to you all." 

This is striking because we're still in the Makkan period, and the primary audience consists largely of those who haven't accepted Islam. The ayah doesn't begin with "O you who believe" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا), which would specifically address Muslims. Instead, it addresses everyone - Muslim and non-Muslim alike.

The rhetorical effect is powerful. Allah ﷻ just reassured His Messenger ﷺ: "You're doing your job. Don't be overwhelmed." Then He immediately turns to the actual audience and says, in effect: "The problem isn't with the Messenger. The problem is with you. He's worried because you're not listening, but the fault lies with you, not with him."

The command is clear: ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ - "Follow closely what is being sent down to you from your Rabb." The word رَبّ - Master, Sustainer, the One who owns you and takes care of you - reminds them of their relationship with their Creator.

Then comes the prohibition: وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ - "And do not follow any protectors besides Him." The word أَوْلِيَآء refers to guardians, protectors, or close allies. Don't take anyone else as your guiding authority. Don't follow tribal customs, ancestral traditions, or societal pressures instead of what has been revealed.

The ayah concludes with a stinging rebuke: قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ - "How little you remember!" or "How little effort you make to engage in remembrance!" This is devastating because the previous ayah just described the Quran as ذِكْرَىٰ - a powerful reminder. The Quran doesn't demand extraordinary intellectual gymnastics or superhuman effort. It's a reminder, meant to be accessible. Yet they don't even make the minimal effort required to benefit from it.

translation: And how many cities have We destroyed, and Our punishment came to them at night or while they were sleeping at noon
commentary:
**وَكَم مِّن قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهَا فَجَآءَهَا بَأْسُنَا بَيَـٰتًا أَوْ هُمْ قَآئِلُونَ**

*And how many cities have We destroyed! Our punishment came upon them by night or while they were sleeping at noon.*

The tone shifts dramatically to warning. وَكَم مِّن قَرْيَةٍ - "And how many towns" or "how many cities." The word قَرْيَة can mean a town, city, or settlement. The كَم here indicates an emphatic quantity - countless civilizations that came before.

أَهْلَكْنَـٰهَا - "We destroyed them." Allah ﷻ uses the first-person plural, indicating His Majesty and the certainty of His decree. Then comes the description of how His punishment arrived: فَجَآءَهَا بَأْسُنَا - "Our war came upon them," "Our might came against them." The word بَأْس refers to intense force, military might, or devastating power.

When did it come? بَيَـٰتًا أَوْ هُمْ قَآئِلُونَ - "By night, or while they were taking an afternoon nap." Both scenarios paint a picture of complete security and relaxation. At night (بَيَـٰتًا), people are home, feeling safe, sleeping peacefully. During the afternoon nap (قَآئِلُونَ - from qaylulah), people are so comfortable and at ease that they can take time off in the middle of the day to rest.

In a society where you can take an afternoon nap, what does that tell you? It means the economy is stable - you don't need to work extra hours. It means law and order prevail - you feel secure enough to let your guard down. It means life is good; everything seems to be going perfectly. The mention of these two times emphasizes that destruction came when they least expected it, when they felt most secure, when everything appeared normal.

This is the warning: don't let prosperity and comfort make you heedless. Previous nations felt just as secure, and their destruction came suddenly, out of nowhere - on a bright morning after a peaceful night, or during a relaxed afternoon.

translation: And their declaration when Our punishment came to them was only that they said, "Indeed, we were wrongdoers
commentary:
**فَمَا كَانَ دَعْوَىٰهُمْ إِذْ جَآءَهُم بَأْسُنَآ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ**

*And their declaration when Our punishment came to them was only that they said, "Indeed, we were wrongdoers."*

فَمَا كَانَ دَعْوَىٰهُمْ - "Then what was their call?" or "What was their declaration?" The word دَعْوَىٰ can mean a claim, a call, or a declaration. Throughout their lives, these nations made many claims - claims about their traditions, their ancestors, their way of life, their gods. But what did they say when the punishment actually arrived?

إِذْ جَآءَهُم بَأْسُنَآ - "at the very moment Our war came against them" - not after some time to think, not after a chance to reconsider, but right at that instant.

إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ - "Except that they said, 'Indeed, we were the wrongdoers.'" They admitted their fault. They confessed. All their previous arrogance, all their justifications, all their arguments evaporated instantly. The only words they could produce were an admission of guilt.

The implication is powerful: You're refusing to admit your error now, while you still have time. But when the punishment begins, that defiant tone will completely change. At that moment, confession will come readily - but it will be too late. This is a call to humility and self-reflection before it's too late, while the opportunity for repentance still exists.

translation: Then We will surely question those to whom [a message] was sent, and We will surely question the messengers
commentary:
**فَلَنَسْـَٔلَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَنَسْـَٔلَنَّ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ**

*Then We will surely question those to whom messengers were sent, and We will surely question the messengers.*

The (فَ) at the beginning connects this ayah to what came before, moving from the moment of worldly punishment to the ultimate accountability on the Day of Judgment. Allah ﷻ makes a solemn oath, indicated by the lam of emphasis: لَنَسْـَٔلَنَّ - "We will surely, absolutely interrogate."

Who will be questioned? Two groups:

First, ٱلَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ - "those to whom messengers were sent," meaning the nations who received messengers and messages. They will be questioned about how they responded. Did they accept the message? Did they reject it? What were their reasons? What did they do with the guidance they received?

Second, وَلَنَسْـَٔلَنَّ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ - "and We will surely question the messengers themselves." The messengers will be asked: Did you deliver the message? Did you convey what was entrusted to you? Did you fulfill your responsibility?

This is a profound and sobering ayah. It reveals that the Messenger ﷺ isn't delivering this message as a hobby or a side project. He operates under divine mandate and will be held accountable for whether he fulfilled his mission. Multiple places in the Quran warn even the messengers. For example, "And if you were to follow their desires after the knowledge that has come to you, you would be among the wrongdoers" (2:145).

The message to the Quraysh is clear: This Messenger isn't trying to establish personal authority over you. He himself is operating under Allah's authority. The interrogation applies equally to all. Don't imagine that he's exempt from accountability - if anything, his responsibility is even greater. This should make them reflect: if the one delivering the message will be questioned about his delivery, how much more will we be questioned about our response?

translation: Then We will surely relate [their deeds] to them with knowledge, and We were not [at all] absent
commentary:
**فَلَنَقُصَّنَّ عَلَيْهِم بِعِلْمٍ ۖ وَمَا كُنَّا غَآئِبِينَ**

*"So We will surely relate to them with knowledge, and We were not absent."*

Allah ﷻ declares that He will narrate to people their own life stories on the Day of Judgment. The word قَصَّ means to narrate something elaborately, to tell a story with great detail and in a compelling way. This is significant because for any criminal, their greatest escape is denial—claiming the evidence is fabricated, that witnesses weren't present, that it's all lies made up against them.

But Allah ﷻ says **فَلَنَقُصَّنَّ عَلَيْهِم بِعِلْمٍ** (we will narrate these stories onto them with knowledge)—with proper footage, with great records and documentation. Every detail of their lives will be presented back to them with perfect accuracy.

Then He adds: **وَمَا كُنَّا غَآئِبِينَ** (and We were not absent at all). The word غائب is particularly interesting because it has multiple dimensions. It means "unseen" or "hidden," but by implication it also means "absent." For example, in the ayah **مَن خَافَ الرَّحْمَـٰنَ بِالْغَيْبِ** (whoever feared the Most Merciful in the unseen), the غيب refers both to the fact that Rahman is unseen and also to the person's fear being displayed while they are unseen from others—genuine fear of Allah ﷻ practiced in private, not just displayed publicly in the masjid.

Here, Allah ﷻ is telling us: I'm unseen to you, I'm in the غيب to you, but that does not mean I am absent. I was always there. This is a powerful continuation of the conversation from Surah Al-An'am, where the disbelievers rejected faith in what they could not see. Allah ﷻ responds: you couldn't see Me, but that doesn't mean I wasn't there. I was always there, witnessing everything.

translation: And the weighing [of deeds] that Day will be the truth. So those whose scales are heavy - it is they who will be the successful
commentary:
**وَٱلْوَزْنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ فَمَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ**

*"And the weighing [of deeds] that Day will be the truth. So those whose scales are heavy—it is they who will be the successful."*

The word **الْوَزْنُ** refers to the weighing scale or measure. Allah ﷻ says this weighing on that Day is **الْحَقُّ** —the reality, the truth. Some scholars have interpreted this to mean that truth is the only thing that will carry weight. In other words, good deeds performed with sincerity, based on faith and proper intention, are the ones that will have weight. Everything else will be worthless.

The Quran uses powerful imagery elsewhere, describing how Allah ﷻ will take the deeds of certain people and turn them into **هَبَاءً مَّنثُورًا** (scattered dust)—like those tiny particles you see floating in a beam of sunlight, completely weightless and without value. Only actions and lifestyles based on truth will carry weight on that Day.

Notice the beautiful precision in Allah's words: **فَمَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ** (whose scales became heavy)—scales, plural, not scale. The implication is that there are many kinds of deeds, and each has been given its own precise measure. Here's your scale for your prayers, here's your scale for your intentions, here's your scale for your speech, for your timeliness, for your laziness. Everything has a scale, everything has been given its own standard and measure.

**فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ** (it is they who will be the successful)—these are the ones who will enjoy ultimate success.

translation: And those whose scales are light - they are the ones who will lose themselves for what injustice they were doing toward Our verses
commentary:
**وَمَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَظْلِمُونَ**

*"And those whose scales are light—they are the ones who have lost themselves for what injustice they were doing toward Our verses."*

The word خَفَّتْ comes from خِفَّة, meaning lightness. As for those whose scales have become light, **فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُم** (they are the ones who have put themselves in loss)—they have bankrupted themselves.

Allah ﷻ tells us exactly why: **بِمَا كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَظْلِمُونَ** (on account of the wrong they were doing with Our miraculous signs, with Our revelations). They committed ظلم—injustice, wrongdoing—against Allah's signs. They knew the truth but rejected it, they saw the evidence but turned away from it, they had the guidance but chose to ignore it.

translation: And We have certainly established you upon the earth and made for you therein ways of livelihood. Little are you grateful
commentary:
**وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّـٰكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَجَعَلْنَا لَكُمْ فِيهَا مَعَـٰيِشَ ۗ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ**

*"And We have certainly established you upon the earth and made for you therein ways of livelihood. Little are you grateful."*

The word مَكَّنَّا comes from مكان, meaning place or location. **مَكَّنَّـٰكُمْ** means to give someone a station on the earth, to settle them down—this is your land, this is your place. Allah ﷻ says: We have settled you down on the earth.

Then He says **وَجَعَلْنَا لَكُمْ فِيهَا مَعَـٰيِشَ** (and We have placed for you, installed for you, means of living). The word مَعَـٰيِشَ comes from عاش, meaning to live well, to live nicely. The Quran uses this word when describing someone who will **يَعِيشُ عِيشَةً رَّاضِيَةً** (live a content, pleasing life). Allah ﷻ has given us the means by which we can live well.

What are these means? If you want to understand **مَعَـٰيِشَ** fully, go to Surah Quraysh at the end of the Quran. The Quraysh enjoyed all kinds of luxuries—they could travel safely in summer and winter without being robbed by other caravans, they were protected from the army of elephants (an impossible army to defend against), they were given security and prosperity. These are examples of the ma'ayish Allah ﷻ provides.

Yet after all these blessings, Allah ﷻ concludes: **قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ** (how little you are grateful, or how little effort you make to think and be grateful). No matter how little effort you make to think or reflect, the blessings are still there—yet gratitude remains scarce.

translation: And We have certainly created you, [O Mankind], and given you [human] form. Then We said to the angels, "Prostrate to Adam"; so they prostrated, except for Iblees. He was not of those who prostrated
commentary:
**وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْنَـٰكُمْ ثُمَّ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ لَمْ يَكُن مِّنَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ**

*"And We have certainly created you, [O mankind], and given you [human] form. Then We said to the angels, 'Prostrate to Adam'; so they prostrated, except for Iblees. He was not of those who prostrated."*

Allah ﷻ now takes us even further back in time: **وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ** (We have already created you). This isn't just about the people in Makkah right now—this goes back to the very beginning of humanity. **ثُمَّ صَوَّرْنَـٰكُمْ** (then We fashioned you, We molded you). The word صَوَّرَ means to fashion artistically, to form with precision and beauty.

Then comes something fascinating: **ثُمَّ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ** (then We said to the angels, 'Prostrate to Adam'). Notice the sequence: He created all of you (humanity), then He fashioned all of you, **then** He told the angels to prostrate to Adam (AS). This represents an interesting view of the chronology of creation, one that scholars continue to discuss and examine.

There's another intriguing reference in the Quran where Allah ﷻ says: **إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰٓ ءَادَمَ وَنُوحًا وَءَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَءَالَ عِمْرَٰنَ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ** (Indeed, Allah chose Adam and Nuh and the family of Ibrahim and the family of Imran over the worlds). I understand the family of Imran being chosen, the family of Ibrahim being chosen, Nuh (AS) being chosen—but how is Adam (AS) chosen? Being chosen implies there was a choice, a selection from among options.

These are profound questions that require careful study from both the textual and scientific perspectives. I've seen recent papers from scholars at Al-Azhar University addressing questions about evolution and modern anthropology, as students began asking how to reconcile these with the Quranic creation narrative. We need to have these conversations thoughtfully, with scholars from both the religious sciences and the natural sciences.

We must be careful not to accept scientific theories that contradict our faith, but at the same time, we cannot believe that the natural laws of creation—which are also from Allah ﷻ—are in conflict with revelation. Both come from the same Source. There is a conversation to be had here, and it will help many students who struggle with these questions after taking anthropology or evolutionary biology classes. The goal isn't to shut our brains off when thinking about religion, but to find the proper reconciliation—and it exists.

**فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ** (so they prostrated, except for Iblees). All the angels obeyed immediately. **لَمْ يَكُن مِّنَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ** (he was not from those who prostrated)—he didn't join the ranks of those who performed sujud.

The name إِبْلِيس itself comes from the root ب-ل-س, which means to lose hope completely, to be in utter despair.

translation: [Allah] said, "What prevented you from prostrating when I commanded you?" [Satan] said, "I am better than him. You created me from fire and created him from clay
commentary:
**قَالَ مَا مَنَعَكَ أَلَّا تَسْجُدَ إِذْ أَمَرْتُكَ ۖ قَالَ أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِى مِن نَّارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُۥ مِن طِينٍ**

*"[Allah] said, 'What prevented you from prostrating when I commanded you?' [Satan] said, 'I am better than him. You created me from fire and created him from clay.'"*

Allah ﷻ asks: **مَا مَنَعَكَ أَلَّا تَسْجُدَ** (What prevented you from prostrating?). The question could have ended there, but Allah ﷻ adds two crucial phrases: **إِذْ أَمَرْتُكَ** (when I commanded you). The word إِذْ emphasizes immediacy—why didn't you immediately prostrate the moment I commanded you? And notice that Allah ﷻ directly connects Himself to the command: "when **I** commanded you"—making the disobedience even more severe.

Iblees responds: **أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهُ** (I am better than him). This is the essence of Iblees's problem. He's not denying that Allah ﷻ is the Creator—notice he says **خَلَقْتَنِى** (You created me) and **خَلَقْتَهُۥ** (You created him). He fully acknowledges Allah's creative power. He's even aware of his own creation story: **خَلَقْتَنِى مِن نَّارٍ** (You created me from fire) and **خَلَقْتَهُۥ مِن طِينٍ** (You created him from clay).

This ayah reveals something critical: you don't have to be an atheist to follow in Shaytan's footsteps. Iblees is technically a theist—he believes in God, he's talking to Him, he acknowledges Him as Creator. He's not declaring himself God like Fir'awn did when he said **أَنَا۠ رَبُّكُمُ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ** (I am your highest lord). Iblees simply believes he deserved a promotion.

The problem wasn't prostrating to Allah ﷻ—Iblees had no issue with that. The problem was obeying Allah ﷻ when it came to accepting the authority of someone other than Allah Himself. It's about chain of command. Allah ﷻ was establishing the human being as the highest authority on Earth, and the prostration wasn't one of worship—it was a ceremonial prostration, an acknowledgment of rank.

Think of the modern equivalent: a salute. When soldiers salute their new general, they're acknowledging his authority, his rank. That's essentially what this prostration was—an acknowledgment of Adam's (AS) ranking and authority.

What's one of the fundamental problems people have with religion? Why should they listen to a prophet? Why him? What's so special about him? "I could worship God myself, I don't need him." This sounds reasonable on the surface—after all, the prophet isn't God. But the underlying attitude is: "I can't accept that he has authority over me." 

This mentality sounds noble—"I'm humbled to God, but not to anybody else"—but at its core, it's actually Satanic. If Allah ﷻ grants authority to someone and commands that person to be obeyed, then rejecting that authority is rejecting Allah's command. That's why throughout the Quran, prophets say: **ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ** (Be conscious of Allah and obey me). The command to obey the messenger is always paired with consciousness of Allah ﷻ, because the messenger's authority comes from Allah ﷻ.

Allah ﷻ isn't going to come and command us directly at every moment, but whose example is always there? The Messenger ﷺ. His practical example provides guidance for every situation. That's why you have people today saying, "I'm spiritual, but I don't follow religion." It's a modern version of Iblees's rebellion—acknowledging something "out there" but refusing to submit to the structured guidance and authority that comes from it.

Iblees was jealous of Adam (AS), but what was he jealous of? What status did Adam (AS) have? Allah ﷻ placed humanity on Earth and told us why we were created: to worship Him. So Iblees essentially wanted the religious role, the honor of being the one who worships and serves Allah ﷻ.

This reveals something crucial about arrogance: you might think pride and arrogance are only about worldly things—territory, power, control. But arrogance can absolutely make its way into religious discourse. "I'm the rightful leader of this community. I'm the scholar. Everyone should learn Islam from me. People should come to me to understand the religion." That's seeking power through religion, seeking religious recognition and authority—which is exactly what Iblees was after.

This is the same arrogance displayed by some among the Bani Israel. They wanted religious authority, and accepting the Prophet ﷺ would undermine that authority. People would no longer come to them for religious guidance. That was their essential problem.

translation: [Allah] said, "Descend from Paradise, for it is not for you to be arrogant therein. So get out; indeed, you are of the debased
commentary:
**قَالَ فَٱهْبِطْ مِنْهَا فَمَا يَكُونُ لَكَ أَن تَتَكَبَّرَ فِيهَا فَٱخْرُجْ إِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰغِرِينَ**

*"[Allah] said, 'Descend from it, for it is not for you to be arrogant therein. So get out; indeed, you are of the debased.'"*

Allah ﷻ commands: **فَٱهْبِطْ مِنْهَا** (descend from it). The word مِنْهَا (from it) is interesting because Jannah hasn't been explicitly mentioned yet in this passage, so we can't say for certain what "it" refers to. But we can understand it as the station, the honor, the rank, the elevated place Iblees had been enjoying.

**فَمَا يَكُونُ لَكَ أَن تَتَكَبَّرَ فِيهَا** (it is not becoming of you to be arrogant in it)—this place you've reached, as high as you've gotten, is not a place for arrogance. This is the exact opposite of how the world works. In worldly systems—corporations, governments, any hierarchy—the higher you climb, the more arrogant you can become, the more prideful you're allowed to be.

But with Allah ﷻ, it's the reverse formula: the higher your rank, the less place there is for arrogance. The more you're supposed to humble yourself, the more you put yourself down, the more Allah ﷻ elevates you. High spiritual rank demands greater humility.

**فَٱخْرُجْ** (so get out, leave). **إِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰغِرِينَ** (you are from those who have been made small, who have been humiliated). Notice the beautiful rhetorical device here—two opposite concepts in the same passage. First, تَتَكَبَّرَ (you're trying to make yourself big, to be arrogant), and then الصَّـٰغِرِينَ (you've been made small, insignificant).

This should remind us of the warning given to the Quraysh toward the end of Surah Al-An'am: they will suffer صَغَار —they'll be made small, humiliated, rendered insignificant.

translation: [Satan] said, "Reprieve me until the Day they are resurrected
commentary:
**قَالَ أَنظِرْنِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ**

*"[Satan] said, 'Reprieve me until the Day they are resurrected.'"*

Iblees responds: **أَنظِرْنِىٓ** (give me time, wait for me). The old English translations use "give me respite"—which sounds appropriately ominous. **أَنظِرْنِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ** (give me time until the day they are resurrected). Don't punish me right away, just give me time.

Notice the grammatical precision: يَوْمِ is in the genitive case (مجرور) because it follows إِلَىٰ, and يُبْعَثُونَ is also in the genitive because it's part of an إضافة structure—the day on which they will be raised. Give me until that time, Iblees asks. Just give me until then.

translation: [Allah] said, "Indeed, you are of those reprieved
commentary:
**قَالَ إِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنظَرِينَ**

*"[Allah] said, 'Indeed, you are of those reprieved.'"*

Allah ﷻ responds: **إِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنظَرِينَ** (you are from those who have been given that time). Without a doubt, you are among those who have been waited for, who have been granted this reprieve.

Notice Allah ﷻ didn't say إِنَّكَ مُنظَرٌ (you are one who is reprieved). He said **مِنَ ٱلْمُنظَرِينَ** (you are from among those reprieved)—meaning Iblees has followers, an army that has also been given time. They can roam free too. They're not going to be cast into Hell immediately either. Shaytan isn't working alone—he has a following, and they've all been granted this temporary freedom to test humanity until the Day of Resurrection.

translation: [Satan] said, "Because You have put me in error, I will surely sit in wait for them on Your straight path
commentary:
**قَالَ فَبِمَآ أَغْوَيْتَنِى لَأَقْعُدَنَّ لَهُمْ صِرَٰطَكَ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمَ**

*He said, "Because You have put me in error, I will surely sit in wait for them on Your straight path."*

The word أَغْوَيْتَنِى is particularly revealing of Iblis's nature. It comes from the root غ و ي, which means to be lost or misguided. This word carries a specific connotation in Arabic - when someone becomes stuck in a problem as a result of not knowing better. Picture taking a wrong turn unknowingly and ending up on an unpaved road where your tires burst. Your problems stem from that incorrect turn, but there's an element of innocence because you simply didn't know. The word ghawa adds another dimension: you now have no hope of getting back on the right road. You've become hopeless on top of being lost.

One of the clearest manifestations of pride is the refusal to take responsibility. It's always someone else's fault: "I didn't know. You did this to me. You took advantage of my ignorance." A decent human being, when something goes wrong, looks inward first. We learned this principle at the very beginning of this surah when Allah ﷻ told the Prophet ﷺ not to blame himself or be overwhelmed with sorrow if people rejected the message. A person of integrity examines their own actions when things don't work out.

But an indecent creature - and Iblis is our case study here - blames everyone else when things go wrong. Never himself. It's always external factors. Someone comes late to work and blames traffic, the city planning, the highway department. Students blame the teacher for assigning too much homework. People blame Netflix for their late nights. Everything and everyone becomes a scapegoat except the person who made the choice. Taking responsibility is the first act of decency, and this is what fundamentally separates a decent person from someone following in the footsteps of Shaytan.

When Iblis says, "Because You have caused me to go astray," he's essentially arguing: "Now that You've conned me into this situation, I'm going to take revenge." His response reveals his true nature through and through.

The word لَأَقْعُدَنَّ - "I will surely sit" - is emphatic and carries weight. قعد is different from جلس, which simply means to sit. Qa'ada implies sitting for an extended period, keeping watch, staying on patrol. Iblis is making an oath: "I swear I will sit waiting for them on Your straight path." He's declaring his intention to remain stationed, vigilant, unrelenting.

And where will he wait? On صِرَٰطَكَ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمَ - Your straight path. The path that You consider so straight, so perfect. Iblis is saying, "I'm going to make sure I divert them, make them take a left turn here, a right turn there. Every opportunity I get, I'll be there." This teaches us something profound about the spiritual journey: the straight path is incredibly difficult to traverse, and one of the primary reasons is that Shaytan sits right on it, constantly, without rest. 

Nobody reaches a state where they're safe from his influence. Nobody gets to say, "Alhamdulillah, I'm finally guided. I left Shaytan two miles back." No - he's on your windshield. He doesn't go anywhere. He never leaves. This is a warning for every believer: vigilance is not optional, it's essential.

translation: Then I will come to them from before them and from behind them and on their right and on their left, and You will not find most of them grateful [to You]
commentary:
**ثُمَّ لَـَٔاتِيَنَّهُم مِّنۢ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ وَعَنْ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ وَعَن شَمَآئِلِهِمْ ۖ وَلَا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شَـٰكِرِينَ**

*"Then I will come to them from before them and from behind them and on their right and on their left, and You will not find most of them grateful."*

Iblis doesn't just promise to wait passively. He says ثُمَّ لَـَٔاتِيَنَّهُم - "I will come to them," or more accurately, "I will attack them." The word آتي in this context carries the meaning of an assault, an aggressive approach. He's not merely observing - he's actively engaging in warfare against humanity.

His strategy is comprehensive: مِّنۢ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ - from between their hands, meaning right in front of them, directly in their face. These are the obvious temptations, the clear and present distractions. وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ - and from behind them, through ways they cannot see coming. Sometimes his whispers will be blatant, making evil appear attractive right before their eyes. Other times, he'll work subtly, making them think an idea is their own when it originated from him.

Then He says وَعَنْ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ - from their right - وَعَن شَمَآئِلِهِمْ - and from their left. Some scholars have interpreted these directions literally, suggesting he attacks from all sides. But there's a deeper meaning here. The right side traditionally represents good deeds in Islamic teaching, while the left represents evil deeds. What Iblis is revealing is chilling: "I'll even mislead them through good deeds." 

How? By making someone so focused on one aspect of religion that they neglect everything else. Someone becomes obsessed with learning and forgets their prayers. Another person perfects their prayer but abandons their family responsibilities. Yet another becomes so engaged in da'wah that they neglect their own spiritual development. Shaytan will gladly let you do good deeds if it means you lose balance and miss other obligations. This is coming at them "from the right."

From the left means through clear evil deeds - addictions, sins, and disobedience. He attacks from every direction, using every tool at his disposal.

Notice what Iblis doesn't mention: he doesn't say "from above them." Why? Because guidance comes from above. That direction is not accessible to him. He has no power over the mercy and guidance that descends from Allah ﷻ. This is our source of hope - no matter how comprehensive Shaytan's attacks may be, the door to divine guidance remains open and beyond his reach.

The ayah concludes with Iblis's prediction: وَلَا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شَـٰكِرِينَ - "and You will not find most of them grateful." This is particularly significant because earlier in the surah, the Quraysh were already told, قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ - "How little you are grateful." 

This conversation about Iblis isn't just ancient history - it's a direct message to those listening. Allah ﷻ created all human beings, fashioned them with care, and commanded the angels to honor this creation. Iblis refused and vowed to prove that most humans would be ungrateful. And here, millennia later, the Quraysh and countless others fulfill his prophecy. It's a continuation of one conversation, one cosmic conflict playing out across generations.

translation: [Allah] said, "Get out of Paradise, reproached and expelled. Whoever follows you among them - I will surely fill Hell with you, all together
commentary:
**قَالَ ٱخْرُجْ مِنْهَا مَذْءُومًا مَّدْحُورًا ۖ لَّمَن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ**

*[Allah] said, "Get out of it, disgraced and expelled. Whoever follows you among them - I will surely fill Hell with you, all together."*

Allah's ﷻ response is swift and decisive: ٱخْرُجْ مِنْهَا - "Get out of it." The command is absolute and immediate. 

The words مَذْءُومًا مَّدْحُورًا are both powerful and related in meaning. مَذْءُوم refers to someone who is deeply blameworthy, someone in whom you find extensive faults, someone who disgusts you. The word contains an emphasis because of the hamzah, making it stronger than its simpler form. It describes someone utterly despicable.

مَّدْحُور comes from the root د ح ر, which means to expel or kick someone out. But it's more intense than simple expulsion - it combines two elements. First, you escort or throw someone out forcibly. Second, you don't just remove them from the immediate vicinity; you take them far away, to the outskirts, and abandon them there. Picture being kicked out of a fancy restaurant - that's expulsion. But madhoor is when they don't just throw you outside the door; they drive you to the other side of town and dump you in a ditch. It carries the meaning of exile, of being banished to a distant place with no hope of return.

Then Allah ﷻ makes a solemn declaration: لَّمَن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ - "Whoever follows you among them, I will surely fill Hell with you, all together." The word لَأَمْلَأَنَّ is emphatic - "I swear I will fill." Not partially fill, but fill completely, to the brim. جَهَنَّمَ - Hell - will be filled مِنكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ - with all of you together. 

The word أَجْمَعِينَ is particularly strong - it means every single one, down to the last individual. The dual form emphasizes totality: you and every last person who followed you. It's a promise of complete and perfect justice.

translation: And "O Adam, dwell, you and your wife, in Paradise and eat from wherever you will but do not approach this tree, lest you be among the wrongdoers
commentary:
**وَيَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ ٱسْكُنْ أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ فَكُلَا مِنْ حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ**

*"And O Adam, dwell, you and your wife, in Paradise and eat from wherever you will but do not approach this tree, lest you be among the wrongdoers."*

After addressing Iblis with severity, Allah ﷻ turns to Adam (AS) with gentleness: وَيَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ - "And O Adam." Imagine Adam (AS) witnessing this entire exchange, this cosmic confrontation. He's just seen what Iblis has vowed to do. The contrast in tone is remarkable.

Allah ﷻ says ٱسْكُنْ - "settle down, dwell, live peacefully." This is the imperative form addressing Adam (AS) directly. Then أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ - "you and your wife." Notice the grammar here: أَنتَ (you - masculine singular) is separated by و (and), creating a respectful distance in the address. Allah ﷻ is speaking to Adam (AS) as the head of the household, with his wife mentioned alongside him but not directly addressed.

This isn't about superiority or inferiority - it's about responsibility and structure. The choice of the word زَوْج (spouse, wife) rather than calling her by name is also significant. Allah ﷻ speaks to the husband, who carries responsibility for the household. This echoes what we learn elsewhere in the Quran: men are maintainers and protectors of their families. This divine pattern was established with the very first husband and wife.

Some might object to this as outdated or oppressive, but we cannot shy away from what our teachings clearly establish. The husband is made the قَيِّم over the household - the maintainer, the one with ultimate responsibility. He is the أَمِير of the family unit. This doesn't give license for authoritarianism or abuse - those are grave sins. But neither can we abandon the structure Allah ﷻ has established simply to conform to modern ideologies.

Justice in Islam is not the same as equality. Allah ﷻ created two very different creatures with different roles, different rights, and different responsibilities. Both are honored, both are addressed by their Lord, but they are not identical in function.

Look at how this plays out in the rest of the ayah. When it comes to enjoyment - فَكُلَا مِنْ حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا - "eat from wherever you both wish" - Allah ﷻ uses the dual form, addressing both of them equally. Both are included in the permission to eat and enjoy Paradise. Neither is excluded from the blessing.

Similarly, when prohibition comes - وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةَ - "do not approach this tree" - again the dual form addresses both equally. Both are warned. Both are given the same boundary. Both will be held accountable.

This is the pattern: in matters of halal and haram, consumption and prohibition, spiritual responsibility and accountability, Allah ﷻ addresses men and women equally. The food is for both, the restrictions apply to both, the consequences affect both. What differs is the structure of authority within the domestic sphere. That authority was given to Adam (AS), and through him, to husbands as heads of households.

The warning concludes: فَتَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - "lest you both be among the wrongdoers." Again, both are addressed. Both would become wrongdoers if they violated this command. The dual form makes this absolutely clear - neither would be innocent if they transgressed.

translation: But Satan whispered to them to make apparent to them that which was concealed from them of their private parts. He said, "Your Lord did not forbid you this tree except that you become angels or become of the immortal
commentary:
**فَوَسْوَسَ لَهُمَا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لِيُبْدِىَ لَهُمَا مَا وُۥرِىَ عَنْهُمَا مِن سَوْءَٰتِهِمَا وَقَالَ مَا نَهَىٰكُمَا رَبُّكُمَا عَنْ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَا مَلَكَيْنِ أَوْ تَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰلِدِينَ**

*Then Shaytan whispered to them to make apparent to them what was concealed from them of their private parts. He said, "Your Lord did not forbid you this tree except that you become angels or become of the immortal."*

The word وَسْوَسَ is significant. Arabic has multiple words for whispering - همس is one of them. But waswasa is distinct because of its structure. The repetition of syllables - was-wasa - linguistically mimics the action it describes: whisper, disappear, whisper again, disappear again. It's the kind of whisper where you hear something, turn around to look, and nobody's there. You face forward, and the whisper returns. You spin around again - nothing. This is the nature of Shaytan's influence: unseen, persistent, elusive, maddening.

This clarifies something important about the story of Adam and Hawwa (AS). Some Christian traditions depict a serpent approaching them openly, having a direct conversation. That's not waswasa. That's overt and direct. The Quranic account describes something far more insidious - unseen whispers that come and go, that plant ideas subtly, that work on the mind gradually.

Notice the grammar: فَوَسْوَسَ لَهُمَا - "he whispered to both of them." The dual form appears again and again. Shaytan didn't target just one of them - this is crucial. Both were victims of his whispers simultaneously.

His goal is stated clearly: لِيُبْدِىَ لَهُمَا مَا وُۥرِىَ عَنْهُمَا مِن سَوْءَٰتِهِمَا - "to make apparent to them what was concealed from them of their private parts." The word وُورِيَ comes from the root و ر ي, meaning to cover up or conceal. When you hide something completely, it's called wara. The passive form wuriya means "it was hidden" - specifically referring to their عَوْرَة, their private parts that should remain covered.

Shaytan's first goal wasn't just to make them eat the fruit. His objective was exposure - to remove their clothes, to strip away their dignity, to eliminate their sense of shame. This is a profound lesson: one of the primary goals of Shaytan throughout human history is to normalize shamelessness, to erode modesty, to make exposure acceptable and even celebrated.

Then comes his argument: مَا نَهَىٰكُمَا رَبُّكُمَا عَنْ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةَ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَا مَلَكَيْنِ - "Your Lord did not forbid you from this tree except that you would become angels."

The irony here is staggering. We just learned in the previous passages that the angels had already prostrated to Adam (AS). They were in a position of honoring him, showing him reverence. Adam (AS) already held a rank higher than the angels. And now Shaytan is suggesting, "Don't you want to be angels?" Why would he want to be angels when he's already been given a higher status? The argument makes no logical sense, but waswasa doesn't need logic - it needs only to plant doubt.

Or, Shaytan continues, أَوْ تَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰلِدِينَ - "or you would become among the immortals," those who reside in Paradise permanently. But there was already an indication that Adam (AS) was meant for the Earth. Allah ﷻ had announced, "I am placing a khalifah on the Earth." The memo was out: you're going to Earth. This was part of the divine plan.

So Shaytan's argument essentially was: "The only thing preventing you from gaining permanent residence here, from getting your green card in Paradise, is this tree. That's why He doesn't want you to eat it - because it would mess up His whole plan of sending you to Earth. But you could stay here as angels or as permanent residents if only you ate from it."

translation: And he swore [by Allah] to them, "Indeed, I am to you from among the sincere advisors
commentary:
**وَقَاسَمَهُمَآ إِنِّى لَكُمَا لَمِنَ ٱلنَّـٰصِحِينَ**

*And he swore to them, "Indeed, I am to you both from among the sincere advisors."*

This didn't happen once. That's the critical point to understand. Shaytan didn't approach them one time with his suggestion, get rejected, and give up. The word قَاسَمَ tells us something profound. It comes from قَسَم, which means to swear an oath. The form قَاسَمَ (qasama) in the third form indicates intensity and repetition - he swore to them over and over again, emphatically, persistently.

The phrase إِنِّى لَكُمَا لَمِنَ ٱلنَّـٰصِحِينَ contains multiple emphasis particles. إِنَّ adds emphasis. The ل before لَكُمَا adds more emphasis. The ل before مِنَ adds yet more emphasis. It's emphatic upon emphatic upon emphatic: "I swear to you, I am absolutely, certainly, definitely one of your sincere well-wishers."

The word نَاصِح means someone who gives advice that carries benefit for the other person, with no benefit for themselves. It's pure, selfless counsel. Shaytan was claiming, "I'm giving you advice that's good for you. I have nothing to gain from this. I'm just looking out for your welfare."

Now here's what we need to understand about waswasa: when it's successful, you don't recognize its source. If Adam and Hawwa (AS) had recognized that these thoughts were from Shaytan, they would have immediately rejected them. But that's not how waswasa works. Successful waswasa makes you think the idea is your own. That's why it's dangerous - you believe you came up with it yourself, that it's your own reasoning, your own conclusion.

This process took time. The first whisper: "Maybe you should eat from the tree." Response: "No, Allah ﷻ forbade it." Second attempt: "But do you know why He forbade it?" Third try: "Just think about it - what if you could become angels?" Fourth attempt: "Or what if you could stay here forever?" Fifth try: "I swear to you, I'm only trying to help."

Over and over, persistently, patiently. Maybe they considered it and rejected it. Maybe they thought about it the next day. Maybe they discussed it between themselves. Perhaps they walked past the tree and wondered. Little by little, the resistance weakened. The idea didn't seem so bad. Then it seemed reasonable. Then it seemed appealing. Finally, it seemed like their own decision, their own choice.

This is what فَدَلَّىٰهُمَا means - from the root د ل و, which refers to lowering a bucket into a well slowly to fill it with water. It takes time. The word implies a long-term process of convincing someone to do something through persistent, patient effort. Shaytan wasn't in a hurry. He was willing to put in the time, willing to work on them gradually, willing to chip away at their resistance bit by bit.

This is perhaps the most critical lesson we can extract from these ayahs about Shaytan's methodology. He is not impulsive. He is not impatient. He doesn't give up after one failed attempt or ten failed attempts or a hundred. He'll take 1% today and come back for another 1% tomorrow. He'll chip away at your resolve gradually. Every free moment you have, every unguarded thought, every moment of weakness - he's there, working on you, sculpting your decisions.

We cannot think of Shaytan's whispers as something primitive or unsophisticated. If anyone has mastered human psychology, it's him. He's been studying humanity since the beginning. He knows what works. He has an impeccable track record of successful conversions to his cause. He knows his customers intimately because he's been observing us for millennia. He understands our conditioning, our patterns, our weaknesses, our triggers.

And unlike a salesperson who wants to close the deal quickly, Shaytan has patience. He'll take his time making the sale. He's not going anywhere. Neither are you. He'll work on you for as long as it takes.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from the whispers of Shaytan, help us recognize his tactics, and give us the wisdom and strength to use His book as a shield against this persistent enemy.

translation: So he made them fall, through deception. And when they tasted of the tree, their private parts became apparent to them, and they began to fasten together over themselves from the leaves of Paradise. And their Lord called to them, "Did I not forbid you from that tree and tell you that Satan is to you a clear enemy
commentary:
فَدَلَّىٰهُمَا بِغُرُورٍ ۚ فَلَمَّا ذَاقَا ٱلشَّجَرَةَ بَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ وَنَادَىٰهُمَا رَبُّهُمَآ أَلَمْ أَنْهَكُمَا عَن تِلْكُمَا ٱلشَّجَرَةِ وَأَقُل لَّكُمَآ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ لَكُمَا عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ

**So he brought them down through deception. And when they tasted of the tree, their private parts became apparent to them, and they began to fasten over themselves from the leaves of Paradise. And their Lord called to them, "Did I not forbid you from that tree and tell you that Satan is to you a clear enemy?"**

The word فَدَلَّىٰهُمَا captures the gradual nature of Shaytan's deception. He slowly lowered them, bringing them down step by step through غُرُور - deception and false promises. This wasn't a sudden fall but a calculated process of manipulation.

When the ayah states ذَاقَا ٱلشَّجَرَةَ (they tasted the tree), this is a beautiful example of Arabic eloquence. While the literal meaning refers to tasting the fruit of the tree, Arabic often identifies things by their essence. This is similar to how someone might say "I killed him with my leg" meaning with the sword that hangs from the leg. The tree represents its fruit, and this linguistic device emphasizes the central role the tree played in this pivotal moment.

The immediate consequence was profound: بَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتُهُمَا (their private parts became apparent to them). The word سَوْءَة carries layers of meaning - it refers to that which should be covered, like a corpse that must be concealed. It also carries the connotation of ugliness and shame. The exposure of their private parts represented the first experience of shame in human history.

What follows is remarkable: وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ (they began to fasten). The verb طَفِقَ indicates an immediate action, almost reflexive. They didn't deliberate or discuss it - they immediately began trying to cover themselves. The word خَصْف refers to the act of sticking things on top of one another, like attaching a sole to a shoe or layering pieces of paper with glue. They frantically gathered leaves from Paradise and began layering them over themselves, desperate to restore their lost dignity.

This immediate response reveals something profound about human nature: shame is inherent to humanity. Allah ﷻ didn't need to teach them to cover themselves. He didn't command them to feel shame as a punishment. Rather, when the clothing was removed, the natural human response was to cover up. This stands in stark contrast to modern philosophies that claim modesty and clothing are merely social constructs. The naturalist argument that claims we came from our mothers without clothes, therefore clothing is unnecessary, fundamentally misunderstands human dignity. Allah ﷻ didn't create us as animals - He created us as human beings and honored us فَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنَا بَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ (We have certainly honored the children of Adam). He honored us above animals and even above angels. Part of that honor is our clothing.

The removal of clothing in this story represents disgrace and humiliation. For Muslims, this establishes a critical principle: to be inappropriately dressed or exposed is not simply a matter of preference but a form of humiliation. It echoes the punishment that resulted from disobedience. How then can shamelessness be celebrated?

This story also establishes fundamental social institutions. The narrative of Adam (AS) holds immense significance for anthropology and sociology because it's the original human story. Modern sociology often teaches that marriage is merely a social construct, something humans invented that has no basis in our nature. The push toward promiscuity and the questioning of traditional institutions stems from this thinking - that these structures are optional products of human imagination rather than essential to human dignity.

But the Quran presents a different picture. Before society even existed, before human civilization began, the first human beings had these institutions: shame, modesty, clothing, and marriage. These aren't social inventions - they're part of how Allah ﷻ created us. Marriage is mentioned right at the beginning of human existence: وَقُلْنَا يَـٰٓءَادَمُ ٱسْكُنْ أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ (We said, "O Adam, dwell, you and your wife, in Paradise"). It's original to human nature, programmed into how we were created.

Now comes the divine call: وَنَادَىٰهُمَا رَبُّهُمَآ (their Lord called to them). Notice the word رَبّ - their Master, their Lord, their Nurturer. Even in this moment of failure, Allah ﷻ refers to Himself with this merciful attribute.

The questions that follow carry profound weight: أَلَمْ أَنْهَكُمَا عَن تِلْكُمَا ٱلشَّجَرَةِ (Did I not forbid you from that tree?) وَأَقُل لَّكُمَآ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ لَكُمَا عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (And tell you that Satan is to you a clear enemy?). These aren't questions seeking information - Allah ﷻ already knows the answer. They're rhetorical questions that prompt reflection and acknowledgment. The word مُّبِين emphasizes that Shaytan's enmity is clear, obvious, undeniable. There was no ambiguity in the warning.

Some scholars throughout Islamic history, like some in the Christian tradition, have attempted to interpret the forbidden fruit metaphorically. They've asked whether it represents temptation in general or perhaps the first intimate act between husband and wife. These questions arise partly because of the immediate mention of exposure and shame. However, we don't have definitive answers to such speculation, so it's best to understand the fruit as literally forbidden fruit and leave the matter at that.

translation: They said, "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers
commentary:
قَالَا رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَآ أَنفُسَنَا وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ

**They said, "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers."**

The response of Adam and Hawwa (AS) stands in stark contrast to Iblis's reaction when he disobeyed. Consider what they have in common: both were honored by Allah ﷻ at some point, both possessed free will and the ability to choose, both engaged Allah ﷻ directly in conversation, both were given rank above angels, and both disobeyed Allah's command. The similarities are striking.

But here's where the paths diverge completely: what happens after the mistake. When Iblis disobeyed, he blamed Allah ﷻ: فَبِمَآ أَغْوَيْتَنِى (By Your having misguided me). His response was to rationalize, to offer logical explanations, to maintain his position: أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِى مِن نَّارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُۥ مِن طِينٍ (I am better than him. You created me from fire and created him from clay). 

This is actually a logical argument. Fire versus clay - there's no comparison. Fire rises, it has power, it transforms. Some narrations mention that Iblis entered Adam's body before it had a soul, examined it, and emerged declaring it hollow and weak. Jinn possess capabilities humans don't - they can travel vast distances instantly, assume different forms, access realms we cannot. From a purely logical standpoint, Iblis's argument makes sense.

But here's the critical point: you cannot argue logic with Allah ﷻ. You cannot counter His decisions with your reasoning. When you question Allah's judgment - Why would You choose him over me? - you've fundamentally misunderstood who you're dealing with. The crime isn't having a question. The crime is believing you're in a position to demand an explanation, to sit in judgment over divine wisdom.

This manifests today when someone says, "Why would God create Hell? Why does Islam have this ruling? Why does the Quran say that?" When you feel entitled to an explanation before you'll submit, whose footsteps are you following? The premise of Islam - the very meaning of the word - is submission. You've surrendered your will, your criticisms, your demands for everything to make sense to you first.

Look at Ibrahim (AS) as the perfect example of submission. Every command he received defied logic: Put a knife to your son's neck. Jump into a fire. Take your family to the middle of an empty desert and leave them there. Then when your son is finally grown, sacrifice him. None of it makes conventional sense. But when his Lord said أَسْلِمْ (Submit), he responded أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (I have submitted to the Lord of the worlds). That's the essence of Islam.

Following Iblis's footsteps means responding to divine commands with "How does that make sense? That's not logical to me." Why three different rak'ahs for different prayers instead of a consistent number? Why can we wipe over socks but must wash our feet thoroughly when not wearing them? The bottom of the foot gets dirtier than the top - why wash the top? When we start down this road of demanding everything conform to our logic first, we've already stepped onto Shaytan's path.

This is different from seeking to appreciate wisdom in Allah's commands. Trying to understand and discover some of the wisdom in what Allah ﷻ has ordained is praiseworthy. But even when we discover something, that doesn't necessarily mean we've uncovered the complete reason for the command. People explain the harms of alcohol to show why it's forbidden. But it's forbidden because Allah ﷻ made it forbidden - that's the primary reason. And what does the Quran say? يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْخَمْرِ وَٱلْمَيْسِرِ ۖ قُلْ فِيهِمَآ إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنَـٰفِعُ لِلنَّاسِ (They ask you about wine and gambling. Say, "In them is great sin and [yet, some] benefit for people"). It acknowledges there may be benefits even as it forbids them.

Similarly with riba (interest) - you can point out its evils, but the modern economy functions on it. There are undeniable economic advantages to interest-based systems. Doesn't matter. It's prohibited. That's it.

Now, this doesn't mean Islam demands blind following without thought. There's a crucial distinction: Before entering Islam, you must ask critical questions. Is there really a God? Is this truly revelation? Is Muhammad ﷺ genuinely a prophet? Is there really an afterlife? These are valid, necessary questions, and the Quran addresses them openly. But once you've entered the fold of Islam, once you've accepted that there is a God and He possesses superior wisdom, then the door to criticism closes. You've accepted His authority, which means you've accepted that His wisdom supersedes yours.

So when it comes to our beliefs, we arrive at them through a critical process. But in our submission to Allah ﷻ after accepting those beliefs, there's no more room for disputing His commands.

This brings us back to the profound difference between Iblis and Adam (AS). Adam and Hawwa's response was immediate and complete: رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَآ أَنفُسَنَا (Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves). They admitted their fault. They took responsibility. No excuses, no rationalizations, no blaming Allah ﷻ or circumstances.

Then they made a beautiful dua that teaches us how to turn to Allah ﷻ: وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا (And if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us). Notice both words - forgiveness and mercy. Why both?

Forgiveness means you're out of trouble. Forgiveness means the punishment is lifted, the consequences are removed. But just because you're not in prison doesn't mean you have a good life. Just because you're not in trouble doesn't mean you're living well. We don't only need forgiveness from Allah ﷻ - we also need His mercy, His blessings, His positive favor.

But observe the order: forgiveness comes first, then mercy. You must be out of trouble before you can ask for the good things. The sequence matters.

Consider also that they had just experienced punishment - their clothing was removed, they felt shame and disgrace. Logic would suggest they should focus entirely on seeking forgiveness. Yet Adam (AS) demonstrates beautiful expectations of Allah ﷻ. He's not just hoping for forgiveness - he's hoping that on top of forgiveness, Allah ﷻ will show mercy and grant blessings too. Even in this moment of failure, he hasn't lost hope in Allah's generosity.

They conclude: لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (we will surely be among the losers). This is complete acknowledgment of their dependence on divine mercy. Without Your forgiveness and mercy, we have no hope. We will be completely and utterly lost.

translation: [Allah] said, "Descend, being to one another enemies. And for you on the earth is a place of settlement and enjoyment for a time
commentary:
قَالَ ٱهْبِطُوا۟ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ ۖ وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَـٰعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ

**[Allah] said, "Descend, being to one another enemies. And for you on the earth is a place of settlement and enjoyment for a time."**

Allah ﷻ responds with ٱهْبِطُوا۟ (descend) - addressed to the plural. This isn't just Adam and Hawwa. Who else is included? Iblis as well. All of you, come down.

The statement بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ (some of you will be enemies to others) establishes the reality of earthly existence. Enmity will exist on multiple levels: man will be enemy to woman, woman to man. Humanity will be enemies to Shaytan, and Shaytan will be enemy to humanity. Even humans will develop enmity amongst themselves. This is the fundamental tension that will characterize life on earth.

But notice what follows: وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَـٰعٌ (And for you on the earth is a place of settlement and enjoyment). The word مُسْتَقَرّ means a temporary dwelling place, somewhere to rest and settle for a while. But Allah ﷻ adds مَتَـٰع - enjoyment, pleasure, provision.

This is remarkable. Allah ﷻ didn't have to create an earth that's enjoyable. He could have made food that's merely nutritious without being delicious. He could have created an environment where humans merely survive - like prisoners in a cell drinking protein shakes for sustenance. Survival doesn't require beauty or pleasure.

But Allah ﷻ gave us مَتَـٰع - He made this earth a place of enjoyment إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (for a time). He created food with flavors, scenery with beauty, experiences with joy. This world is temporary, yes, but it's also a gift meant to be appreciated and enjoyed within the boundaries He has set.

translation: He said, "Therein you will live, and therein you will die, and from it you will be brought forth
commentary:
قَالَ فِيهَا تَحْيَوْنَ وَفِيهَا تَمُوتُونَ وَمِنْهَا تُخْرَجُونَ

**He said, "Therein you will live, and therein you will die, and from it you will be brought forth."**

Allah ﷻ establishes the complete cycle of human existence in one concise statement. فِيهَا تَحْيَوْنَ (In it you will live) - the earth will be your home, the place where your life unfolds, where generations will succeed generations.

وَفِيهَا تَمُوتُونَ (And in it you will die) - this same earth that gives you life will be where you return to dust. Birth and death, both take place here.

وَمِنْهَا تُخْرَجُونَ (And from it you will be brought forth) - but death isn't the end. From this very earth, from these very particles, you will be extracted and resurrected. The earth is both womb and grave, both beginning and gateway to the eternal realm.

This ayah encompasses the entirety of human existence: life, death, and resurrection. Everything between your first breath and your final accountability is summarized here.

translation: O children of Adam, We have bestowed upon you clothing to conceal your private parts and as adornment. But the clothing of righteousness - that is best. That is from the signs of Allah that perhaps they will remember
commentary:
يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ قَدْ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ لِبَاسًا يُوَٰرِى سَوْءَٰتِكُمْ وَرِيشًا ۖ وَلِبَاسُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ

**O children of Adam, We have bestowed upon you clothing to conceal your private parts and as adornment. But the clothing of righteousness - that is best. That is from the signs of Allah that perhaps they will remember.**

The conversation now shifts from the story of Adam (AS) to addressing all of his descendants. Allah ﷻ doesn't say يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ (O mankind) but rather يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ (O children of Adam). This direct reference to Adam keeps the story we just read fresh in our minds. As He addresses us as children of Adam, we're meant to remember our father's story - the loss of clothing, the shame, the immediate need to cover.

قَدْ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ لِبَاسًا (We have sent down upon you clothing). In the previous ayah, Allah ﷻ said ٱهْبِطُوا۟ (descend) - our parents were sent down to earth. Now He says أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ لِبَاسًا (We have sent down clothing upon you). This is extraordinary. The verb أَنزَلَ (sent down) is typically used for revelation, for rain, for divine gifts. Clothing is described with the same word used for the Quran being sent down.

This tells us the punishment of nakedness was temporary. Our parents' clothing was removed as a consequence of disobedience, but Allah ﷻ immediately restored it. We're not permanently cursed. We're not born into sin that can never be washed away. That punishment ended, and Allah ﷻ gave clothing back as a gift.

This stands in direct contrast to the Christian narrative of original sin - that we're born into sin, that we inherit our parents' guilt, that we need someone else's sacrifice to be cleansed. No. They asked for forgiveness, Allah ﷻ forgave them, the punishment ended, and you got a fresh start with clothing restored.

The ayah describes two purposes for clothing: يُوَٰرِى سَوْءَٰتِكُمْ (to conceal your private parts). The word يُوَٰرِى means to cover, to conceal, to hide. Just as your father and mother needed to cover themselves, so do you. This is the primary function - concealment of what should remain private.

But then Allah ﷻ adds وَرِيشًا (and as adornment). The word رِيش is beautiful - it literally refers to the feathers of a bird. Just as feathers are the beauty and adornment of birds, making them magnificent to look at, so clothing serves as beauty and adornment for human beings. Some scholars said رِيش refers to particularly beautiful, fashionable clothing with fine accessories.

Allah ﷻ wants human beings to look beautiful. He wants us to dress well, to present ourselves nicely. This is mentioned right here in the Quran - not just covering, but beauty. Fashion isn't discouraged in Islam; it's acknowledged as part of why Allah ﷻ gave us clothing. The Prophet ﷺ himself appreciated fine clothing. He loved receiving clothes as gifts. He even had garments imported from Persia that he particularly enjoyed wearing. Looking good, dressing well - this is recognized and encouraged.

It changes your entire perspective on shopping. When you're selecting clothes, you're not just fulfilling a mundane necessity - you're engaging with one of Allah's gifts sent down for your benefit and beauty.

Then comes the profound addition: وَلِبَاسُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ (But the clothing of righteousness - that is best). This works on multiple levels.

Literally, it refers to clothing that represents taqwa - clothing that's modest, that doesn't expose what should be concealed, that isn't inappropriately tight or revealing, that protects from indecency. Clothing that embodies the consciousness of Allah ﷻ in how you present yourself.

Figuratively, it refers to adorning your heart with taqwa. Physical clothing covers the body, but taqwa clothes the soul. You can be dressed beautifully on the outside but spiritually naked on the inside. The best clothing is when both the external and internal are proper - when your physical appearance reflects modesty and your heart is adorned with consciousness of Allah ﷻ.

This becomes challenging in our times. You go to stores looking for children's clothing and find inappropriate options even for small kids. Who designs such things? Why is modesty so difficult to find? This ayah actually inspired the thought: sisters should establish children's clothing companies. Entire fashion lines based on the principle of لِبَاسُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ. Modest, beautiful, fashionable clothing that fulfills both purposes - concealment and adornment - while reflecting Islamic values.

ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ (That is from the signs of Allah). Your clothing is an ayah, a sign pointing to Allah ﷻ. Every time you get dressed, you're engaging with one of His miraculous signs. The ability to create fabric, to design clothing, to cover and beautify ourselves - all of this points back to Him.

لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (That perhaps they will remember). The purpose of recognizing clothing as an ayah is remembrance of Allah ﷻ. What Allah ﷻ has done in the Quran is profound - He hasn't let you escape from dhikr, from remembrance. The sky above you is dhikr. The trees around you are dhikr. And now, the clothes on your body have become dhikr.

You dress yourself in the morning and you remember: Allah ﷻ gave me these clothes. يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ قَدْ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ لِبَاسًا (O children of Adam, We have bestowed upon you clothing). This is from Allah ﷻ. This is His gift. وَرِيشًا - He wants me to look good. وَلِبَاسُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ - and He wants my heart and my appearance to reflect consciousness of Him.

What a beautiful religion. Everything becomes spiritual. Everything connects you back to your Creator. You don't need to sit in a prayer room to remember Allah ﷻ. The very act of getting dressed becomes an act of dhikr, a moment of remembrance and gratitude.

translation: O children of Adam, let not Satan tempt you as he removed your parents from Paradise, stripping them of their clothing to show them their private parts. Indeed, he sees you, he and his tribe, from where you do not see them. Indeed, We have made the devils allies to those who do not believe
commentary:
**يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ لَا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ كَمَآ أَخْرَجَ أَبَوَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ يَنزِعُ عَنْهُمَا لِبَاسَهُمَا لِيُرِيَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتِهِمَآ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ يَرَىٰكُمْ هُوَ وَقَبِيلُهُۥ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا تَرَوْنَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ**

"O Children of Adam, let not Shaytan tempt you as he removed your parents from Paradise, stripping from them their clothing to show them their private parts. Indeed, he sees you, he and his tribe, from where you do not see them. Indeed, We have made the devils allies to those who do not believe."

Allah ﷻ begins this ayah with a powerful warning: لَا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ (do not let Shaytan put you in trial and difficulty). The emphasis here is clear—don't let him attack you at all. Allah ﷻ reminds us that we should hate Shaytan for multiple reasons, and one of the most personal is that he is an enemy to our parents, Adam and Hawwa (AS).

We need to understand something fundamental about our relationship with Adam (AS). We don't think of him as our father in the way we think of our biological fathers, but we should. We're supposed to have love for Adam (AS) just as we do for our own dad, and love for Hawwa (AS) as we do for our own mother. When someone is an enemy to our parents, naturally we have animosity toward them. That's the loyalty we're supposed to have to those parents of ours.

The ayah then describes what Shaytan did: يَنزِعُ عَنْهُمَا لِبَاسَهُمَا (he snatched from them their clothes, he pulled their clothes off) لِيُرِيَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتِهِمَآ (so he may expose their shamelessness). That same enemy who did that to your parents—he's watching you. إِنَّهُۥ يَرَىٰكُمْ (he sees you, no doubt about it).

But notice the ayah doesn't stop there: هُوَ وَقَبِيلُهُۥ (he and his entire tribe). Earlier in the surah, we learned that Shaytan was given respite until the Day of Judgment. Now we learn something more about those others who have been given time to wait—his entire tribe, those following him. The word قَبِيلُهُۥ (his tribe) is particularly interesting because it can mean both those who are right ahead of him and those right behind him. This language of opposites captures the complete nature of his followers.

And they see you مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا تَرَوْنَهُمْ (from places you can't even see them). This is our reality—an enemy we cannot see with our eyes is constantly watching us, looking for opportunities to lead us astray.

The ayah concludes with a powerful statement: إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (We made all of those devils protective friends for the people who don't believe). Notice the connection here—just before this ayah, Allah ﷻ told us to cover ourselves properly with the clothing of taqwa. Now He tells us that devils are the best friends to people who don't believe. So how are people who don't believe going to dress? The connection becomes clear—the fact that you're not from the allies of Shaytan means the way you dress is different.

This isn't just a theoretical conversation. We're a religious community, yet even in our Muslim communities, we find men and women dressing inappropriately. The restrictions in our religion aren't complicated. Don't wear tight clothing. Don't wear revealing clothing. Don't wear see-through clothing. What's the point of wearing hijab when the rest of your body is completely exposed? The Prophet ﷺ called this "covered and naked at the same time"—women dressed in tight clothes even when covered.

The hijab is actually icing on the cake. It's one manifestation of modesty, but there are so many other aspects of modesty. And then on top of that comes the لِبَاسُ التَّقْوَىٰ (clothing of taqwa) which means we have to protect ourselves not just with our clothes but with the way we carry ourselves.

There are sisters and brothers who are properly covered—the beard is long, the hijab and niqab are in place—but the way they carry themselves is inappropriate. They make small talk that crosses boundaries, all "for the sake of Islam." When you call it out and try to give advice, what do they say? "What, do you think I am perfect? You're being extreme!" They can't admit fault. It's always someone else's fault. Who does that sound like? Shaytan never admitted he did anything wrong. Some people can't do it either—they get offended immediately.

We're living in a time now where shamelessness is a multibillion dollar industry. We're living in times where a mother, father, husband, wife, brother and sister can sit together and watch the same film on TV with no discomfort. They listen to disgusting songs on the radio while driving together. What happened to our sense of shame? What happened to our dignity? You can sit with your parents and watch something inappropriate, and all that's left is the fast-forward button? That's what your dignity is reduced to?

These ayahs aren't just historical teachings. We're supposed to maintain our character with these kinds of guidelines.

translation: And when they commit an immorality, they say, "We found our fathers doing it, and Allah has ordered us to do it." Say, "Indeed, Allah does not order immorality. Do you say about Allah that which you do not know
commentary:
**وَإِذَا فَعَلُوا۟ فَـٰحِشَةً قَالُوا۟ وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهَآ ءَابَآءَنَا وَٱللَّهُ أَمَرَنَا بِهَا ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ ۖ أَتَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ**

"And when they commit an immorality, they say, 'We found our fathers doing it, and Allah has ordered us to do it.' Say, 'Indeed, Allah does not order immorality. Do you say about Allah that which you do not know?'"

The ayah addresses what happens وَإِذَا فَعَلُوا۟ فَـٰحِشَةً (whenever they do something shameless, inappropriate, disgusting, indecent). The word فَـٰحِشَةً comes from the root that relates to excess and wildness—it's when people act like wild animals, when they get close to that level of behavior.

Their justification is telling: وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهَآ ءَابَآءَنَا (we found our ancestors doing the same thing). Some scholars mention this is a reference to the idol worshippers in Mecca before Islam. They had this idea that when you came out of your mother, you were as God wanted you to be—naked. So if you really wanted to get close to God, they believed you should take all your clothes off and perform tawaf around the Kaaba. The highest form of their spirituality was to strip themselves and run around the Kaaba naked, under the claim that they were following ancestral tradition.

But فَـٰحِشَةً doesn't have to take that extreme form. There are other manifestations too. Look at Muslim weddings as a clear example. The extravagance, the mixing, the music—when you try to have a simple, dignified wedding according to Islamic principles, what do you hear? "We found our ancestors doing it this way. Your uncle did it this way. Your cousin did it this way. Are you saying all of them did haram? Don't you want your mother to be happy? Doesn't Islam want your mother to be happy?"

When you try to resist, suddenly your Islam is questioned. But this is just one day, you might think—one time in your life. Yet this is the one day that sets the tone for your entire marriage. You want to start it with disobedience? You're going to celebrate an act of worship—nikah—which is from the sunnah of the Prophet ﷺ. It's an act of worship. You wouldn't play music and have inappropriate gatherings during prayer. Why would you do it for another act of worship?

"But it's just one day, it's okay." No—stick to your guns. Be respectful with your family, but stick to your guns. This change is not going to come by us caving in. We talk about bringing Islam to the world, but we have a hard time bringing it to our own families, to our own traditions. We worry: "What is this one going to say? What is that one going to say?"
