And incredibly, to this day, Muslims will tell you "Allah ﷻ ordered us this way. Allah ﷻ doesn't want us to be so uptight." There's this perception that the more religious you get, the less you'll enjoy life, that everything becomes haram around you.

Allah's ﷻ response is direct: قُلْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ (Tell them: No doubt about it, Allah does not command, does not order and instruct anybody to do lewd, shameless acts). Then He asks: أَتَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (Are you saying against Allah that which you have no knowledge of?)

This is the reality—people who stand up for these kinds of things and use Allah's ﷻ name have, pretty much all the time, no idea what they're talking about. And they have to be called out like that: you have no idea what you're talking about.

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "My Lord has ordered justice and that you maintain yourselves [in worship of Him] at every place [or time] of prostration, and invoke Him, sincere to Him in religion." Just as He originated you, you will return [to life]
commentary:
**قُلْ أَمَرَ رَبِّى بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ عِندَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ وَٱدْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ ۚ كَمَا بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ**

"Say, 'My Lord has ordered justice and that you maintain yourselves [in worship of Him] at every place of prostration, and invoke Him, sincere to Him in religion.' Just as He originated you, you will return [to life]."

After refuting the false claim that Allah ﷻ commands shamelessness, He now tells us what He actually does command: قُلْ أَمَرَ رَبِّى بِٱلْقِسْطِ (Tell them: My Master commands to stay firm on justice, to be committed to justice and to stay away from injustice).

Then comes a beautiful instruction: وَأَقِيمُوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ عِندَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ (establish your faces at every place of prostration). In other words, make sure you show up at every time of prayer and every place of prayer. The word مَسْجِدٍ refers to both the time of prayer and the place of prayer.

وَٱدْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ (And call on Him, sincerely making your religion to Him). Notice what Allah ﷻ did here—instead of giving a lengthy sermon about not being shameless, He said: pray, take your salah seriously. Then that modesty and shame will come automatically.

Then comes a profound statement: كَمَا بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ (As He started you off is the way you'll be coming back). Why is this important? When you were started off—when you were born—you were without clothes. When you will be raised on Judgment Day, you will be without clothes. But today, in this life, you have to be covered. This connects the entire discussion—from the story of Adam (AS) losing his clothing in Paradise, to our need for modesty in this world, to our return to Allah ﷻ in the next.

translation: A group [of you] He guided, and a group deserved [to be in] error. Indeed, they had taken the devils as allies instead of Allah while they thought that they were guided
commentary:
**فَرِيقًا هَدَىٰ وَفَرِيقًا حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةُ ۗ إِنَّهُمُ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ**

"A group [of you] He guided, and a group deserved [to be in] error. Indeed, they had taken the devils as allies instead of Allah while they thought that they were guided."

This ayah divides humanity into two groups. فَرِيقًا هَدَىٰ (A huge group of people He did guide). وَفَرِيقًا حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةُ (And a huge group of people—misguidance materialized on them, manifested, became a reality for them).

Why did misguidance become their reality? إِنَّهُمُ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ (They're the ones who took devils as close friends, as protective friends, as opposed to Allah, besides Allah).

And here's the tragic part: وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ (And they're under the confident assumption that they are the ones committed to guidance). They think they're on the right path. They've convinced themselves that what they're doing is correct, that their justifications are valid, that their ancestral traditions are more important than divine command.

translation: O children of Adam, take your adornment at every masjid, and eat and drink, but be not excessive. Indeed, He likes not those who commit excess
commentary:
**۞ يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ خُذُوا۟ زِينَتَكُمْ عِندَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ وَكُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ وَلَا تُسْرِفُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ**

"O Children of Adam, take your adornment at every masjid, and eat and drink, but be not excessive. Indeed, He likes not those who commit excess."

This ayah beautifully combines the themes that have been building throughout this passage. Allah ﷻ just told us not to be shameless. Before that, He told us He gave us clothes to beautify ourselves. Then He told us to pray to Him at every time of prayer. Now He says: يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ خُذُوا۟ زِينَتَكُمْ عِندَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ (O Children of Adam, take your beautification at every point of prayer and every place of prostration).

When you come to pray, at every prayer, dress beautifully. خُذُوا۟ زِينَتَكُمْ—take the beautification I revealed to you. Beautification includes putting on nice scent, perfume, good clothes at every point of prayer and every place of prostration. When you go to the masjid, dress nicely. Don't dress like you're homeless. Don't go in your wrinkled clothes that stay crumpled in your closet—your "religious outfit." Don't go looking like you just rolled out of bed. Dress properly.

The Prophet ﷺ taught us to dress up even at home when we're about to make salah. You're going to talk to Allah ﷻ—dress up for it. That's an attitude Allah ﷻ is teaching us. You're coming to talk to Me? You should dress up. People dress up for job interviews, for graduation ceremonies, to meet someone important. Who are you going to meet when you make salah? Beautify yourselves.

Then comes a remarkable statement: وَكُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ (And eat and drink). This is the Arabic expression for enjoying yourself—live life! Allah ﷻ is not telling you not to enjoy life. Remember, Adam and Hawwa (AS) were told in Paradise: eat whatever you want, wherever you want. Here, Allah ﷻ is not saying you have to suffer. We don't have that concept in Islam. We don't have the concept that you must live a miserable life to get close to Allah ﷻ.

Some extremes developed historically in certain Sufi traditions where practitioners wore uncomfortable wool clothing to remind themselves this world is not for pleasure. That's an extreme. Yes, we don't live for this world, but there's nothing wrong with enjoying it. Allah ﷻ didn't forbid that from us.

Now, put this in perspective. These are the Sahabah (RA) listening to this in Mecca. They're struggling, having a hard time in life. Their major task right now is not to enjoy life but to fulfill the mission ahead of them with the Messenger ﷺ. Everything around them is difficulty and persecution. And even at that point, they're being told not to forget that this religion is not about misery. Even in their hardship, Allah ﷻ reminds them: eat well, don't go hungry unnecessarily.

But then comes the limit: وَلَا تُسْرِفُوٓا۟ (Don't go over the limits, don't be excessive). إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (Indeed, He does not like those who go overboard).

The word إِسْرَاف means to exceed what is actually appropriate. It's different from doing something haram. إِسْرَاف is when you overdo something that in and of itself is legitimate. Eating halal food is good—don't overeat, even if it's halal. That would be israf. Spending is halal—don't overspend. Sports are halal—don't make them consume your entire life. Sleeping is legitimate—don't oversleep. Keep limits.

Shopping is halal, entertainment has its place, but when every one of your free time conversations revolves around shopping, entertainment, sports or technology, then you know you have a problem. I'm not saying these things are entirely bad—I watch cartoons with my kids, and we talk about them. But they can't take over your life.

You can't have so many games on your phone that you can't live a single free minute without playing them. People can't even put that stuff down in the bathroom—that's disturbing. At that point, you're not living a balanced life.

Islam is different from other value systems. Islam teaches us to enjoy the good things of this world but within limits. Don't let entertainment invade your mind to the point where it controls you. Don't overeat, even at a halal restaurant. Don't overspend, even on permissible things. Don't oversleep, even though sleep is necessary.

Allah ﷻ is teaching us a balanced approach to life—take care of yourself, enjoy what He has provided, but always maintain the boundaries He has set. This is the middle path of Islam, where we neither live lives of extreme asceticism nor fall into the trap of excessive indulgence. Both extremes lead away from the path Allah ﷻ has outlined for us.

translation: Say, "Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has produced for His servants and the good [lawful] things of provision?" Say, "They are for those who believe during the worldly life [but] exclusively for them on the Day of Resurrection." Thus do We detail the verses for a people who know
commentary:
قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَخْرَجَ لِعِبَادِهِۦ وَٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ مِنَ ٱلرِّزْقِ ۚ قُلْ هِىَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا خَالِصَةً يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ

Notice the powerful construction in this ayah. Allah ﷻ doesn't say "the beauty that Allah gave" or "the beauty Allah created." He says زِينَةَ ٱللَّهِ - the beauty of Allah. He attributes that beauty directly to Himself. This is significant because when Allah ﷻ commands us to dress well and present ourselves properly, He's teaching us that this isn't just about appearance. The human being is a beautiful creation of Allah ﷻ, and when we take care of ourselves properly, we become a manifestation of His beautiful signs on this earth.

The ayah continues: الَّتِىٓ أَخْرَجَ لِعِبَادِهِۦ - "that which He extracted, He brought out for His slaves." Then Allah ﷻ asks: وَٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ مِنَ ٱلرِّزْقِ - "Who is going to make haram the good and pure things from provisions?"

This question addresses two dangerous extremes. On one end are people who make everything halal regardless of divine guidance. On the other extreme are those who become so excessively religious that they make even the halal things haram for themselves. They live depressed lives because they mistakenly believe Allah ﷻ doesn't want them to be happy. You'll encounter people like this sometimes in the community - their default state is a frown, constantly looking for something wrong. This isn't the spirit of Islam. We must be positive people.

Allah ﷻ is describing the halal provisions as beautiful here, which is remarkable. These blessings - قُلْ هِىَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - are for those who believe in this worldly life. خَالِصَةً يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ - And they will be exclusively for the believers on the Day of Resurrection. The message is clear: believers can enjoy a good life here too, and these blessings will be exclusively theirs in the next life.

كَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ - "That is how We thoroughly explain the miraculous signs for a nation that wants to know." This was particularly directed at the Quraysh, who thought that coming to Islam would end all the joys in their lives - all their freedoms, all their pleasures would stop. But Allah ﷻ is saying: there are beautiful things that are halal for you, and the things you desire that are evil and ugly - those are what's truly forbidden.

translation: Say, "My Lord has only forbidden immoralities - what is apparent of them and what is concealed - and sin, and oppression without right, and that you associate with Allah that for which He has not sent down authority, and that you say about Allah that which you do not know
commentary:
قُلْ إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ رَبِّىَ ٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا وَمَا بَطَنَ وَٱلْإِثْمَ وَٱلْبَغْىَ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَأَن تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا وَأَن تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ: قُلْ إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ رَبِّىَ ٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ - "Tell them: the only thing my Master forbade is الْفَوَٰحِشَ (lewd, inappropriate, indecent things), all forms of them." Then He specifies: مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا وَمَا بَطَنَ - "whatever is obvious of those things and whatever is concealed, whatever is hidden."

The word بَطَنَ literally means "what's inside, what's in the belly." This encompasses secret shamelessness, implicit indecency, shamelessness expressed through literature, through poetry, through implication, through allegorical speech. Even satire that promotes indecency is forbidden. Allah ﷻ doesn't want any of that.

وَٱلْإِثْمَ وَٱلْبَغْىَ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ - "And He forbade sin, and rebellion without any justification." وَأَن تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا - "And that you should associate with Allah something for which He sent down no authority, no authorization." وَأَن تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ - "And that you should say against Allah that which you have no knowledge of."

translation: And for every nation is a [specified] term. So when their time has come, they will not remain behind an hour, nor will they precede [it]
commentary:
وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ سَاعَةً ۖ وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ

After these warnings comes a sobering reminder: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ - For every single nation without exception, there is a deadline. Allah ﷻ isn't going to keep endlessly warning. فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهُمْ - Then when their deadline does arrive, when that time comes, لَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ سَاعَةً - they're not able to procrastinate or put it off even for a single hour. وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ - And they're not able to bring it any closer or advance it even slightly.

translation: O children of Adam, if there come to you messengers from among you relating to you My verses, then whoever fears Allah and reforms - there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve
commentary:
يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ إِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّنكُمْ يَقُصُّونَ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِى ۙ فَمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ وَأَصْلَحَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ

يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ - O Children of Adam! إِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّنكُمْ - If and when any messengers at all would come to you - and they really will - مِّنكُمْ - that are from among you, يَقُصُّونَ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِى - narrating onto you, reading onto you so eloquently My miraculous signs.

فَمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ وَأَصْلَحَ - Then whoever would want to protect themselves and fix themselves up, correct their behavior and do the right thing, فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ - then there's not going to be any fear on them, and they're not going to be the ones that are grieving.

translation: But the ones who deny Our verses and are arrogant toward them - those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ عَنْهَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا - And those who lied against Our miraculous signs, وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ عَنْهَآ - and tried to show their arrogance. اسْتَكْبَرُوا means to try to become great, to show your arrogance. أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ - Those are the companions of the Fire, هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ - in which they will remain.

translation: And who is more unjust than one who invents about Allah a lie or denies His verses? Those will attain their portion of the decree until when Our messengers come to them to take them in death, they will say, "Where are those you used to invoke besides Allah?" They will say, "They have departed from us," and will bear witness against themselves that they were disbelievers
commentary:
فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَنَالُهُمْ نَصِيبُهُم مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنَا يَتَوَفَّوْنَهُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ ضَلُّوا۟ عَنَّا وَشَهِدُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ كَـٰفِرِينَ

فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ - Who could be a worse wrongdoer مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا - than someone who made up a baseless lie against Allah ﷻ, an offensive fabrication against Him? أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦٓ - Or deliberately lied against His miraculous signs to delegitimize them, to make people not take them seriously?

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَنَالُهُمْ نَصِيبُهُم مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ - Those people, their portion - what they deserve - نَصِيب means what they deserve - their portion is going to come to them from the Book, meaning from the record that's being written for them. Allah ﷻ has decided that every crime they've made against the Quran and the revelation is being documented.

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنَا يَتَوَفَّوْنَهُمْ - Until the time when Our messengers come to them - meaning the angels of death - taking them away. قَالُوٓا۟ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ - They'll be asking them: "Where are those things that you used to call besides Allah? What's that stuff y'all used to be calling? Where'd it go?"

In other places in the Quran, we learn that these angels will be beating them on their faces and their backs. When you hit somebody in their face, they recoil forward. When you hit somebody on the back, they recoil backward. The angels ask them while striking them: "Where are those guys? Call them. Let's see."

The disbelievers used to make arrogant statements against the Muslims. Now the angels engage in this devastating questioning. قَالُوا۟ ضَلُّوا۟ عَنَّا - They'll say: "We lost them. They're lost. We can't find them." وَشَهِدُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ كَـٰفِرِينَ - And they will bear witness against their own selves that they were, in fact, the ones who were disbelievers, the ones who were ungrateful.

translation: [Allah] will say, "Enter among nations which had passed on before you of jinn and mankind into the Fire." Every time a nation enters, it will curse its sister until, when they have all overtaken one another therein, the last of them will say about the first of them "Our Lord, these had misled us, so give them a double punishment of the Fire. He will say, "For each is double, but you do not know
commentary:
قَالَ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ فِىٓ أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِكُم مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ فِى ٱلنَّارِ ۖ كُلَّمَا دَخَلَتْ أُمَّةٌ لَّعَنَتْ أُخْتَهَا ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا ٱدَّارَكُوا۟ فِيهَا جَمِيعًا قَالَتْ أُخْرَىٰهُمْ لِأُولَىٰهُمْ رَبَّنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ أَضَلُّونَا فَـَٔاتِهِمْ عَذَابًا ضِعْفًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ قَالَ لِكُلٍّ ضِعْفٌ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تَعْلَمُونَ

Now we're witnessing Judgment Day. We ended the story of Adam (AS) and we're moving toward the end of history. قَالَ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ فِىٓ أُمَمٍ - "Go ahead, enter among all the other nations قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِكُم - that have come much before you مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ فِى ٱلنَّارِ - from among the jinn and human beings that are already waiting in the Fire."

They're about to enter Hellfire, and they see generation after generation, previous nations, ancient civilizations of the world, their own ancestors - they see them already inside. كُلَّمَا دَخَلَتْ أُمَّةٌ لَّعَنَتْ أُخْتَهَا - Every time a new nation enters, imagine this: every new batch entering Hell, it curses its sister. In other words, it curses its predecessor.

"You are the people that set those traditions that we followed, that we were so committed to! Because of you, we rejected the messengers! Oh, I hate you so much! How were you so foolish? Why did you teach us these things? Where did you get them from anyway?" They're cursing their predecessors out.

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا ٱدَّارَكُوا۟ فِيهَا جَمِيعًا - Until all of them have slowly descended and come to meet with each other, all together. ادَّارَكُوا comes from تَدَارَكَ, from دَرَكَ - to come down, to catch up with each other. قَالَتْ أُخْرَىٰهُمْ لِأُولَىٰهُمْ - Then the later ones, the later generations, the new entries, the newcomers, will say to the earliest ones, the ones who have been there for a while:

رَبَّنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ أَضَلُّونَا - "Our Master! They're the ones that misguided us!" This is Shaytan's legacy continued. Never take responsibility for yourself. Even when you're burning in Hell, they refuse accountability. فَـَٔاتِهِمْ عَذَابًا ضِعْفًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ - "Give them punishment that's twice as much from the Fire!"

قَالَ لِكُلٍّ ضِعْفٌ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تَعْلَمُونَ - The response comes: "Everybody gets double. Don't you worry." Why did they say the ancestors should get double? Because they misguided themselves AND they misguided others. But Allah ﷻ says: "You people deserve double too. Because on the one hand, you should have been smart enough to know that you're human beings. You should have abandoned their tradition. And you added to their sins just like they added to yours. They left a bad tradition for you, and you engaged in bad deeds. Both of you get doubled."

translation: And the first of them will say to the last of them, "Then you had not any favor over us, so taste the punishment for what you used to earn
commentary:
وَقَالَتْ أُولَىٰهُمْ لِأُخْرَىٰهُمْ فَمَا كَانَ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا مِن فَضْلٍ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ

وَقَالَتْ أُولَىٰهُمْ لِأُخْرَىٰهُمْ - And then the ancestors say to their newer generation: فَمَا كَانَ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا مِن فَضْلٍ - "You didn't have any advantage over us. You weren't any better than us. You didn't have any preference over us." فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ - "Then taste the punishment over what you earned yourselves!"

Now the ancestors - for whose legacy you were willing to kill, for whose traditions you were willing to fight the messengers - those same ancestors are telling you: "You're not any better than us. Burn in Hell with us now. Enjoy. This is what you worked so hard for. This is what you gave your life for. You called the entire nation to hate the messengers of Islam. Now enjoy it."

translation: Indeed, those who deny Our verses and are arrogant toward them - the gates of Heaven will not be opened for them, nor will they enter Paradise until a camel enters into the eye of a needle. And thus do We recompense the criminals
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ عَنْهَا لَا تُفَتَّحُ لَهُمْ أَبْوَٰبُ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَلَا يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ حَتَّىٰ يَلِجَ ٱلْجَمَلُ فِى سَمِّ ٱلْخِيَاطِ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ عَنْهَا - No doubt about it: those who lied against Our miraculous signs and showed arrogance regarding them, لَا تُفَتَّحُ لَهُمْ أَبْوَٰبُ ٱلسَّمَآءِ - the doors of the sky will not be opened, not even a little, for them.

The word تُفَتَّحُ implies a slow, gradual opening. If a door opens even slightly, you can slip in. The idea here is that when souls are taken up to the first heaven, what happens? They're sent back down. The gates aren't even opened a crack. لَا تُفَتَّحُ لَهُمْ أَبْوَٰبُ ٱلسَّمَآءِ - The doors aren't opened at all.

وَلَا يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ - And just like that, they're not going to be entering Paradise. حَتَّىٰ يَلِجَ ٱلْجَمَلُ فِى سَمِّ ٱلْخِيَاطِ - Until the camel passes through the eye of the needle.

The word حَتَّىٰ (until) is powerful here because it creates anticipation. They're not going to be entering until... what? Until when? "Okay, okay, until what? Then we can enter?" Until the camel enters through... "Okay, the camel enters through a pass, and then we can enter?" No. Until the camel enters through the eye of a needle. In other words: it ain't gonna happen.

This expression is still used in Arabic to this day when something is absolutely impossible. "When are you gonna come over to my house?" "I'll come when the camel passes through the eye of a needle" - meaning never.

وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ - And that is how We compensate criminals.

translation: They will have from Hell a bed and over them coverings [of fire]. And thus do We recompense the wrongdoers
commentary:
لَهُم مِّن جَهَنَّمَ مِهَادٌ وَمِن فَوْقِهِمْ غَوَاشٍ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

لَهُم مِّن جَهَنَّمَ مِهَادٌ - They're going to have a cradle in Hellfire, out of Hellfire. Allah ﷻ will make for them a مِهَاد - a cradle. مِهَاد is what a mother provides when she hugs her child. Jahannam is going to hug them.

Some descriptions by the Prophet ﷺ of the punishment of the grave mention that for the criminal, the grave squeezes them so much that their ribs intertwine. It crushes them. And that's not even Jahannam yet - that's just the punishment of the grave. Now this is Jahannam itself.

وَمِن فَوْقِهِمْ غَوَاشٍ - And above them, they're going to have غَوَاشٍ - layers upon layers upon layers of covers. وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - And that is how We compensate wrongdoers. Layers upon layers are piled on top of them.

translation: But those who believed and did righteous deeds - We charge no soul except [within] its capacity. Those are the companions of Paradise; they will abide therein eternally
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ - And those who believed and did the righteous deeds. Notice the beautiful structure of this sentence. The actual statement is: "Those who believe and do good deeds - they did a few good things expected of them - they're the people of Paradise in which they will remain." That's the complete sentence.

But there's an interjection in the middle: لَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَآ - Before we even get to Jannah, let Me tell you something: We never make anybody carry a burden except that which they can carry.

Don't think: "Oh, those believers who did good deeds - not me." No! Allah ﷻ says: I never gave you a burden you couldn't carry. You could have been those people. You can't make that excuse. This interjection is powerful - someone might read this and say, "But those righteous people? That's not me. Where's my category?" Why are you absolving yourself of responsibility? Allah ﷻ isn't asking for much. You can live without excessive sin. You can live a good life.

Then He continues: أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ - Those are the companions of Paradise, in which they will remain.

translation: And We will have removed whatever is within their breasts of resentment, [while] flowing beneath them are rivers. And they will say, "Praise to Allah, who has guided us to this; and we would never have been guided if Allah had not guided us. Certainly the messengers of our Lord had come with the truth." And they will be called, "This is Paradise, which you have been made to inherit for what you used to do
commentary:
وَنَزَعْنَا مَا فِى صُدُورِهِم مِّنْ غِلٍّ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ وَقَالُوا۟ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى هَدَىٰنَا لِهَـٰذَا وَمَا كُنَّا لِنَهْتَدِىَ لَوْلَآ أَنْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ ۖ لَقَدْ جَآءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَنُودُوٓا۟ أَن تِلْكُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةُ أُورِثْتُمُوهَا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

وَنَزَعْنَا مَا فِى صُدُورِهِم مِّنْ غِلٍّ - We will remove from them, We will pull out of them - surgically removed from their chests, from their feelings, from their sentiments - any type of غِلّ. غِلّ is secret animosity, secret hatred toward someone, secret dislike, hidden resentment.

This happens between Muslims too. You're working somewhere and a boss said something mean. A volunteer didn't get along with another. Students preparing for a test had a conflict. Something happened that didn't sit well with you. Now you don't sit in the same room with that person - you sit diagonally opposite so you can keep an eye on them. There's this ill feeling that developed and it just kept festering, and you couldn't get rid of it.

You were on opposite sides of a dispute at the masjid with the board members. You used to be part of some organization and then you left, and there's tension. You used to volunteer somewhere and you don't anymore, and there's friction. This happens. It happens between Muslims, between friends. Sometimes it's incredibly hard to get rid of these feelings. No matter how hard you try, you just can't release them.

Allah ﷻ says: on that Day, We will remove this غِلّ, this tension, this thing inside. He'll get rid of it. All of us, to some extent, have this in our lives. There's somebody who hurt our feelings and never really apologized for it. Maybe they don't even know that we harbor that feeling toward them. But it lingers. Allah ﷻ will remove those feelings.

تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ - And underneath them, rivers are going to be flowing. Notice: first He took care of the inside, then the outside. وَقَالُوا۟ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ - Then they will say: "Alhamdulillah! All praise and gratitude, all credit goes to Allah ﷻ!"

ٱلَّذِى هَدَىٰنَا لِهَـٰذَا - "The One who guided us to this." What is "this"? Isn't it Jannah? The gardens? هَدَىٰنَا لِهَـٰذَا isn't just referring to the physical garden. The masculine demonstrative pronoun هَـٰذَا is inclusive - it includes everything: the removal of those negative feelings. Once those feelings are removed, you really get to enjoy the garden.

If you're in a bad mood and you're in a beautiful place, the place doesn't look as beautiful, does it? What did Allah ﷻ do first? He got rid of the hidden resentment. Then He showed them the gardens. And they're thinking: "Oh, I can really enjoy this now. All praise belongs to Allah who guided me to this. Now I know what this feels like."

One of the greatest joys in life is to have ill feelings removed. It's often impossible for us in this world. Sometimes those feelings just persist. But they'll experience this for the first time in Jannah. That's why they say: ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى هَدَىٰنَا لِهَـٰذَا - "Alhamdulillah for the One who guided us to this!"

وَمَا كُنَّا لِنَهْتَدِىَ لَوْلَآ أَنْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ - "And we could never have been ones who were going to be committed to guidance. We couldn't have been guiding ourselves at all. We could never possibly have done that لَوْلَآ أَنْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ - had it not been that Allah guided us."

لَوْلَآ is significant here because it validates both realities simultaneously. What they're saying is: "My commitment to guidance was not going to be sufficient at all, had it not been that Allah ﷻ completed it with His favor, with His completion of guidance." Both are given credit. We couldn't have completely attained guidance had Allah ﷻ not given us guidance, but we had our commitment, and Allah ﷻ more than fulfilled His end.

لَقَدْ جَآءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ - "And the messengers of our Master - they really did come with the truth!"

Can you imagine this? They're inside Jannah, they're looking at it and thinking: "Oh, that ayah! That's what that meant! Oh, look at that! That's the tafsir of the ayah I thought I understood before! That river, those huge trees - the messengers really did bring the truth!" They're looking at this magnificent reality and saying: "It's so true. It's so true."

وَنُودُوٓا۟ أَن تِلْكُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةُ - And then there's going to be an announcement made, a public service announcement: "That is Jannah, everybody!" أُورِثْتُمُوهَا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ - "All of you have inherited it! It's yours now because of the things that you used to do!"

Look at the beautiful contrast. The previous statement says: "We didn't do anything. Allah ﷻ got us here." And Allah ﷻ says: "No, you did so much work!" The attitude of the slave: "I didn't do anything. I couldn't have gotten here unless You guided me." And the attitude of Allah ﷻ: "I'm so proud of you. You did so much good work."

بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ - "Because of the things you used to do." That's why you're getting this Jannah. Both are true. We have to do work for Jannah, but we won't get it until Allah ﷻ grants it to us. Allah ﷻ showers His mercy because our deeds by themselves are not enough. But those little deeds, those few deeds - Allah ﷻ is so proud of them that He even makes an announcement about it.

Judgment Day is already over. The deeds have already been counted. But they're in Jannah now, and He's so pleased with them that He makes this declaration. This last part of the ayah is one of those statements in the Quran that captures the real joy of Jannah. The real joy of Jannah isn't just the gardens, the drinks, the palaces, the beauty. All of that is wonderful. But you know that feeling when your father comes over to you and says: "I'm proud of you. I'm so happy with you today"? When your teacher says: "I want to let you know I'm very, very proud of you. I'm honored to call you my student"? That makes your day.

Now imagine hearing that from Allah ﷻ. That's your Jannah. "It's yours because of what you did." No words can describe what that feeling is going to be like. May Allah ﷻ grant us entrance into Jannah and allow us to hear these beautiful words.

translation: And the companions of Paradise will call out to the companions of the Fire, "We have already found what our Lord promised us to be true. Have you found what your Lord promised to be true?" They will say, "Yes." Then an announcer will announce among them, "The curse of Allah shall be upon the wrongdoers
commentary:
وَنَادَىٰٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلنَّارِ أَن قَدْ وَجَدْنَا مَا وَعَدَنَا رَبُّنَا حَقًّا فَهَلْ وَجَدتُّم مَّا وَعَدَ رَبُّكُمْ حَقًّا ۖ قَالُوا۟ نَعَمْ ۚ فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌۢ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن لَّعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

The people of paradise call out to the people of Hellfire in what represents the early phases of separation between these two groups. They've both finally entered their respective places, and now begins a profound exchange. The people of Jannah announce: "We found whatever our Master promised us to be true." Then comes a striking question directed at the inhabitants of the Fire: "How did you find whatever your Master promised you to be true? How's it going down there?"

The response from the people of Hellfire is simply "Yes" - a defeated acknowledgment, an admission that they were wrong and the believers were right.

Then a caller makes an announcement between them, declaring that the curse of Allah ﷻ falls upon the wrongdoers. This announcement serves a dual purpose. Notice the grammatical shift that occurs here - the ayah transitions from past tense to present continuous with الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ (those who continue to obstruct). If this description were still referring only to those already in Hellfire, the grammar would indicate past action. But instead, this represents a rhetorical shift - the Quran suddenly turns to address those in the worldly life right now who are actively stopping people from the path of Allah ﷻ. This is you, the ayah declares. The wrongdoers being cursed aren't just those who have already been judged; this curse extends to anyone currently engaged in this behavior.

translation: Who averted [people] from the way of Allah and sought to make it [seem] deviant while they were, concerning the Hereafter, disbelievers
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ كَـٰفِرُونَ

This ayah describes the characteristics of these wrongdoers in detail. They obstruct themselves and others from the path of Allah ﷻ, and they pursue crookedness and deviation in that path.

One of the primary tactics used by those who try to stop others from Islam's path is this: when someone comes to Islam or shows interest in becoming Muslim, they make it their mission to raise every criticism they can find. "What about this? What about that? What about this thing in your religion?" They construct a wall of critical questions that Islam supposedly must answer before anyone should even consider it. They actively seek out perceived deviations and irregularities to present as insurmountable obstacles.

The ayah concludes by identifying their fundamental problem: وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ كَـٰفِرُونَ - in the Hereafter, these people are complete deniers. This explains their methodology. They don't actually seek answers; they seek barriers to keep people away from faith.

translation: And between them will be a partition, and on [its] elevations are men who recognize all by their mark. And they call out to the companions of Paradise, "Peace be upon you." They have not [yet] entered it, but they long intensely
commentary:
وَبَيْنَهُمَا حِجَابٌ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱلْأَعْرَافِ رِجَالٌ يَعْرِفُونَ كُلًّۢا بِسِيمَىٰهُمْ ۚ وَنَادَوْا۟ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ أَن سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ لَمْ يَدْخُلُوهَا وَهُمْ يَطْمَعُونَ

Between the people of paradise and the people of Hellfire stands a barrier, a حِجَابٌ. The full explanation of what this barrier looks like on Judgment Day comes later in the Quran, particularly in Surah Al-Hadid (57:13), where we learn more about this wall and its nature.

Then the ayah introduces a third group: people positioned upon الْأَعْرَافِ (the Heights or Elevations). The word A'raf itself carries multiple meanings - it refers to elevated places, heights from which everything can be seen. It also derives from the concept of recognition, of a place where things become known.

Who are these people? These are individuals whose good deeds and bad deeds essentially balanced out to zero. They performed righteous acts but also committed significant wrongs, and when their scales were weighed, they ended up neither clearly destined for paradise nor clearly destined for Hellfire. The final verdict on them hasn't yet been passed, so they exist in this in-between state. This concept differs from purgatory in Christian theology, but shares the idea of an intermediate position.

Because they're positioned on these heights, they have a unique vantage point - they can see both paradise and Hellfire clearly. Now, if you had a view of both destinations, where would you naturally keep your eyes? On paradise, obviously, for two compelling reasons: the other side is terrifying, and paradise is beautiful. Anyone in this situation would naturally want to look toward the reward rather than the punishment.

These people of the A'raf recognize everyone by their faces - يَعْرِفُونَ كُلًّۢا بِسِيمَىٰهُمْ. They recognize the sinners headed to Hellfire, and they recognize the people of paradise, because these are individuals who had associations with both groups in their worldly lives. They had righteous friends and sinful companions. They can see familiar faces on both sides.

The question becomes: whom do they choose to address? Despite being able to see both groups, they call out to the people of paradise, saying "سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ" (Peace be upon you). The ayah then adds a poignant detail: لَمْ يَدْخُلُوهَا وَهُمْ يَطْمَعُونَ - they haven't entered it yet, but they're calling out anyway. They're just outside paradise, just out of reach, greeting people they recognize from their earthly lives.

The word يَطْمَعُونَ (they hope) is particularly powerful. طَمَع refers to a deep, burning desire in your heart for something you desperately want, something that stays inside you for a prolonged time and won't let you go. They're consumed with hopeful longing to enter paradise.

translation: And when their eyes are turned toward the companions of the Fire, they say, "Our Lord, do not place us with the wrongdoing people
commentary:
۞ وَإِذَا صُرِفَتْ أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ تِلْقَآءَ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلنَّارِ قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

Notice the precise language here: صُرِفَتْ أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ - "their eyes are turned." The passive voice is crucial. Their eyes don't turn on their own volition; rather, their eyes are turned by Allah ﷻ. They naturally want to keep looking at paradise, avoiding the terrifying sight of Hellfire. But Allah ﷻ turns their gaze, forcing them to look at the other side as well. When their vision is directed toward the people of the Fire, they don't receive just a glimpse - the word تِلْقَآءَ (tilqa'a) indicates they get a full, penetrating view deep into the reality of Hellfire.

Their immediate response reveals their terror: "رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ" (Our Master, don't place us with the wrongdoing nation). Notice they don't address the people of Hellfire at all. Why? Because any association with them feels dangerous - guilty by association. They want nothing to do with that group. Instead, they speak only to Allah ﷻ, pleading not to be associated with the wrongdoers.

translation: And the companions of the Elevations will call to men [within Hell] whom they recognize by their mark, saying, "Of no avail to you was your gathering and [the fact] that you were arrogant
commentary:
وَنَادَىٰٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأَعْرَافِ رِجَالًا يَعْرِفُونَهُم بِسِيمَىٰهُمْ قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنكُمْ جَمْعُكُمْ وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ

Eventually, the people of the A'raf do address the inhabitants of Hellfire - specifically, individuals they recognize by their faces, people they knew from their earthly lives. Their words carry a sharp, pointed message: "What good did your entire gathering do you? What benefit came from all your manpower, your numbers, your collective strength?" All that worldly power, all those connections and resources that seemed so impressive in the dunya - where did they go? They offered no protection whatsoever.

"وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ" - And all that arrogance you used to display, all that pride and haughtiness - it didn't come to your aid either, did it?

translation: [Allah will say], "Are these the ones whom you [inhabitants of Hell] swore that Allah would never offer them mercy? Enter Paradise, [O People of the Elevations]. No fear will there be concerning you, nor will you grieve
commentary:
أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أَقْسَمْتُمْ لَا يَنَالُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ ۚ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَآ أَنتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ

This ayah contains a remarkable shift. The people of the A'raf continue speaking to those in Hellfire, but now while pointing at the people of paradise. The demonstrative pronoun هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ (these people here) indicates proximity - they're pointing to people nearby, emphasizing how close paradise is, yet how far the people of Hellfire are from reaching it.

"Remember these people?" they ask the inhabitants of the Fire. "These are the ones you used to swear about. You used to say, 'There's no way Allah ﷻ will show mercy to that slave, to that Persian convert, to that Roman, to those young fools who became Muslim. These weak, insignificant people - you think Allah ﷻ will show them special mercy? Come on, who are you kidding?'"

Then comes the devastating response, but it's not directed at the people of Hellfire. Instead, it's addressed to those very believers they used to mock: "ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَآ أَنتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ" (Enter paradise - there is no fear upon you, and you will not grieve).

The people of the A'raf essentially say to the inhabitants of Hellfire: "You remember making fun of these people? Well, they've been told to enter paradise. They're doing just fine. How's it going for you?"

translation: And the companions of the Fire will call to the companions of Paradise, "Pour upon us some water or from whatever Allah has provided you." They will say, "Indeed, Allah has forbidden them both to the disbelievers
commentary:
وَنَادَىٰٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ أَنْ أَفِيضُوا۟ عَلَيْنَا مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ أَوْ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ حَرَّمَهُمَا عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

After conversations between the people of the A'raf and both sides, we now witness a direct exchange between the people of Hellfire and the people of paradise - one of the few such conversations recorded in the Quran.

The people of the Fire call out to the people of paradise with a desperate request. Notice the word they use: أَفِيضُوا۟, which comes from the root meaning to overflow, to splash out. When you have a glass of water and you jostle it so water splashes over the edges, that's what this word describes. They're not even asking for a proper drink. They're saying, "Just splash some down to us. We're not asking for the whole cup - just the overflow, just the droplets that spill out, just a sprinkle. That's all we need."

"أَفِيضُوا۟ عَلَيْنَا مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ" - Just pour on us a little bit of water. "أَوْ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ" - Or anything else from what Allah ﷻ has provided you. You have plenty. You don't need all of it. Can't you spare just a little?

The response from the people of paradise is direct and final: "إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ حَرَّمَهُمَا عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ" (Indeed, Allah ﷻ has made both of these things forbidden to the disbelievers). We can't help you. This water, this provision - both are prohibited for you.

The implication throughout this exchange is clear: these aren't strangers calling out to each other. The people of Hellfire are calling out to people they knew in their worldly lives. The burning pain of this rejection cuts deeper because it comes from people who were once familiar to them.

The Quran provides an even more chilling description of this wall and these conversations in Surah Al-Hadid. There we learn that on Judgment Day, people are raised and heading toward paradise together. Everything seems to be going well. Then suddenly, a wall appears, and half the group finds themselves on one side while the other half ends up on the opposite side.

Those separated cry out: "What's happening? We were with you! We studied at the same institute, we watched the same videos, we learned together. How can you be on that side and I'm on this side?" The response comes back: you used to delay responding to the call of Allah ﷻ, and your life aspirations were no different than the disbelievers.

That wall has a door - one single door mentioned in Surah Al-Hadid. Why mention a door if it leads nowhere? Because it represents that something is still left open, some possibility still exists for those on the A'raf, some hope remains.

translation: Who took their religion as distraction and amusement and whom the worldly life deluded." So today We will forget them just as they forgot the meeting of this Day of theirs and for having rejected Our verses
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ لَهْوًا وَلَعِبًا وَغَرَّتْهُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ فَٱلْيَوْمَ نَنسَىٰهُمْ كَمَا نَسُوا۟ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِهِمْ هَـٰذَا وَمَا كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَجْحَدُونَ

This ayah describes those who took their religion as nothing more than entertainment and mockery, as something to play with rather than take seriously. Then comes a phrase that repeats throughout the Quran: وَغَرَّتْهُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا (worldly life deceived them). Understanding what this means is critical for every believer.

All human beings have aspirations. We aspire toward careers, homes, financial security, nice cars, healthy families, successful futures. We have social, professional, and personal goals. These aspirations aren't inherently wrong. Our religion teaches us that these worldly goals are acceptable and even good - but only when they're kept in their proper place, minimized in importance compared to our main aspiration.

When success in the next life becomes your primary goal, and worldly achievements become secondary means to that end, you're on the right path. But when these worldly aspirations become your only goals, when these become the ultimate things worth living for, then you've been deceived. You've been deluded into thinking temporary achievements constitute success worth sacrificing everything for.

The reality is that for Muslims, we should never be "done." We're never finished striving, never finished working toward our real goals. These worldly successes - Allah ﷻ may grant them to us if we have the right work ethic, but they're not what we ultimately aspire toward. We aspire for much higher things.

The ayah continues: فَٱلْيَوْمَ نَنسَىٰهُمْ كَمَا نَسُوا۟ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِهِمْ هَـٰذَا (So today We forget them just as they forgot the meeting of this Day of theirs). Understanding "forgetting" here is important. Forgetting has two meanings in both Arabic and English.

The first type is a flaw in memory: "I forgot my homework, I forgot that word." That's نِسيان as a deficiency.

The second type is intentional neglect: when your mother calls after you haven't contacted her for three days and asks, "Have you forgotten about me?" She doesn't mean you literally can't remember her. She means you've been ignoring her, treating her as insignificant, putting her aside as unimportant.

When Allah ﷻ says "We forget them," it's not the first type - Allah ﷻ has no deficiency in knowledge. It's the second type: "I'm making you insignificant. You're nobody to Me now, just as you made insignificant the meeting of this Day. You treated it as unimportant, so now you're unimportant."

The ayah ends: وَمَا كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَجْحَدُونَ (and because they used to deny Our signs). All those compelling arguments they made against the ayat of Allah ﷻ, all their debates and objections - this is the consequence.

translation: And We had certainly brought them a Book which We detailed by knowledge - as guidance and mercy to a people who believe
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ جِئْنَـٰهُم بِكِتَـٰبٍ فَصَّلْنَـٰهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ هُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ

Allah ﷻ declares: We already came to them, We already brought them a magnificent Book. فَصَّلْنَـٰهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ - We explained that Book thoroughly, We detailed it based on complete knowledge, on the basis of comprehensive understanding.

هُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ - as guidance and mercy for a nation that wants to believe. Notice the wording here: the ayah doesn't say "for a nation of believers" (لِّقَوْمٍ مُؤْمِنِينَ), which would indicate those who already believe. Instead it says لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ - for people who want to believe, who are willing to believe, who will believe. The door remains open. The invitation extends to anyone ready to accept it.

translation: Do they await except its result? The Day its result comes those who had ignored it before will say, "The messengers of our Lord had come with the truth, so are there [now] any intercessors to intercede for us or could we be sent back to do other than we used to do?" They will have lost themselves, and lost from them is what they used to invent
commentary:
هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا تَأْوِيلَهُۥ ۚ يَوْمَ يَأْتِى تَأْوِيلُهُۥ يَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ نَسُوهُ مِن قَبْلُ قَدْ جَآءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ فَهَل لَّنَا مِن شُفَعَآءَ فَيَشْفَعُوا۟ لَنَآ أَوْ نُرَدُّ فَنَعْمَلَ غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ ۚ قَدْ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ

The ayah asks: What are they waiting for except its تَأْوِيل - its true interpretation, its actual manifestation, its real fulfillment?

Understanding ta'wil is crucial here. When the Quran says "the sky will split open and turn into doors," you can read tafsir, you can study with scholars like Ibn Kathir and At-Tabari, you can understand the concepts intellectually. But the ta'wil - the actual reality of watching the sky tear apart before your eyes - that's something entirely different. That's when the abstract becomes concrete, when the promised becomes present, when the interpretation manifests into reality.

Are they waiting until they actually witness these events unfold? Until the Day comes when they can see it all happening?

يَوْمَ يَأْتِى تَأْوِيلُهُۥ - The Day its manifestation actually comes, what will happen? يَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ نَسُوهُ مِن قَبْلُ - those who ignored it, who forgot it, who treated it as insignificant for so long - they will say: قَدْ جَآءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ (The messengers of our Master did come with the truth). They finally admit it. The messengers were right. The message was true.

This exact statement appeared earlier in the Surah when describing the people of paradise calling to each other, confirming that their Lord's promises came true. Now we see the mirror image - the criminals making the same admission, but from a place of loss rather than victory.

Their desperation becomes clear in their questions: فَهَل لَّنَا مِن شُفَعَآءَ فَيَشْفَعُوا۟ لَنَآ (Is there anyone out there who can help us out? Any connections we can use? Anyone who will intercede on our behalf?). The word شُفَعَآءَ means intercessors, but in contemporary terms, they're asking: "Is there someone who can hook us up? Someone who can take the brunt of this and slip us through? Like getting a guest pass to a party you weren't invited to - can someone speak on our behalf, give us that connection?"

أَوْ نُرَدُّ فَنَعْمَلَ غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ (Or is it possible we could be sent back, so we could do things differently? So we could do other than what we used to do?). 

Notice they don't even specify what they would do. They don't say "we'll pray five times daily" or "we'll give charity" or any specific good deed. They just know they won't do what they used to do. They'll do something - anything - other than what they did before.

This vague statement reveals two profound truths. First, they never really knew what the good deeds were because they never cared to find out. They weren't interested in learning what righteousness actually required. Second, they fully admit that what they were doing was absolutely worthless. They're willing to do anything except repeat their previous actions because they now recognize how bankrupt those actions were.

The ayah concludes with Allah's ﷻ judgment: قَدْ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ (They have already bankrupted themselves). The word خَسِرُوٓا۟ means complete loss, total bankruptcy. They've put themselves in absolute loss, squandered their most valuable possession - their own souls.

وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (And lost from them is whatever they used to invent). All those things they used to make up frivolously, all the false beliefs they fabricated with malicious intent, all the lies and deceptions they created - everything they invented has disappeared, abandoned them, become utterly lost. Nothing remains of what they used to claim except the devastating reality they now face.

translation: Indeed, your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and earth in six days and then established Himself above the Throne. He covers the night with the day, [another night] chasing it rapidly; and [He created] the sun, the moon, and the stars, subjected by His command. Unquestionably, His is the creation and the command; blessed is Allah, Lord of the worlds
commentary:
إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ يُغْشِى ٱلَّيْلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ يَطْلُبُهُۥ حَثِيثًا وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ وَٱلنُّجُومَ مُسَخَّرَٰتٍۭ بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ ۗ أَلَا لَهُ ٱلْخَلْقُ وَٱلْأَمْرُ ۗ تَبَارَكَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Allah ﷻ begins by establishing His absolute authority: "Indeed, your Master is Allah." The use of "Master" (رب) immediately places us in the position of those who are owned, who belong to someone with complete authority over them.

He is the One who created the heavens and the earth in six days. We must understand that these "days" cannot be measured by our earthly standards. Later in the Quran, we learn that one day with Allah ﷻ can be equivalent to fifty thousand of our years. These are Allah's days, operating on a scale beyond our comprehension. When we think of a "day," we naturally think of the rotation of the Earth and the movement of the sun. But in this context, none of those celestial bodies existed yet. The very mechanisms we use to measure time had not been created, so we cannot impose our limited understanding of time onto Allah's creative process.

"Then He rose upon the Throne" (ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ) - this speaks to His sovereignty and dominion over all creation. The ayah continues with a beautiful description: "He makes the night cover the day, pursuing it rapidly" (يُغْشِى ٱلَّيْلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ يَطْلُبُهُۥ حَثِيثًا). The word يُغْشِى means to cover or wrap, and the phrase "pursuing it rapidly" uses the word حَثِيثًا, which comes from the root meaning to incite or encourage quick action. When applied to animals, this same root describes making them run faster, herding them swiftly. The imagery here is remarkable: the night chases the day, herding it along, rushing it out of the way with urgency and speed.

"And the sun, the moon and the stars are all subdued by His command" (وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ وَٱلنُّجُومَ مُسَخَّرَٰتٍۭ بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ). The word مُسَخَّرَٰت describes something completely overpowered, domesticated, brought under control—like a wild horse that has been tamed. These mighty celestial bodies that we gaze at in wonder are themselves completely subdued before Allah ﷻ.

Then comes a profound statement: "Unquestionably, His is the creation and the command" (أَلَا لَهُ ٱلْخَلْقُ وَٱلْأَمْرُ). One interpretation is straightforward: if all creation belongs to Him, then all authority over that creation naturally belongs to Him as well. He owns everything, so He has every right to command it.

But there's a deeper dimension explored by scholars like Fakhr al-Din al-Razi (may Allah ﷻ have mercy on him). He understood this as describing two distinct realms: the world of الخلق (creation) and the world of الأمر (command). Creation operates within the constraints of time and process. A seed must grow into a tree over months or years. But the realm of command transcends these limitations. When Allah ﷻ commands the angels in the highest heaven to descend to the lowest heaven, they do so instantaneously, beyond the mechanics of physical movement.

Consider the human being: our physical body is from the world of khalq, following natural laws of growth and decay. But our soul, our روح, is from the realm of amr. Allah ﷻ says, "They ask you about the soul. Say, 'The soul is of the affair of my Lord'" (17:85). The miracles granted to the prophets operated from the world of amr, not the world of khalq—fire refusing to burn Ibrahim (AS), the sea parting for Musa (AS). Both realms belong entirely to Allah ﷻ.

The ayah concludes with تَبَارَكَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - "Blessed is Allah, Lord of all the worlds." The word تَبَارَكَ is profound. Baraka means blessing, but more specifically, it refers to the power to create good and cause it to increase and multiply. When we say "Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Rahim" before any action, we're calling upon this divine power—that even if we're doing something good, Allah ﷻ will extract even more good from it than we could have imagined, simply because His name carries the power to multiply blessings. So تَبَارَكَ means: how full of blessings Allah ﷻ is, how overflowing with the power to increase all goodness.

translation: Call upon your Lord in humility and privately; indeed, He does not like transgressors
commentary:
ٱدْعُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ تَضَرُّعًا وَخُفْيَةً ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ

Having established who Allah ﷻ is, the Quran now teaches us how to turn to Him. These ayahs about dua (supplication) are beautifully positioned here because the essence of worship, the very core of being a servant of Allah ﷻ, is calling upon Him and returning to Him.

"Call upon your Lord تَضَرُّعًا and خُفْيَةً." The word تَضَرُّع combines two states: physical weakness and emotional overwhelm. It's when your knees feel weak, when you're on the ground with your head bowed, when you can't control your tears. It's approaching Allah ﷻ like someone who doesn't deserve to be close but quietly, humbly tries to draw near anyway—not demanding, but humbly inching closer, recognizing the vast distance between servant and Master.

And خُفْيَةً—secretly, in private moments. Not making a performance of your supplications, but pouring out your heart when you're alone with your Lord. The public, collective dua certainly has its place, but Allah ﷻ is emphasizing here the importance of that private, intimate conversation with Him.

"Indeed, He does not love those who transgress" (إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ). In this context, transgression means exceeding the proper limits in dua—turning it into a performance, making a spectacle of worship, or calling upon Allah ﷻ with arrogance rather than humility. The ethics of dua require sincerity, humility and privacy.

translation: And cause not corruption upon the earth after its reformation. And invoke Him in fear and aspiration. Indeed, the mercy of Allah is near to the doers of good
commentary:
وَلَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَـٰحِهَا وَٱدْعُوهُ خَوْفًا وَطَمَعًا ۚ إِنَّ رَحْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

"And do not cause corruption on the earth after it has been set right" (وَلَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَـٰحِهَا). Allah ﷻ had brought guidance to the earth, particularly to the land of Makkah through Ibrahim (AS). The corruption being warned against here is the return to falsehood and idol worship after the truth had been established.

Then the command continues: "And call upon Him with fear and hope" (وَٱدْعُوهُ خَوْفًا وَطَمَعًا). This is the balanced state of a believer's heart. When you raise your hands to Allah ﷻ, you should be filled with both خَوْف (fear) and طَمَع (hope, longing, desire). Fear that you might not be worthy of His response, that your sins might stand between you and His mercy. But simultaneously, overflowing with hope that He will answer, that His mercy will reach you, that your Master is generous and kind. Both emotions must coexist in the heart of the one who supplicates.

"Indeed, the mercy of Allah is near to those who do good" (إِنَّ رَحْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ). There's a beautiful grammatical point here: the word رَحْمَة (mercy) is feminine, and we would expect the adjective describing it to also be feminine—قريبة. But Allah ﷻ uses the masculine form قَرِيبٌ. This shift teaches us that it's not just the mercy of Allah ﷻ that is near—Allah ﷻ Himself is near to the believers, to those who excel in their worship and character. Both Allah ﷻ and His mercy are close to the one who strives for excellence.

translation: And it is He who sends the winds as good tidings before His mercy until, when they have carried heavy rainclouds, We drive them to a dead land and We send down rain therein and bring forth thereby [some] of all the fruits. Thus will We bring forth the dead; perhaps you may be reminded
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُرْسِلُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ بُشْرًۢا بَيْنَ يَدَىْ رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَقَلَّتْ سَحَابًا ثِقَالًا سُقْنَـٰهُ لِبَلَدٍ مَّيِّتٍ فَأَنزَلْنَا بِهِ ٱلْمَآءَ فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِۦ مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ

You might ask: how can I be sure that Allah's mercy is truly near? The answer comes immediately: "And He is the One who sends the winds as good news before His mercy" (وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُرْسِلُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ بُشْرًۢا بَيْنَ يَدَىْ رَحْمَتِهِۦ). The next time you feel a gentle breeze on your face, remember: this is a sign that Allah's mercy is near.

Imagine a farmer who has worked his land all season. If the rains don't come soon, everything will be lost—the entire year's effort destroyed, insects will devour what remains. He wakes up anxious, scanning the sky. He doesn't see clouds yet, but he feels something: wind. And that wind is itself a bearer of good news—بُشْرًۢا. Before the clouds arrive, before the rain falls, the wind comes as an announcement: the mercy of Allah ﷻ is on its way. The wind itself becomes a congratulation.

"Until, when they have carried heavy clouds" (حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَقَلَّتْ سَحَابًا ثِقَالًا) - here we encounter beautiful imagery. The word أَقَلَّتْ comes from a root meaning "to consider something small or insignificant." Picture this: these clouds are described as ثِقَالًا—heavy, massive, like mountains in the sky. Yet the winds treat them as if they weigh nothing at all. Just as you might casually toss a pen while thinking, spinning it in your hand without effort—that's the relationship between these winds and these massive clouds. The winds, empowered by Allah ﷻ, consider these heavy clouds trivial and toss them across the sky with ease.

"We drive them to a dead land, and We send down water through them, and We bring forth thereby fruits of all kinds" (سُقْنَـٰهُ لِبَلَدٍ مَّيِّتٍ فَأَنزَلْنَا بِهِ ٱلْمَآءَ فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِۦ مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ). Notice the shift in pronouns. First, the winds carry the clouds—that's their function. But then Allah ﷻ takes direct credit: "We drive them," "We send down water," "We bring forth." The winds are His servants, but the blessing comes from Him alone.

Then comes the powerful conclusion: "Thus We will bring out the dead" (كَذَٰلِكَ نُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ). Just as dead earth receives water and brings forth life, your dead body will be brought back to life. Every time rain revives barren land, you're witnessing a preview of your own resurrection. "So that you may remember" (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ)—so that you consistently make the effort to remember this reality.

translation: And the good land - its vegetation emerges by permission of its Lord; but that which is bad - nothing emerges except sparsely, with difficulty. Thus do We diversify the signs for a people who are grateful
commentary:
وَٱلْبَلَدُ ٱلطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَبَاتُهُۥ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِۦ ۖ وَٱلَّذِى خَبُثَ لَا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِدًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَشْكُرُونَ

"And the good land—its vegetation emerges by permission of its Lord" (وَٱلْبَلَدُ ٱلطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَبَاتُهُۥ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِۦ). This ayah builds directly on the imagery established in the previous verses. We were already thinking about rain and crops and earth—now Allah ﷻ wants us to think about ourselves. From this point forward, when you contemplate the earth and what grows from it, you should see a reflection of your own heart.

Good land, when it receives water, produces good crops—but even this happens بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِۦ (by the permission of its Lord). Similarly, when the revelation from the sky (the Quran) enters the heart of a human being, if that heart has maintained its purity and goodness, beautiful things emerge: faith, righteous deeds, good character. But notice: even then, even when the heart is receptive and good things result, it happens only by Allah's permission. The good earth doesn't get to take credit. The righteous person doesn't get to claim, "Look what I produced." It all happens by His permission.

"But that which is bad—nothing emerges from it except with difficulty" (وَٱلَّذِى خَبُثَ لَا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِدًا). The word نَكِد is powerful. It describes something you worked incredibly hard to obtain, but when you finally got it, the benefit was minuscule, disappointing, underwhelming. Picture climbing an entire mountain because someone told you there's treasure at the top, and when you finally reach the summit, exhausted, you find a quarter. That's nakid—massive effort for worthless result.

The corrupted heart is like bad soil. Even when it receives the revelation, even when good influences come to it, almost nothing beneficial emerges. Whatever small good does come requires enormous effort and produces minimal lasting benefit. This doesn't mean no good can ever come from such a person—the ayah acknowledges that something might emerge, but it will be nakid, underwhelming compared to the effort expended.

"Thus do We diversify the signs for a people who are grateful" (كَذَٰلِكَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَشْكُرُونَ). The connection to gratitude here is profound. Why does Allah ﷻ mention gratitude specifically? Because even people who don't believe sometimes do good things. The good that emerges nakid from bad soil represents this reality—occasional good deeds from hardened hearts. But those who are truly grateful, who have cultivated good soil in their hearts, they're the ones who produce abundant fruits from the rain of revelation.

translation: We had certainly sent Noah to his people, and he said, "O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him. Indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous Day
commentary:
لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ

Now we enter the stories of the prophets, and a question arises: which prophet is not mentioned in this surah? Ibrahim (AS). Why? Because he was already discussed extensively in Surah Al-An'am. That surah dealt more with rational arguments and theological proofs—what we call البرهان, the evidence-based approach. Now, in Surah Al-A'raf, we're in the realm of stories—الخبر (al-khabar), the historical narrative approach.

We begin with Nuh (AS), and we'll proceed chronologically through the prophets. What's striking is that these events are separated by centuries, sometimes millennia. Yet the way Allah ﷻ describes them, it feels like the same scene playing out over and over, with only minor variations. The language is deliberately similar. Why? Because thousands of years passed, and humanity didn't learn anything. The same conversation, the same rejection, the same arguments repeated across generations.

"We certainly sent Nuh to his people, and he said, 'O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him'" (لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ). This is the core message, the foundation of every prophet's call: the Oneness of Allah ﷻ, and the abolition of all false worship.

"Indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous Day" (إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ). Notice the gentleness in Nuh's approach (AS). He doesn't say, "You deserve punishment." He says, "I fear for you." This is the heart of a prophet—warning his people not from a place of superiority or condemnation, but from genuine concern and fear on their behalf.

translation: Said the eminent among his people, "Indeed, we see you in clear error
commentary:
قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ

"The eminent among his people said, 'Indeed, we see you in clear error'" (قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ). The الملأ—the chieftains, the elite, the top-ranking officials—respond. Not the common people, but those in positions of power and influence.

Their response is devastating in its simplicity: "We clearly see you in obvious error." They dismiss him completely. This is one of the great powers of political and social leadership—the ability to dismiss opposition with a single statement. They can declare someone's entire effort to be fringe thinking, propaganda, confusion. In one public pronouncement, they undermine everything.

When these leaders say, "We see you as clearly lost and confused," what message does that send to the masses? If the most educated, the most powerful, the most respected members of society all see this prophet as deluded, why should the common person waste their time listening to him? This is a sophisticated form of social pressure and intellectual intimidation.

translation: [Noah] said, "O my people, there is not error in me, but I am a messenger from the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِى ضَلَـٰلَةٌ وَلَـٰكِنِّى رَسُولٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Notice what Nuh (AS) does. The chieftains spoke to him, but he doesn't direct his response to them. He turns his attention back to his nation: "O my people, there is not error in me" (قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِى ضَلَـٰلَةٌ). He addresses the masses directly, refusing to let the elite monopolize the conversation or control the narrative.

"But I am a messenger from the Lord of all the worlds" (وَلَـٰكِنِّى رَسُولٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ). He doesn't just say "from Allah" or "from your Lord"—he says رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ, the Master of all nations, all peoples, all creation. This is significant: the message isn't tribal or local; it's universal. He's representing the Master of everything that exists.

translation: I convey to you the messages of my Lord and advise you; and I know from Allah what you do not know
commentary:
أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى وَأَنصَحُ لَكُمْ وَأَعْلَمُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

"I convey to you the messages of my Lord" (أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى)—notice the word رِسَـٰلَـٰت, plural, "messages." These are communications from the Master, letters of guidance and instruction that must be delivered.

"And I advise you" (وَأَنصَحُ لَكُمْ). The word نَصِيحَة means sincere counsel, but it has a deeper implication: advice that is intended purely for the other person's benefit, not your own. There's no hidden agenda, no self-interest. Nuh (AS) gains nothing from their belief except the pleasure of fulfilling his mission.

"And I know from Allah what you do not know" (وَأَعْلَمُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ). This isn't arrogance—it's a statement of fact. He has received revelation; they haven't. He has knowledge from the source; they're operating on assumption and tradition. This knowledge isn't about his superiority as a person, but about the truth he's been entrusted to convey.

translation: Then do you wonder that there has come to you a reminder from your Lord through a man from among you, that he may warn you and that you may fear Allah so you might receive mercy
commentary:
أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَن جَآءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنكُمْ لِيُنذِرَكُمْ وَلِتَتَّقُوا۟ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ

"Are you amazed that there has come to you a reminder from your Lord through a man from among you?" (أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَن جَآءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنكُمْ). Nuh (AS) addresses their shock directly. Is it really that surprising? Is this the stumbling block—that the messenger is a human being, someone who walks among you, eats food as you do, is known to you?

The word ذِكْرٌ is profound here—a reminder. The truth isn't something alien or new; it's a reminder of what the human فِطْرَة, the natural disposition, already knows deep down.

"That he may warn you" (لِيُنذِرَكُمْ)—this is certain, definite. The warning will be delivered whether you accept it or not.

"And that you may become righteous" (وَلِتَتَّقُوا۟)—this too is stated as purpose, as something you should embrace.

"And that you may receive mercy" (وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ)—but notice the shift. The word لَعَلَّ introduces hope, possibility. Mercy is conditional. It may come or it may not, depending on how you respond. The warning is guaranteed; taqwa is expected; but mercy? That remains in the realm of hope, because ultimately, the people might choose to reject and therefore deserve punishment instead.

Even here, at this stage of his mission, Nuh (AS) maintains hope for his people. He's still optimistic that mercy might reach them.

translation: But they denied him, so We saved him and those who were with him in the ship. And We drowned those who denied Our signs. Indeed, they were a blind people
commentary:
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ وَأَغْرَقْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا عَمِينَ

"But they denied him" (فَكَذَّبُوهُ)—after all his patience, his gentle approach, his sincere counsel, they rejected him.

"So We saved him and those with him in the ship" (فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ). The rescue is described with beautiful simplicity. Those who believed were few, but they were preserved.

"And We drowned those who denied Our signs" (وَأَغْرَقْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ). They didn't just deny a man—they denied the signs of Allah ﷻ. That's what sealed their fate.

"Indeed, they were a people who were blind" (إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا عَمِينَ). The word عَمِينَ ('amin) means blind, but not physically. They had eyes but couldn't see the truth. Despite the clarity of the message, despite the sincerity of the messenger, despite the obvious signs, they remained blind to reality.

It's worth noting that with Nuh (AS), and later with Hud (AS), we don't find elaborate miracles mentioned—no staff turning into a serpent, no hand glowing white. The miracles we do see are rescue miracles: the ark itself, salvation from the flood. The same pattern appears with Ibrahim (AS)—the knife refusing to cut, the fire refusing to burn. These are miraculous rescues, not signs presented as proof to convince the people beforehand.

Later prophets, particularly Musa (AS) and Isa (AS), will be given miracles as evidence to present to their nations. But for these early messengers, the miracle comes in how Allah ﷻ saves them from impossible situations. The message itself, the truth of the call to tawheed, was meant to be sufficient. The miraculous rescue comes only after the people have made their choice to reject.

translation: And to the 'Aad [We sent] their brother Hud. He said, "O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him. Then will you not fear Him
commentary:
وَإِلَىٰ عَادٍ أَخَاهُمْ هُودًا ۗ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ

The narrative continues in chronological order. After the destruction of Nuh's (AS) people, Allah ﷻ sent to the nation of 'Ad their brother Hud (AS). The word "brother" here emphasizes that he was one of them, from their own people. He came with the same essential message: "My people, enslave yourselves to Allah. There is no entity worthy of worship and obedience except Him."

Then Hud (AS) posed a critical question: "Why is it that you don't become conscious? Why don't you become aware and careful?" The word تَتَّقُونَ (tattaqun) carries this meaning of consciousness, awareness and carefulness before Allah ﷻ.

translation: Said the eminent ones who disbelieved among his people, "Indeed, we see you in foolishness, and indeed, we think you are of the liars
commentary:
قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰكَ فِى سَفَاهَةٍ وَإِنَّا لَنَظُنُّكَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ

Here we see a pattern emerging. Just as with Nuh (AS), the الْمَلَأُ (al-mala') spoke first—the leaders, the influential elite who disbelieved from among his nation. This teaches us something important about how nations respond to messengers: the first line of defense to undermine the message and the work of the messengers comes from the very top, from the most influential people.

Their response was twofold. First, they attacked his intelligence: "We see you immersed in سَفَاهَة (safahah)—stupidity, idiocy. You're an idiot. What you're calling people to is stupid." But when that line of attack didn't work, they shifted to questioning his motives: "We think you're lying. You're not actually stupid—we know you from before, you're smarter than this. We think you've got some alternative agenda going on."
