This is classic character assassination, a propaganda technique still used today. These exact arguments were being deployed thousands of years later against the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ by the Quraysh. When these ayahs were recited at the time of the Prophet ﷺ, they rang a very familiar bell. The tactics hadn't changed at all.

translation: [Hud] said, "O my people, there is not foolishness in me, but I am a messenger from the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِى سَفَاهَةٌ وَلَـٰكِنِّى رَسُولٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Hud (AS) responded with clarity and dignity: "My nation, I am not suffering from stupidity or idiocy." He didn't get defensive or emotional. Instead, he simply stated the truth: "However, I am a messenger from the Master of all peoples."

Notice how he addressed each accusation directly. They accused him of confusion and stupidity—he denied it plainly. Then he presented his true identity and mission. This response pattern applies to all the prophets, including the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Every accusation they faced, he ﷺ would later face as well.

translation: I convey to you the messages of my Lord, and I am to you a trustworthy adviser
commentary:
أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى وَأَنَا۠ لَكُمْ نَاصِحٌ أَمِينٌ

Hud (AS) defined his role with three key characteristics: "I am communicating to you messages of my Master, and I am to you an honest deliverer of advice and counsel."

The word نَاصِحٌ (nasih) means one who gives sincere advice, and أَمِينٌ (amin) means trustworthy, honest. These characteristics that the prophets described of themselves all apply to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. This entire conversation is meant to be superimposed on the conversation Allah's Messenger ﷺ was having with the Quraysh. These are not merely historical accounts—they are living lessons for every generation.

translation: Then do you wonder that there has come to you a reminder from your Lord through a man from among you, that he may warn you? And remember when He made you successors after the people of Noah and increased you in stature extensively. So remember the favors of Allah that you might succeed
commentary:
أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَن جَآءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنكُمْ لِيُنذِرَكُمْ ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفَآءَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَزَادَكُمْ فِى ٱلْخَلْقِ بَصْۜطَةً ۖ فَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ ءَالَآءَ ٱللَّهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ

Hud (AS) challenged their shock and disbelief: "Are you really that amazed that a reminder has come to you from your Master, delivered by a man from among yourselves, so that he could warn you?"

Sound familiar? Thousands of years later, the same conversation would happen again with the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. The Quraysh would express the same shock that the messenger was just a human being like them.

Then Hud (AS) reminded them of Allah's favors: "Remember when He made you خُلَفَاء (khulafa')—successors, keepers of the earth. You were the ones left behind after the nation of Nuh (AS) was gone." The word khulafa' carries the meaning of those who remain after others have passed, those given stewardship and authority.

Allah ﷻ had also increased them فِى ٱلْخَلْقِ بَصْۜطَةً (fil-khalqi bastatan)—in physical strength and stature. They were a powerful, imposing people. Hud (AS) concluded: "So remind yourselves of Allah's favors—His miracles and wonders—so that you may be successful."

translation: They said, "Have you come to us that we should worship Allah alone and leave what our fathers have worshipped? Then bring us what you promise us, if you should be of the truthful
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ أَجِئْتَنَا لِنَعْبُدَ ٱللَّهَ وَحْدَهُۥ وَنَذَرَ مَا كَانَ يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَا ۖ فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ

Their response reveals the depth of their attachment to ancestral tradition: "Have you come to us so we should worship Allah alone by Himself, and abandon everything our ancestors used to worship?"

This conversation takes on new meaning when we remember what was mentioned earlier in the surah—the scene of the ancestors burning in the Fire, congratulating their descendants on joining them. Here we flash back to history and hear them speaking the very words they will later come to regret. They're quoting the words of those doomed ancestors as their defense.

Then they issued a challenge: "Bring whatever it is you've been promising. Bring the punishment, if in fact you should be counted among the truthful."

translation: [Hud] said, "Already have defilement and anger fallen upon you from your Lord. Do you dispute with me concerning [mere] names you have named them, you and your fathers, for which Allah has not sent down any authority? Then wait; indeed, I am with you among those who wait
commentary:
قَالَ قَدْ وَقَعَ عَلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ رِجْسٌ وَغَضَبٌ ۖ أَتُجَـٰدِلُونَنِى فِىٓ أَسْمَآءٍ سَمَّيْتُمُوهَآ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُم مَّا نَزَّلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَـٰنٍ ۚ فَٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنِّى مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُنتَظِرِينَ

Hud's (AS) response was sobering: "From my Master, the رِجْس (rijs)—the abomination, the curse, the affliction—and His anger has already fallen upon you. It has already manifested. Your fate is sealed."

Then he exposed the emptiness of their theology: "Are you debating with me over أَسْمَآء (asma')—names that you made up yourselves, you and your ancestors? Your entire theology consists of figments of your imagination. Allah did not send any سُلْطَان (sultan)—any authorization, any validation—for any of those things at all."

Finally, he left them with a stark declaration: "Wait and see what happens. I'm among those who wait alongside you. Let's see who comes out on top."

translation: So We saved him and those with him by mercy from Us. And We eliminated those who denied Our signs, and they were not [at all] believers
commentary:
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَقَطَعْنَا دَابِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا ۖ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ

The resolution came swiftly. Allah ﷻ says: "Then We rescued him and those who were with him through a mercy that came specifically from Us."

The word قَطَعْنَا دَابِرَ (qata'na dabir) is particularly powerful. دَابِر in classical Arabic refers to disabling or paralyzing someone, to chop off the right arm and render them completely incapable. Allah ﷻ completely disabled and destroyed those who lied against His miraculous signs. The final phrase emphasizes the totality of their rejection: "They were not believers at all"—they had no intention of ever believing.

translation: And to the Thamud [We sent] their brother Salih. He said, "O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him. There has come to you clear evidence from your Lord. This is the she-camel of Allah [sent] to you as a sign. So leave her to eat within Allah 's land and do not touch her with harm, lest there seize you a painful punishment
commentary:
وَإِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا ۗ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ قَدْ جَآءَتْكُمْ بَيِّنَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ هَـٰذِهِۦ نَاقَةُ ٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ ءَايَةً ۖ فَذَرُوهَا تَأْكُلْ فِىٓ أَرْضِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَلَا تَمَسُّوهَا بِسُوٓءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

The nation of 'Ad was destroyed, and the few survivors migrated to new territory. Eventually they built and expanded, and the nation of Thamud developed. To Thamud, Allah ﷻ sent their brother Salih (AS).

His message began the same way: "My people, enslave yourselves to Allah. I don't know of any entity worthy of worship or obedience that you should submit to except Him." But Salih (AS) had something unique—he spoke with confidence because he had been given a miraculous sign: "The clearest proofs have already come to you from your Master."

He pointed to the miracle: "This is نَاقَةُ ٱللَّهِ (naqat Allah)—the she-camel of Allah—a miraculous sign specifically for you." Then came the warning and the command: "Leave it alone and let it eat on Allah's land. It's not your earth, it's Allah's earth. Let it eat. And don't touch it with any evil intent—don't even come near it. If you do, a painful punishment will grab you."

translation: And remember when He made you successors after the 'Aad and settled you in the land, [and] you take for yourselves palaces from its plains and carve from the mountains, homes. Then remember the favors of Allah and do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption
commentary:
وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفَآءَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ عَادٍ وَبَوَّأَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ تَتَّخِذُونَ مِن سُهُولِهَا قُصُورًا وَتَنْحِتُونَ ٱلْجِبَالَ بُيُوتًا ۖ فَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ ءَالَآءَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ

Notice the parallel: The previous nation was told to remember how they were left behind after Nuh's (AS) people were destroyed. Now Salih (AS) reminds Thamud: "Remember when Allah made you the ones to follow after the nation of 'Ad was destroyed." It's the same chain continuing through history.

But there's a new element here. The word بَوَّأَكُمْ (bawwa'akum) means to settle someone in the perfect circumstances—exactly where they want, with the neighbors they desire, the weather they like, the commute they prefer. Everything is ideal. Allah ﷻ didn't just let them survive—He gave them prime real estate.

And what did they do with it? "You would make out of the valleys قُصُورًا (qusur)—castles, palaces. And you would تَنْحِتُونَ (tanhitun)—carve out the mountains and make homes from them." This wasn't easy terrain, yet Allah ﷻ gave them the skill and ability to create architectural marvels. The ruins of these mountain dwellings still exist today, and they remain impressive achievements of engineering.

Salih (AS) reminded them: "So remember Allah's favors, and don't go about the land causing corruption." The word تَعْثَوْا۟ (ta'thaw) means to be corrupt and then to spread corruption to others—becoming corrupt yourself and corrupting those around you.

translation: Said the eminent ones who were arrogant among his people to those who were oppressed - to those who believed among them, "Do you [actually] know that Salih is sent from his Lord?" They said, "Indeed we, in that with which he was sent, are believers
commentary:
قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوا۟ لِمَنْ ءَامَنَ مِنْهُمْ أَتَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ صَـٰلِحًا مُّرْسَلٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلَ بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنُونَ

Here we see a shift in description. Previously, the opposition was described as الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (alladhina kafaru)—those who disbelieved. Now they're called الَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا (alladhina istakbaru)—those who were full of arrogance. This is significant because arrogance (istikbar) is directly connected to Shaytan, who was described as being مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (minal-kafirin) because he showed arrogance (استكبر). The elite of Thamud were walking in Shaytan's footsteps.

These arrogant leaders approached الَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوا۟ (alladhina stuḍ'ifu)—those who were kept weak, those who were deliberately kept powerless. Notice the passive form: there was an active attempt to suppress them and keep them weak. The elite came to them mockingly: "Do you really know for sure that Salih has been sent from his Master? Seriously, how can you be so sure?"

The response of the believers was unwavering: إِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلَ بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنُونَ—"We are complete believers in whatever he has been sent with." They used the word مَآ (ma), which is more general and encompassing. They were saying: "Whatever he gives us, we're going to believe it. Whatever we know so far and whatever we don't yet know, we're going to believe it all."

translation: Said those who were arrogant, "Indeed we, in that which you have believed, are disbelievers
commentary:
قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ

The arrogant elite responded with contempt: إِنَّا بِٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ—"Whatever you believe in, we are disbelievers in it."

Notice the linguistic subtlety. The same revelation is being discussed, but now they use الَّذِى (alladhi) instead of مَا (ma). This more specific, definite word suggests that they're saying: "We have actually come to know this message pretty well, we're fully acquainted with it, and that's exactly why we're rejecting it." They're not claiming ignorance—they're claiming full understanding and deliberate rejection.

translation: So they hamstrung the she-camel and were insolent toward the command of their Lord and said, "O Salih, bring us what you promise us, if you should be of the messengers
commentary:
فَعَقَرُوا۟ ٱلنَّاقَةَ وَعَتَوْا۟ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِمْ وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰصَـٰلِحُ ٱئْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

Then they crossed the line: فَعَقَرُوا۟ ٱلنَّاقَةَ (fa'aqaru al-naqah)—"They slaughtered the camel." The word عَقَرَ ('aqara) describes a cruel stabbing from underneath the neck.

And they didn't stop there: وَعَتَوْا۟ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِمْ (wa 'ataw 'an amri rabbihim). The word عَتَوْا۟ ('ataw) means rebellion, but specifically rebellion done as a show of defiance—a "what are you going to do about it?" kind of arrogance. They killed the camel as a dare, as a challenge.

They taunted Salih (AS) directly: "Salih, bring us whatever you're promising, if you're really from among the messengers!"

translation: So the earthquake seized them, and they became within their home [corpses] fallen prone
commentary:
فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دَارِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ

The response was immediate and devastating: فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ (fa akhadhat-humu al-rajfah)—"A violent earthquake seized them."

The word رَجْفَة (rajfah) comes from the root meaning to create a serious disturbance. It describes a violent, snapping motion—like ocean waves that slam with tremendous force, or when you jerk a rope so hard it snaps. This earthquake was that intense.

The result: فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دَارِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (fa asbahu fi darihim jathimin). The word أَصْبَحُوا۟ (asbahu) means "by morning time they became..." And جَـٰثِمِينَ (jathimin) is particularly vivid—it's used for a sick bird that can no longer fly and sits collapsed on its chest on the ground, about to die.

All of their homes were crushed into rubble, and their bodies lay toppled over like dying birds, corpses scattered everywhere on their chests. Remember how Allah ﷻ had increased them in physical strength? The last thing you'd associate with strength is powerless bodies lying dead. Yet that's exactly what their arrogance led to.

translation: And he turned away from them and said, "O my people, I had certainly conveyed to you the message of my Lord and advised you, but you do not like advisors
commentary:
فَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ وَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ رِسَالَةَ رَبِّى وَنَصَحْتُ لَكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تُحِبُّونَ ٱلنَّـٰصِحِينَ

This ayah is deeply moving. Salih (AS) turned away from the corpses of his people and said: "My nation, I delivered the message of my Master to you completely. I advised you sincerely. But you just don't love people who give advice."

Notice something important here: Was he actually talking to them? No—they were dead. This teaches us something beautiful about language and longing. When your grandmother passes away and you miss her, you might sit alone and say, "Grandma, I miss you." That doesn't mean she's floating around the room listening. It's your way of expressing sadness and love, speaking to someone who isn't even there out of longing for them.

When we send salutations upon the Prophet ﷺ in the Tashahud, some get caught up in theological debates about whether he's physically present. But we're missing the point—it's an expression of love and longing for him. The Prophet ﷺ loved his Ummah deeply.

Salih (AS) didn't celebrate the destruction of his people. There was no "I told you so," no victory dance. He was horrified. He loved them despite their rejection. So he turned away from their corpses—he couldn't even bear to look at them—and spoke words of longing as if they were still alive: لَّا تُحِبُّونَ ٱلنَّـٰصِحِينَ (la tuhibbuna al-nasihin)—"But you don't love those who give sincere advice."

This is the heart of a prophet—grief over those who rejected guidance, not triumph over their destruction.

The Quraysh hearing these accounts were being given a powerful warning: Don't let it get to the point where the Prophet ﷺ will turn away from you saying, "I advised you, but you just didn't like people who give advice." The formula of this surah is clear: Fix your future by learning from the past. History is not merely information—it's instruction for those who will listen.

translation: And [We had sent] Lot when he said to his people, "Do you commit such immorality as no one has preceded you with from among the worlds
commentary:
وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ أَتَأْتُونَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُم بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

And We sent Lut (AS) when he said to his people: "Do you commit such shameless lewdness as none has preceded you among the nations?"

This ayah marks the beginning of the account of Prophet Lut (AS) and introduces what Allah ﷻ depicts as one of the fundamental wrongs in a society. The word الْفَـٰحِشَةَ refers to a shameless, lewd act—something so aberrant that no previous nation had engaged in it in this manner. This is connected to the earlier discussion in Surah Al-Araf about الْفَوَاحِشِ, the shameless acts that corrupt societies.

What makes this particularly significant is that Allah ﷻ emphasizes the unprecedented nature of this behavior: "no individual before you ever committed such an act in this way." This wasn't simply another sin among many; it represented a complete deviation from the natural order that Allah ﷻ established for humanity.

The Islamic position on homosexuality is unambiguous—it is considered an abomination and something that Allah ﷻ depicts as fundamentally wrong in a society. However, we must distinguish between religious prohibition and how we address this as a contemporary issue. While certain historical leaders like Umar (RA) implemented specific verdicts regarding this matter, these were executive decisions made by the head of state rather than established Shariah punishments. The religion grants such authority to the leader, but this doesn't automatically make every decision part of Islamic law itself.

Our approach should recognize this as a spiritual disease requiring counseling and rehabilitation. Unlike the modern Western narrative that frames this as genetic or innate, Islamic understanding sees it as a deviation that can be addressed through proper guidance and support.

translation: Indeed, you approach men with desire, instead of women. Rather, you are a transgressing people
commentary:
إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ ٱلرِّجَالَ شَهْوَةً مِّن دُونِ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُّسْرِفُونَ

"Verily, you approach men with desire instead of women. Rather, you are a people who transgress all bounds."

The word شَهْوَةً means desire or lust. This ayah clarifies that the issue isn't the existence of desire itself—Allah ﷻ created desire within human beings. The problem is when that desire becomes deviated from its natural, intended purpose. The phrase مِّن دُونِ ٱلنِّسَآءِ indicates "instead of women," highlighting that this represents a turning away from what is natural and wholesome.

The conclusion of the ayah describes them as مُّسْرِفُونَ—those who have gone beyond all limits. The word israf means excess, transgression beyond boundaries. They weren't simply sinning; they had completely abandoned the norm that Allah ﷻ Himself established.

This reality presents significant challenges in our contemporary context. The normalization of shamelessness through modern media has made these diseases more rampant in society. What was always present but hidden has now become accessible, promoted and defended through the internet and popular culture. These industries generate billions of dollars, creating powerful economic and political forces that reshape how society views right and wrong.

The psychological framing has also shifted dramatically. What was once recognized as abnormal became comedy, then acceptance, and finally anything short of full endorsement was rebranded as "homophobia"—itself a psychological disorder. By redefining phobia as an irrational fear, the narrative cleverly positioned religious objection as a mental illness rather than a moral stance.

Muslims face particular difficulties in this environment because our principles are non-negotiable. Politics operates on compromise, but we cannot compromise on fundamental matters of right and wrong. We can engage in civic matters—advocating for community improvements, opposing harmful businesses in our neighborhoods—but when it comes to core ethical issues, we must maintain our principles even when it puts us in awkward political situations.

The more pressing question for our generation is this: Does Islam itself offer a methodology for bringing about positive change in society? We often engage in activism assuming it will create change, but have we examined whether our actions align with the ethical framework and approach that our religion teaches? We need to explore how to pursue justice and reform without compromising our values.

This isn't just a theoretical problem. Homosexuality is a growing challenge within Muslim communities as well. Whatever problems affect the broader society inevitably reach Muslims too—whether alcohol, drugs or sexual deviance. Youth counselors report deeply troubling patterns emerging among young Muslims. This isn't something we can address by simply condemning it and quoting verses about Prophet Lut (AS). We need real solutions.

One root cause is exposure to pornography, which kills spiritual sensitivity and opens the door to all forms of sexual corruption. Young people need honest conversations with parents and access to proper counseling. Practical steps matter: keeping computers in public spaces like the kitchen rather than bedrooms, installing filters, staying busy with wholesome activities, avoiding isolation. These may seem simple, but they create necessary boundaries.

The uncomfortable truth is that this affects people across all ages and circumstances. Being married doesn't make someone immune. Having a beard doesn't provide protection. The spiritual war being waged through media and technology requires active defense, not passive assumptions that "it won't happen to us."

Parents must overcome the discomfort of discussing these topics with their children. Silence doesn't make problems disappear. Young people already know these issues exist—they need to know that their parents understand the reality they're facing. Opening these conversations with wisdom and compassion creates the support system that can prevent or address these struggles.

translation: But the answer of his people was only that they said, "Evict them from your city! Indeed, they are men who keep themselves pure
commentary:
وَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ أَخْرِجُوهُم مِّن قَرْيَتِكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ أُنَاسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ

And the answer of his people was only that they said: "Expel them from your town, for they are people who keep themselves pure!"

The response of Lut's (AS) people reveals a disturbing pattern: they had no substantive answer to his message. Their only reaction was to demand his expulsion, sarcastically mocking him and his followers as أُنَاسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ—"people who consider themselves so pure."

This mockery of religious people as self-righteous isn't new. The Arabic phrase captures what we would call self-righteousness today. Even in modern discourse, those who maintain religious moral standards are often dismissed as self-righteous, judgmental or puritanical. The people of Lut (AS) essentially said: "Oh, you're so pure and holy? Why don't you just leave us filthy people alone and get out of here?"

This defensive hostility when confronted with truth remains a common human reaction. Rather than examine their own behavior, they attacked the character of those calling them to righteousness.

translation: So We saved him and his family, except for his wife; she was of those who remained [with the evildoers]
commentary:
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَهُۥ كَانَتْ مِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ

So We saved him and his family, except his wife—she was of those who remained behind.

When destruction came, Allah ﷻ rescued Lut (AS) and his family with one painful exception: his own wife. The word ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ refers to those who lag behind when others are traveling forward. She should have been saved with the believers, but she remained behind with those who were destroyed.

This tragic detail reminds us that proximity to righteousness doesn't guarantee salvation. Being married to a prophet didn't save her because she didn't embrace his message. Faith is individual, and family connections cannot substitute for personal commitment to truth.

translation: And We rained upon them a rain [of stones]. Then see how was the end of the criminals
commentary:
وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّطَرًا ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ

And We rained down upon them a rain. Then see what was the end of the criminals.

The word أَمْطَرْنَا is used in the Quran specifically for rain that comes as punishment—not the beneficial rain that brings life, but a devastating rain of destruction. In this case, it refers to the rain of fire and stones that annihilated the people of Lut (AS).

Allah ﷻ commands us to look and reflect: فَٱنظُرْ—"then see!" This isn't casual observation but deep reflection on the outcome of those who persist in criminal behavior. The word ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ emphasizes that their actions were crimes against the natural order and divine law. Their end serves as a warning for all generations.

translation: And to [the people of] Madyan [We sent] their brother Shu'ayb. He said, "O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him. There has come to you clear evidence from your Lord. So fulfill the measure and weight and do not deprive people of their due and cause not corruption upon the earth after its reformation. That is better for you, if you should be believers
commentary:
وَإِلَىٰ مَدْيَنَ أَخَاهُمْ شُعَيْبًا ۗ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ قَدْ جَآءَتْكُم بَيِّنَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَأَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ وَلَا تَبْخَسُوا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ أَشْيَآءَهُمْ وَلَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَـٰحِهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

And to Madyan, their brother Shu'ayb. He said: "O my people! Worship Allah, you have no deity other than Him. Clear proof has indeed come to you from your Lord. So give full measure and weight, and do not deprive people of their things, and do not cause corruption in the land after it has been set right. That is better for you if you are believers."

The narrative shifts from the social evil of Lut's (AS) people to the economic corruption of Madyan. Just as the earlier passages addressed political evil (when the chiefs rejected their messengers), we now see the pattern: political corruption, social corruption and economic corruption.

Prophet Shu'ayb (AS) begins with the same fundamental message as every prophet: worship Allah ﷻ alone. The phrase مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ means "you have no entity worthy of worship or obedience whatsoever except Him." This establishes the foundation—true success only comes through submitting to Allah ﷻ.

Then comes the specific issue plaguing Madyan: فَأَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ—"fulfill the scale and the balance." They were cheating people in business, using dishonest weights and measures. The command وَلَا تَبْخَسُوا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ أَشْيَآءَهُمْ means "don't short-change people in the things you owe them." Don't give people less than what they deserve.

The warning وَلَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَـٰحِهَا—"don't cause corruption in the land after it's been set right"—reveals something critical about economic evil: it doesn't remain isolated. When someone takes bribes, it becomes an expectation. When someone price-gouges and profits unfairly, others follow suit. When one person leaves work early without consequence, others feel entitled to do the same. These unethical practices spread like disease through a society.

This pattern isn't limited to ancient Madyan. Contemporary examples abound: resume fraud where people add false experience to get jobs, consulting companies that overbill clients by keeping employees on-site unnecessarily, mechanics who charge for unnecessary repairs, banks with obscure fees that customers can never quite understand. The medical industry, auto industry, nearly every sector has these issues.

Even within Muslim communities, we see this corruption taking root. The mentality of "everyone does it" or "it's just how business works" creeps in. Someone might justify lying on a resume because "they'll take you more seriously that way." A contractor might overbill knowing the client won't notice. These are the same economic evils that destroyed Madyan.

The modern business world has even developed specialized jargon and complex terminology specifically designed to obscure unethical practices. "Inconvenience charges" and "service fees" appear on statements with no clear explanation. Warranty fine print includes exceptions that conveniently exclude the most common problems. This is sophisticated cheating, but it's still cheating.

Shu'ayb's (AS) message concludes with ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ—"that would be better for you if you are truly believers." Honest business practices aren't just ethical obligations; they're what's actually better for us. Short-term gains from cheating pale in comparison to the long-term destruction such practices bring to individuals and societies. If we truly believe in Allah ﷻ, in accountability and in the consequences of our actions, then we must conduct ourselves with complete integrity in all economic dealings.

translation: And do not sit on every path, threatening and averting from the way of Allah those who believe in Him, seeking to make it [seem] deviant. And remember when you were few and He increased you. And see how was the end of the corrupters
commentary:
وَلَا تَقْعُدُوا۟ بِكُلِّ صِرَٰطٍ تُوعِدُونَ وَتَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِهِۦ وَتَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ كُنتُمْ قَلِيلًا فَكَثَّرَكُمْ ۖ وَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ

This ayah addresses a particularly disturbing aspect of how the people of Madyan opposed Shuaib (AS). The command "do not sit on every path" refers to a practice of intimidation and obstruction. The Arabic word صِرَٰطٍ means path or way, and here it carries both literal and metaphorical meanings. These people would literally station themselves on streets and pathways to threaten and intimidate anyone who showed interest in the message Shuaib (AS) brought.

The word تُوعِدُونَ comes from وعد, which normally means promise, but specifically refers to threatening promises—warnings of harm. They would confront people with threats: "You better not come back here" or "This is our territory." This kind of behavior, where groups gather to intimidate passersby, is unfortunately timeless. Young people especially fall into this pattern, hanging around in groups, making threatening comments, starting conflicts, or simply making others feel unsafe walking through their area.

The ayah continues: وَتَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِهِۦ—"and you obstruct from the path of Allah ﷻ whoever believes in Him." They weren't just passively disbelieving; they actively worked to prevent others from accepting the truth. The verb تَصُدُّونَ implies creating barriers and obstacles. Anyone who showed interest in belief was met with intimidation, threats, and pressure to conform.

وَتَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا—"and you seek to make it crooked." The word عِوَجًا means crookedness or deviation. They weren't content with rejecting the straight path themselves; they wanted to corrupt it for others, to make the truth appear twisted and unclear. This is a profound form of spiritual crime—not just refusing guidance but actively working to confuse and mislead those seeking it.

Then comes a powerful reminder: وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ كُنتُمْ قَلِيلًا فَكَثَّرَكُمْ—"And remember when you were few and He multiplied you." The word قَلِيلًا can mean few in number, but also implies weakness in power and influence. Allah ﷻ elevated them from being a small, powerless group to becoming numerous and influential. This blessing should have instilled gratitude and humility, but instead it bred arrogance.

The ayah concludes with a stark warning: وَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ—"And see how was the end of the corrupters." Look at history, Allah ﷻ tells them. Examine what happened to nations before you who caused corruption and turned people away from truth. Their power didn't save them. Their numbers didn't protect them. This is both a reminder and a warning.

translation: And if there should be a group among you who has believed in that with which I have been sent and a group that has not believed, then be patient until Allah judges between us. And He is the best of judges
commentary:
وَإِن كَانَ طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنكُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱلَّذِىٓ أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِۦ وَطَآئِفَةٌ لَّمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ فَٱصْبِرُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَحْكُمَ ٱللَّهُ بَيْنَنَا ۚ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ

After warning his people about the consequences of obstruction and corruption, Shuaib (AS) acknowledges the division that has emerged within his community. "And if there is a group among you who has believed in that which I have been sent with, and a group who has not believed..." The word طَآئِفَةٌ refers to a significant portion or faction, not just a handful of individuals. The community has split into two distinct camps.

His response to this division is remarkable: فَٱصْبِرُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَحْكُمَ ٱللَّهُ بَيْنَنَا—"then be patient until Allah ﷻ judges between us." Rather than escalating the conflict or trying to force the issue, Shuaib (AS) calls for patience and defers judgment to Allah ﷻ. The verb يَحْكُمَ means to pass verdict, to render judgment. This isn't a call for passivity, but rather a recognition that ultimate justice belongs to Allah ﷻ alone.

He ends with a profound statement of faith: وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ—"And He is the best of judges." No human court, no tribal council, no political system can render the kind of perfect, comprehensive judgment that Allah ﷻ delivers. This phrase expresses complete trust in divine justice and serves as a counter to the threats his people had been making. They threatened him with their power and authority, but he appeals to a higher Authority altogether.

translation: Said the eminent ones who were arrogant among his people, "We will surely evict you, O Shu'ayb, and those who have believed with you from our city, or you must return to our religion." He said, "Even if we were unwilling
commentary:
۞ قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكَ يَـٰشُعَيْبُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَكَ مِن قَرْيَتِنَآ أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِى مِلَّتِنَا ۚ قَالَ أَوَلَوْ كُنَّا كَـٰرِهِينَ

The response from the elite is swift and harsh. قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟—"The chieftains who were arrogant said." The word ٱلْمَلَأُ (al-mala') refers to the elite, the upper echelon of society, those with power and influence. They are specifically described as ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟—those who showed arrogance, who considered themselves above accepting guidance.

It's significant that the Quran identifies Shuaib (AS) as مِن قَوْمِهِۦ—from his own people. He wasn't an outsider. He was one of them, from their tribe, someone they knew. In fact, Shuaib (AS) came from the elite class himself. They had high expectations for him—they thought he would grow up to take a leadership position, to have a successful political career. He was the kid with potential, with a bright future ahead of him in their system. When he chose to become a messenger instead, their disappointment turned to hostility.

Their ultimatum is clear: لَنُخْرِجَنَّكَ يَـٰشُعَيْبُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَكَ مِن قَرْيَتِنَآ—"We will surely expel you, O Shuaib, and those who have believed with you from our city." Notice the possessive "our city"—this is about power and control over territory. But then they offer an alternative: أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِى مِلَّتِنَا—"or you must return to our millah."

The word مِلَّةٍ is crucial to understand here. While it's often translated as religion, it carries a broader meaning. Millah refers to a nation unified by devotion—devotion to a religion, an ideology, a leader, or a way of life. It's not just about belief; it's about complete integration into a system, a culture, a collective identity. When they say لَتَعُودُنَّ—"you must return"—the verb عَادَ doesn't simply mean to go back. It means to return to where you started, to revert to your original state. 

What they're demanding is assimilation. "We're going to make you normal again. You've become weird. You used to be one of us. We're going to bring you back, reintegrate you into society, make it so we can't tell the difference between you and us."

This demand for assimilation is timeless. In modern society, religion is increasingly pushed into a sanitized, contained space. It's acceptable as long as it serves certain functions: keeping downtrodden communities hopeful, providing ethical employees for corporations, offering moral guidelines that support the economic system. Religion is welcomed when it makes you a better worker, a more compliant citizen. Companies appreciate employees who are "devoutly ethical" because it serves their bottom line.

But the moment religion starts asking deeper questions—ethical questions about business practices, moral questions about societal structures, challenging questions about justice and purpose—suddenly religion becomes problematic. The elite of modern society often view religious practice with a kind of anthropological curiosity, like studying exotic cultures in a documentary. "How fascinating that you pray five times a day. How interesting, your Quranic recitation—I have a recording of it next to my collection of artifacts from various indigenous peoples." It's a form of superiority disguised as appreciation.

This is exactly what the elite of Madyan wanted: a sanitized, compartmentalized version of Shuaib (AS) that didn't challenge their system. They wanted him to keep his beliefs private, to not let them interfere with the economic and social order they had established.

Shuaib's response is immediate and powerful: قَالَ أَوَلَوْ كُنَّا كَـٰرِهِينَ—"He said, 'Even if we were unwilling?'" The word كَـٰرِهِينَ means to be forced into something painful, to be compelled to do something that hurts you deeply. His question exposes the absurdity of their demand: Are you really going to force us back into your way of life even though it would be completely against our will, even though it would cause us immense pain?

translation: We would have invented against Allah a lie if we returned to your religion after Allah had saved us from it. And it is not for us to return to it except that Allah, our Lord, should will. Our Lord has encompassed all things in knowledge. Upon Allah we have relied. Our Lord, decide between us and our people in truth, and You are the best of those who give decision
commentary:
قَدِ ٱفْتَرَيْنَا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا إِنْ عُدْنَا فِى مِلَّتِكُم بَعْدَ إِذْ نَجَّىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ مِنْهَا ۚ وَمَا يَكُونُ لَنَآ أَن نَّعُودَ فِيهَآ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّنَا ۚ وَسِعَ رَبُّنَا كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا ۚ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا ٱفْتَحْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْفَـٰتِحِينَ

Shuaib (AS) continues his response with a statement that reveals the gravity of what they're demanding. قَدِ ٱفْتَرَيْنَا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا—"We would have invented a lie against Allah ﷻ." The word ٱفْتَرَيْنَا means to fabricate or manufacture a lie, to make up something false deliberately. إِنْ عُدْنَا فِى مِلَّتِكُم بَعْدَ إِذْ نَجَّىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ مِنْهَا—"if we returned to your millah after Allah ﷻ has saved us from it."

The word نَجَّىٰنَا is powerful—it means to thoroughly rescue, to save from danger. Shuaib (AS) isn't just saying they left their old way of life; he's saying Allah ﷻ rescued them from it, implying that returning would be like walking back into a burning building after being pulled to safety. To go back after being saved would be to lie about the entire experience, to deny that Allah ﷻ ever rescued them in the first place.

Then he adds a profound theological point: وَمَا يَكُونُ لَنَآ أَن نَّعُودَ فِيهَآ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّنَا—"And it is not for us to return to it unless Allah ﷻ, our Lord, should will." This isn't expressing doubt or openness to actually returning. Rather, it's a recognition of Allah's absolute sovereignty. He's saying, "The only way we would ever return is if Allah ﷻ Himself willed it—but He hasn't, so we won't." It's a statement of complete dependence on Allah ﷻ while simultaneously expressing certainty about the path they're on.

وَسِعَ رَبُّنَا كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا—"Our Lord encompasses everything in knowledge." The verb وَسِعَ means to encompass, to contain completely. Allah's knowledge isn't limited or partial—it encompasses absolutely everything. This is significant because the elite of Madyan were making calculations based on limited knowledge, earthly power, social dynamics. Shuaib (AS) appeals to One whose knowledge transcends all of that.

عَلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا—"Upon Allah ﷻ we have placed our trust." The word تَوَكَّلْنَا expresses complete reliance and trust. In the face of their threats, expulsion, and demands for assimilation, Shuaib (AS) and the believers with him anchor themselves in trust in Allah ﷻ.

The ayah concludes with a du'a: رَبَّنَا ٱفْتَحْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ—"Our Lord, judge between us and our people in truth." The verb ٱفْتَحْ literally means to open, but in this context it means to make a clear separation, to render a decisive judgment. The word بِٱلْحَقِّ is crucial—he asks for judgment based on truth, on what is real and right, not on power dynamics or social pressure.

وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْفَـٰتِحِينَ—"And You are the best of those who judge." This mirrors his earlier statement that Allah ﷻ is the best of judges, but uses a different word. ٱلْفَـٰتِحِينَ comes from the root meaning to open—Allah ﷻ opens up situations, creates decisive moments, brings matters to resolution. It's a recognition that only Allah ﷻ can truly open up and resolve the conflict they're facing.

translation: Said the eminent ones who disbelieved among his people, "If you should follow Shu'ayb, indeed, you would then be losers
commentary:
وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ لَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتُمْ شُعَيْبًا إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ

The elite respond to Shuaib's powerful statement with a simple smear campaign. وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ—"And the chieftains who disbelieved from among his people said." Notice that earlier they were described as ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ (those who showed arrogance), and now they're called ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ (those who disbelieved). The labels build on each other—arrogance leads to disbelief, and disbelief manifests as arrogance.

Their message is directed not at Shuaib (AS), but at potential followers: لَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتُمْ شُعَيْبًا إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ—"If you follow Shuaib, indeed you will be losers." This is psychological warfare. They're trying to create fear of loss in people's hearts. The word خَـٰسِرُونَ means losers, those who have lost out, who have made a terrible deal and come out on the losing end.

The irony is devastating. They're warning people about loss, but they themselves are about to experience the ultimate loss. This is a pattern throughout the Quran—those who warn others about following the truth are themselves heading toward destruction, but they can't see it.

translation: So the earthquake seized them, and they became within their home [corpses] fallen prone
commentary:
فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دَارِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ

The response from Allah ﷻ is swift and decisive. فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ—"So the earthquake seized them." The word ٱلرَّجْفَةُ refers to a violent shaking, a powerful earthquake. The verb أَخَذَتْهُمُ means it grabbed them, seized them—there was no escape, no warning, no time to prepare.

فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دَارِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ—"And they became within their homes corpses fallen prone." The word أَصْبَحُوا۟ means they became by morning—this all happened so quickly that by the next morning, it was over. جَـٰثِمِينَ means fallen over, prostrate, lifeless. These people who had been so full of threats and power were now just bodies lying in their homes.

The phrase فِى دَارِهِمْ—in their homes—is particularly striking. They weren't out in battle. They weren't even in the streets causing their usual trouble. They were in their homes, in the places where they felt most safe and secure, and that's where the punishment found them.

translation: Those who denied Shu'ayb - it was as though they had never resided there. Those who denied Shu'ayb - it was they who were the losers
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ شُعَيْبًا كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ فِيهَا ۚ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ شُعَيْبًا كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ

This ayah adds a haunting reflection on their destruction. ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ شُعَيْبًا—"Those who denied Shuaib." The word كَذَّبُوا۟ means they rejected him as a liar, they called him false.

كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ فِيهَا—"As if they had never prospered therein." The word يَغْنَوْا۟  is fascinating. It comes from غِنَى, which means wealth so abundant you don't need anything from anyone, complete self-sufficiency, the ultimate luxury. When someone lives in extreme luxury and abundance, they are described with this word.

Allah ﷻ is saying that after the destruction, if you looked at their homes and their town, you would never imagine that these people had lived in such luxury. The word "luxury" wouldn't even come to mind. These were the celebrity homes of their day, the mansions that would have been featured in tours of the rich and famous. After Allah's punishment, you'd walk by and never know that opulence had existed there.

Then the ayah repeats with a devastating conclusion: ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ شُعَيْبًا كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ—"Those who denied Shuaib—it was they who were the losers." Remember what they said just two ayahs ago? "If you follow Shuaib, you will be losers." Allah ﷻ corrects the record: No, they were the losers. The very word they used to threaten others becomes their own description.

translation: And he turned away from them and said, "O my people, I had certainly conveyed to you the messages of my Lord and advised you, so how could I grieve for a disbelieving people
commentary:
فَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ وَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى وَنَصَحْتُ لَكُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ ءَاسَىٰ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ كَـٰفِرِينَ

After the destruction, Shuaib (AS) turns away from what remains. فَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ—"So he turned away from them." The verb تَوَلَّىٰ means to turn away, to leave behind. This is both physical and emotional—he's turning away from the scene of their destruction and from any remaining attachment to them.

وَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ—"And he said, 'O my people.'" Even now, after everything, he still calls them "my people." There's a profound sadness in this address.

لَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى—"I have conveyed to you the messages of my Lord." The verb أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ means to deliver thoroughly, to convey completely. He's saying, "I fulfilled my duty. I didn't hold anything back. I gave you the complete message, not just part of it."

وَنَصَحْتُ لَكُمْ—"And I advised you sincerely." The word نَصَحْتُ means to give sincere advice, to counsel someone with their best interests at heart. He wasn't manipulating them or trying to gain something from them. His advice was pure and sincere.

فَكَيْفَ ءَاسَىٰ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ كَـٰفِرِينَ—"So how could I grieve for a disbelieving people?" The word ءَاسَىٰ is one of several words for sadness in Arabic, but it has a specific nuance. It refers to feeling sad and angry, particularly when you're sad and angry at someone more powerful than you, or at a situation you can't control. It's a helpless kind of grief mixed with frustration.

Shuaib (AS) is questioning himself: Why should I even feel this overwhelming sadness? The words themselves are words of sadness—"I conveyed the message to you, why couldn't you just listen?"—but he's also trying to process the emotion. How can I grieve for people who consciously chose disbelief despite being given every chance?

Notice something significant: he began the story calling them يَـٰقَوْمِ—"O my people"—with the possessive. But at the end, he says عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ كَـٰفِرِينَ—"for a disbelieving people," without the possessive. It's as though he can't bear to claim them as his own anymore. They're just "a disbelieving people," not "my people." The emotional distance is palpable.

translation: And We sent to no city a prophet [who was denied] except that We seized its people with poverty and hardship that they might humble themselves [to Allah]
commentary:
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا فِى قَرْيَةٍ مِّن نَّبِىٍّ إِلَّآ أَخَذْنَآ أَهْلَهَا بِٱلْبَأْسَآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَضَّرَّعُونَ

After concluding the story of Shuaib (AS), Allah ﷻ reveals a pattern that applies to all communities that received messengers. وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا فِى قَرْيَةٍ مِّن نَّبِىٍّ—"And We sent into no city any prophet"—the use of مِن here emphasizes the totality: not a single prophet was sent to any town without this pattern following.

إِلَّآ أَخَذْنَآ أَهْلَهَا بِٱلْبَأْسَآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ—"except that We seized its people with hardship and adversity." The word ٱلْبَأْسَآءِ refers to severe difficulty, often related to war, conflict, and external hardships. ٱلضَّرَّآءِ refers to harm, injury, and personal difficulty. Together, they encompass all forms of calamity.

This reveals something crucial about Allah's methodology: Before destroying a disbelieving nation, He would send them hardships. This wasn't arbitrary punishment—it was an opportunity for reflection and return.

لَعَلَّهُمْ يَضَّرَّعُونَ—"so that they might humble themselves." The word يَضَّرَّعُونَ means to humble oneself in supplication, to get on your knees and beg Allah ﷻ with tears and desperation. The hardships were designed to break their pride, to make them realize their need for Allah ﷻ, to bring them back to genuine humility and prayer.

This is profound divine wisdom. When blessings don't bring people to gratitude and recognition of Allah ﷻ, sometimes difficulty is the only thing that can break through their heedlessness. The nation of Fir'awn (Pharaoh) is a case study of this—Allah ﷻ sent them nine different signs, nine different calamities, hoping they would turn back. They didn't.

translation: Then We exchanged in place of the bad [condition], good, until they increased [and prospered] and said, "Our fathers [also] were touched with hardship and ease." So We seized them suddenly while they did not perceive
commentary:
ثُمَّ بَدَّلْنَا مَكَانَ ٱلسَّيِّئَةِ ٱلْحَسَنَةَ حَتَّىٰ عَفَوا۟ وَّقَالُوا۟ قَدْ مَسَّ ءَابَآءَنَا ٱلضَّرَّآءُ وَٱلسَّرَّآءُ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ

This ayah reveals the second stage of Allah's test for these nations. ثُمَّ بَدَّلْنَا مَكَانَ ٱلسَّيِّئَةِ ٱلْحَسَنَةَ—"Then We replaced the bad with good." After the hardship came ease. The economy rebounded. The weather improved. Health returned. Everything that had been difficult became easy.

حَتَّىٰ عَفَوا۟—"until they increased." The verb عَفَوا۟ means to increase, to go above and beyond. This is the devastating part: when times were hard, they didn't turn to Allah ﷻ in humility. When times became easy, they didn't turn to Allah ﷻ in gratitude. Instead, they took the blessing as an opportunity to increase in sin. They had been sinful before, but now with wealth, health, and opportunity, they went even further in their wrongdoing. They dug their hole deeper.

The difficult times were meant to bring them closer to Allah ﷻ. When that didn't work, the easy times became Allah's way of saying, "Fine. Have it your way." This is one of the most frightening forms of divine response—when Allah ﷻ stops sending difficulties and instead gives people free reign to pursue their desires. It's not a blessing; it's a warning sign.

وَّقَالُوا۟ قَدْ مَسَّ ءَابَآءَنَا ٱلضَّرَّآءُ وَٱلسَّرَّآءُ—"And they said, 'Our fathers were touched by hardship and ease.'" Their response to all of this was to normalize it, to treat it as just the natural cycle of life. "Oh, this is just how things go. Sometimes it's hard, sometimes it's easy. That's life. That's what happened to our ancestors too." They completely missed the spiritual significance. They couldn't see that these weren't just random events—they were messages from Allah ﷻ.

فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ—"So We seized them suddenly while they perceived not." The word بَغْتَةً means unexpectedly, without warning, out of nowhere. Life is good, everything seems fine, and then—suddenly—it's over. They won't even realize what's happening to them. 

This connects back to what was mentioned earlier in Surah Al-A'raf about punishment coming at night while they sleep or during the day while they're playing. The punishment comes when life seems most normal, most safe, most under control.

translation: And if only the people of the cities had believed and feared Allah, We would have opened upon them blessings from the heaven and the earth; but they denied [the messengers], so We seized them for what they were earning
commentary:
وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ لَفَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَرَكَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَـٰكِن كَذَّبُوا۟ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ

This ayah presents the alternative path, what could have been. وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟—"And if only the people of those cities had believed and feared Allah." The phrase لَوْ أَنَّ expresses a hypothetical, a road not taken. If they had just taken these two steps: belief and consciousness of Allah ﷻ.

لَفَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَرَكَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ—"We would have opened upon them blessings from the heaven and the earth." The verb لَفَتَحْنَا means We would have opened—not just given, but opened, implying doors upon doors of opportunity and goodness. The word بَرَكَـٰتٍ is particularly powerful. Barakah isn't just blessing—it's blessing that multiplies, that keeps growing exponentially. It's goodness that compounds.

مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ—"from the sky and the earth." Rain from above. Vegetation and resources from below. This is comprehensive blessing—provision from every direction, abundance in every form.

وَلَـٰكِن كَذَّبُوا۟—"But they denied." Just one word in Arabic, but it carries the weight of their entire choice. Despite the opportunities, despite the warnings, despite the messengers, they denied.

فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ—"So We seized them for what they used to earn." The verb يَكْسِبُونَ means to earn, to acquire. This wasn't arbitrary punishment—it was the natural consequence of what they had earned through their own choices and actions. They worked for this outcome, earned it through their persistent rejection and sin.

translation: Then, did the people of the cities feel secure from Our punishment coming to them at night while they were asleep
commentary:
أَفَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتِيَهُم بَأْسُنَا بَيَـٰتًا وَهُمْ نَآئِمُونَ

Now comes a series of rhetorical questions that shift from past nations to present reality, including the people of Makkah listening to these verses. أَفَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ—"Then did the people of the cities feel secure?" The interrogative أَفَ carries a tone of incredulity: How could they possibly feel safe?

أَن يَأْتِيَهُم بَأْسُنَا بَيَـٰتًا—"that Our punishment would come to them at night?" The word بَيَـٰتًا means during the night hours, in the darkness when people are most vulnerable.

وَهُمْ نَآئِمُونَ—"while they were sleeping?" Picture the scene: a quiet night, families asleep in their homes, everything peaceful and calm. They feel completely safe, completely secure. And that's when the punishment arrives—without warning, without the chance to prepare or escape.

This isn't just about physical sleep. It's about spiritual sleep, about being unaware, unconscious of the reality of one's situation. People going about their lives, thinking everything is fine, not realizing they're heading toward disaster.

translation: Or did the people of the cities feel secure from Our punishment coming to them in the morning while they were at play
commentary:
أَوَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتِيَهُم بَأْسُنَا ضُحًى وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ

The second rhetorical question mirrors the first but shifts the time of day. أَوَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ—"Or did the people of the cities feel secure?"

أَن يَأْتِيَهُم بَأْسُنَا ضُحًى—"that Our punishment would come to them in the morning?" The word ضُحًى refers to the bright morning hours, the time of day associated with clarity, visibility, and activity.

وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ—"while they were playing?" Picture a beautiful morning, kids playing in the park, families enjoying a weekend, everyone relaxed and having fun. The sun is shining, life is good. And that's when it hits—completely unexpected, in the middle of normalcy and joy.

The verb يَلْعَبُونَ means playing, being occupied with leisure and entertainment. There's nothing seemingly wrong with the scene—people are just living their lives, enjoying themselves. But that sense of security, that feeling that everything is fine and always will be, is exactly what makes them vulnerable.

These two ayahs together—nighttime while sleeping and daytime while playing—cover the full spectrum of human vulnerability. Whether we're at rest or at play, whether it's dark or light, whether we feel vulnerable or secure, Allah's plan can unfold at any moment.

translation: Then did they feel secure from the plan of Allah? But no one feels secure from the plan of Allah except the losing people
commentary:
أَفَأَمِنُوا۟ مَكْرَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَلَا يَأْمَنُ مَكْرَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ

This final ayah brings together all the themes and delivers a sobering conclusion. أَفَأَمِنُوا۟ مَكْرَ ٱللَّهِ—"Then did they feel secure from the plan of Allah?" 

The word مَكْرَ is often translated as "plan" or "scheme," but it requires careful understanding. When used about humans, makr typically means deception or plotting. But when used about Allah ﷻ, it means His perfect, comprehensive plan that unfolds in ways human beings cannot anticipate or prevent. It's not deception—Allah ﷻ is never unjust or deceptive. Rather, it refers to the reality that Allah's plan operates on a level beyond human calculation and control.

The people described in these ayahs felt secure. They looked at their wealth, their power, their circumstances, and concluded they were safe. They calculated based on what they could see and measure. But they failed to account for the One whose plan encompasses everything, who can change circumstances in an instant, whose timing and methods are beyond human prediction.

فَلَا يَأْمَنُ مَكْرَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ—"But none feels secure from the plan of Allah except the losing people." This is one of the most powerful statements in this entire passage. The only people who feel completely safe, who think they have everything figured out and under control, who believe nothing can touch them—these are ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ, the ultimate losers.

True wisdom, true faith, involves a balance: trusting in Allah's mercy while maintaining consciousness of His power, working in this world while recognizing its temporary nature, enjoying blessings while remembering their Source, feeling peace through faith while maintaining awareness of accountability.

Those who feel entirely secure from Allah's plan, who live as though they're untouchable, who make no space in their lives for consciousness of the divine—they're not wise or sophisticated or enlightened. They're simply losers who don't yet realize they've lost. And that realization, when it comes, will come suddenly—بَغْتَةً—while they don't even perceive it.

translation: Has it not become clear to those who inherited the earth after its [previous] people that if We willed, We could afflict them for their sins? But We seal over their hearts so they do not hear
commentary:
أَوَلَمْ يَهْدِ لِلَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ ٱلْأَرْضَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَهْلِهَآ أَن لَّوْ نَشَآءُ أَصَبْنَـٰهُم بِذُنُوبِهِمْ ۚ وَنَطْبَعُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ

Before examining this ayah, it's essential to understand how the stories in the Quran function within their respective surahs. Each narrative isn't simply told for its own sake. Rather, each story is strategically placed to advance the central argument of that surah. Only the portions relevant to that argument are mentioned. For instance, in Surah Al-An'am, when the story of Prophet Ibrahim (AS) destroying the idols is recounted, it serves a specific purpose within the surah's conversation about the irrationality of shirk. The point isn't merely to recount historical events, but to further the surah's theological discourse.

This principle is crucial when approaching Surah Al-A'raf. The most fundamental theme running throughout this surah is the conflict between remembrance and heedlessness—between ذِكْر and غَفْلَة. These concepts represent opposing states of spiritual awareness. Ghaflah describes someone completely oblivious to reality, like a person who sees a fire but remains absorbed in their activities, failing to recognize the danger. The heart's capacity for awareness or heedlessness becomes a central concern throughout this surah, which is why the heart will be mentioned repeatedly.

Ghaflah represents a disease from which recovery is possible. When a person remembers and awakens to reality, they can return from this state through dhikr. However, there exists another spiritual disease that offers no return: arrogance (كِبْر). This was Iblis's problem—not forgetfulness, but pride. In contrast, Adam (AS) experienced a momentary lapse, a slip into ghaflah, but he could return through repentance. Throughout the stories in Surah Al-A'raf, you'll observe this pattern: narratives either highlight ghaflah (the recoverable disease) or kibr (the fatal one). The surah's deeper warning is that if someone remains in ghaflah long enough, they inevitably progress to kibr, reaching a point of no return.

With this framework in mind, ayah 100 addresses those who inherited the earth after its previous inhabitants: "Wasn't it enough to guide them?" The Arabs of Makkah had inherited their land long after many nations had been destroyed in that very region. Extensive history had unfolded around them—civilizations had risen and fallen. The ayah asks: wasn't the evidence of these destroyed nations sufficient guidance?

Allah ﷻ states: لَّوْ نَشَآءُ أَصَبْنَـٰهُم بِذُنُوبِهِمْ—"Had We wanted, We would have targeted them because of their sins." The Quraysh are being told: you walk among ruins, you see the remnants of nations destroyed for their transgressions, yet you've become completely heedless. You don't consider that what befell them could befall you. Those ruins should serve as mirrors reflecting your own potential fate, but you see them merely as interesting historical artifacts, disconnected from your present reality.

This warning extends beyond the original audience. While the Quran was addressing the people of Makkah—not yet Muslims—the principle applies universally. Muslims today can fall into the same trap of ghaflah. We study the destroyed nations as academic history, examining their ruins, perhaps even visiting archaeological sites, without internalizing the lesson: this could happen to us. We feel completely safe, assuming that what happened to previous peoples could never touch us. This false sense of security is precisely what this ayah challenges.

The ayah concludes: وَنَطْبَعُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ—"And We place a seal upon their hearts, so they do not hear." The refusal to listen represents a manifestation of arrogance. This sealing only occurs after prolonged heedlessness transforms into stubborn pride. First comes ghaflah—simple obliviousness. But when people persist in that state, refusing to awaken despite clear evidence, Allah ﷻ eventually seals their hearts. At that point, they won't even listen. Listening requires humility, an acknowledgment that you might need to change. The sealed heart has progressed beyond recovery, from ghaflah to kibr.

translation: Those cities - We relate to you, [O Muhammad], some of their news. And certainly did their messengers come to them with clear proofs, but they were not to believe in that which they had denied before. Thus does Allah seal over the hearts of the disbelievers
commentary:
تِلْكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآئِهَا ۚ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَمَا كَانُوا۟ لِيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِمَا كَذَّبُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَطْبَعُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

"Those are the towns—We narrate to you from their events." The word مِنْ أَنۢبَآئِهَا is particularly meaningful here. Allah ﷻ didn't provide exhaustive details about each town—their exact geography, population statistics, precise timelines, or comprehensive historical records. Instead, He gave us مِنْ—"from" their events, meaning a portion, a selection. We receive only what we need to extract the essential lesson: this could happen to us too.

This selective narration serves a specific purpose. The Quran isn't a history textbook aiming for encyclopedic coverage. It's guidance, and the details provided are precisely calibrated to awaken hearts. More information wouldn't necessarily produce more guidance. Sometimes, too much detail distracts from the central point. Allah ﷻ gives us just enough to recognize the pattern, understand the consequences of rebellion, and see ourselves in the mirror of these past nations.

"And certainly their messengers came to them with clear proofs." Despite receiving unambiguous evidence—بَيِّنَات, signs that left no room for reasonable doubt—they refused to believe. But why? The ayah provides a profound explanation: فَمَا كَانُوا۟ لِيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِمَا كَذَّبُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ—"They were not going to believe on account of what they had already denied from before."

This phrase requires careful attention. These people weren't rejecting truth for the first time when messengers arrived. They had already been engaged in denying truth much earlier. There was a prior conversation, a previous commitment to truth that they had already violated. When the messengers came, they were simply continuing a pattern of denial that began long before. This "from before" (مِن قَبْلُ) will be clarified later in the surah, particularly in ayah 172, which describes the primordial covenant when all human souls acknowledged Allah ﷻ as their Lord. That original testimony, that fitrah, represents the truth they had already begun suppressing before any messenger even arrived.

كَذَٰلِكَ يَطْبَعُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ—"Thus does Allah place a seal upon the hearts of the disbelievers." The word كَافِرِين literally means "those who cover" or "those who bury." They have the seed of truth within them—the fitrah, the natural recognition of Allah ﷻ—but they actively bury it. They don't allow it to flourish. They keep it suppressed deep within, refusing to let it grow. The seal comes as a consequence of this persistent burial. When someone continuously covers the truth within them, eventually that covering becomes permanent.

translation: And We did not find for most of them any covenant; but indeed, We found most of them defiantly disobedient
commentary:
وَمَا وَجَدْنَا لِأَكْثَرِهِم مِّنْ عَهْدٍ ۖ وَإِن وَجَدْنَآ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَفَـٰسِقِينَ

"And We did not find for most of them any commitment to covenant." The word عَهْد encompasses promises, commitments, and covenants—the sacred responsibilities that define our relationship with Allah ﷻ and with one another. These people lacked the capacity to honor their commitments. They couldn't be trusted with promises.

وَإِن وَجَدْنَآ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَفَـٰسِقِينَ—"And indeed, We found most of them to be truly corrupt." The word وَإِن here carries the meaning of "instead" or "on the contrary." Rather than finding faithfulness, Allah ﷻ found the opposite: deep-rooted corruption. The term فَاسِقِين describes those who rebel against boundaries, who transgress limits.

The connection between these two statements is instructive. Corruption essentially means having a responsibility and violating it. Whether in government, business, personal relationships, or spiritual matters, corruption occurs when someone toys with the rules, manipulates their obligations, or betrays the trust placed in them. First, Allah ﷻ mentions their failure to uphold covenants. Then He describes them as fasiqeen—those who actively violate the very boundaries their covenants established.

This ayah also points forward to ayah 172, where the primordial covenant will be discussed. That covenant represents the fundamental promise every human soul made to Allah ﷻ. The corruption described here isn't merely social or political—it's spiritual. These people violated the most sacred covenant of all, the one embedded in their very creation.

translation: Then We sent after them Moses with Our signs to Pharaoh and his establishment, but they were unjust toward them. So see how was the end of the corrupters
commentary:
ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِم مُّوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ فَظَلَمُوا۟ بِهَا ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ

"Then after them We raised Musa with Our signs to Fir'awn and his chiefs, but they wronged with them. So observe how was the end of the corrupters."

With this ayah, the narrative shifts to the story of Musa (AS) and Fir'awn, which will become the longest story in Surah Al-A'raf. While previous stories were mentioned briefly, this account will receive extended treatment because it perfectly illustrates the surah's central themes.

First, a historical note: "Fir'awn" (Pharaoh) is a title, not a personal name. It's equivalent to "Caesar" or "King"—a designation for Egyptian rulers of a particular dynasty. According to some historical analyses, when Musa (AS) fled Egypt after the incident where he struck a man who died, the ruling Pharaoh was actually like a father figure to him. By the time Musa (AS) returned with his prophetic mission, that Pharaoh had retired or passed away, and his son had assumed power. This son had grown up alongside Musa (AS) in the palace, viewing him almost as an older brother. This family dynamic adds layers of complexity to their confrontation—it wasn't merely a prophet facing a tyrant, but relationships and history creating an even more poignant rejection of truth.

The story of Musa (AS) will engage with two primary audiences: Fir'awn and his court on one side, and Bani Isra'il (the Children of Israel) on the other. These two groups embody the surah's central spiritual diseases. Fir'awn's fundamental problem is kibr—arrogance. He represents the sealed heart that has progressed beyond recovery. Bani Isra'il's fundamental problem is ghaflah—heedlessness. They had the opportunity to learn, to remember, to return, yet they kept repeating the same mistakes despite witnessing miracle after miracle.

This pattern mirrors the original human story in the surah. Just as Iblis embodied kibr and Adam (AS) experienced a slip he could recover from, now Fir'awn plays the role of Iblis—the one who cannot return—while Bani Isra'il plays the role of Adam (AS)—those who have the opportunity to repent but struggle with consistent remembrance. The narrative creates a case study showing how these spiritual diseases manifest in actual human communities and the devastating consequences when people fail to overcome them.

Some context about Bani Isra'il's presence in Egypt helps illuminate their situation. When Yusuf (AS) was given a position of power in Egypt—as treasurer or chief financial minister—the ruling dynasty was different from the Pharaohs who would later enslave Bani Isra'il. Under that earlier administration, Yusuf (AS), though a foreigner, rose to prominence and eventually sponsored his family to join him in Egypt. His brothers and father Ya'qub (AS) settled there, and over time, Bani Isra'il established themselves as a significant community.

However, a revolution occurred. A new dynasty of native Egyptians overthrew the foreign rulers and established the line of Pharaohs. These new rulers harbored deep resentment toward foreigners, especially those who had held positions of influence under the previous government. Bani Isra'il, having been closely associated with Yusuf (AS) and the former administration, became particular targets of persecution. The new regime needed to demonstrate that times had changed, that the old elite had been displaced.

Beyond political reasons, there were economic motivations. Bani Isra'il provided cheap labor for massive construction projects. The Quran will later reference how Pharaoh's people "built lofty buildings"—شَيَّدَ means both to construct tall structures and to perform delicate agricultural work. Bani Isra'il became the enslaved workforce building Egypt's monuments and working its farms. This systematic oppression formed the backdrop against which Musa (AS) would deliver his message.

The ayah states: فَظَلَمُوا۟ بِهَا—"they wronged with them," meaning they committed dhulm (oppression/wrongdoing) regarding the signs. When Musa (AS) presented clear miracles, Fir'awn and his chiefs didn't simply reject them politely. They actively oppressed through their rejection, wronging both the signs themselves and those who believed in them.

فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ—"So observe how was the end of the mufsideen." The command انظُرْ—"look" or "observe"—is directed to the Makkan Arabs. They would have been familiar with Egypt, perhaps having heard of or even seen the ruins of Pharaonic civilization. Allah ﷻ tells them to take a good, hard look at what became of those mighty builders. Fir'awn's dynasty created monuments that still stand today—they were probably in even better condition 1,400 years ago at the time of revelation. Those ruins testified to immense power and wealth. Yet Allah ﷻ says: look at their end. All that power couldn't save them. All that construction couldn't prevent their destruction.

The term مُفْسِدِين—"those who cause corruption"—connects back to ayah 102. These weren't merely people who rejected a message. They were actively corrupting society, violating covenants, spreading dhulm. Their end serves as a warning: no matter how powerful a civilization appears, when it's built on oppression and denial of truth, its foundations are rotten. Eventually, it will collapse, leaving only ruins as testimony to the consequences of mufsideen.

translation: And Moses said, "O Pharaoh, I am a messenger from the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ يَـٰفِرْعَوْنُ إِنِّى رَسُولٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Musa (AS) declares with absolute confidence: "O Firaun, I am a messenger from the Master of all nations and all peoples." When we read this ayah, we should recognize that though this recounts the story of Musa (AS), the conversation mirrors that of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ with the Quraysh and the people of Makkah. The parallels are intentional, inviting us to see the universal patterns in how prophets confront tyranny.

translation: [Who is] obligated not to say about Allah except the truth. I have come to you with clear evidence from your Lord, so send with me the Children of Israel
commentary:
حَقِيقٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَن لَّآ أَقُولَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِبَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ فَأَرْسِلْ مَعِىَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ

The word حَقِيقٌ comes from the root meaning "deserving" or "one who has earned the right." Musa (AS) essentially says, "I am rightful. I am the one who deserves to be considered someone who shouldn't be saying anything against Allah ﷻ except the truth." He appeals to his credibility: "You know me. I've lived with you. You know my character. There's no way you can just turn around and call me a liar."

He continues: "I've come to you with the clearest proof, with absolutely clear and undeniable evidence from your Master." Then comes his central demand: فَأَرْسِلْ مَعِىَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ - "Send the Sons of Israel along with me."

Consider the magnitude of this request. The Israelites represented free labor for Firaun's empire. In modern terms, imagine companies benefiting from workers they don't have to pay, provide healthcare for, or offer any compensation. The profit margins would be astronomical. Throughout history and even today, the most profitable enterprises are often those built on exploitative labor practices, where workers are paid barely enough to survive. Musa (AS) is asking Firaun to give up this entire economic advantage, to relinquish this source of wealth and power.

translation: [Pharaoh] said, "If you have come with a sign, then bring it forth, if you should be of the truthful
commentary:
قَالَ إِن كُنتَ جِئْتَ بِـَٔايَةٍ فَأْتِ بِهَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ

Firaun's response drips with dismissive skepticism: إِن كُنتَ جِئْتَ بِـَٔايَةٍ - "If you at all came with any kind of miraculous sign..." The conditional structure itself expresses doubt. It's as if he's saying, "I'm pretty sure you're lying, but if - and that's a big if - you actually came with something, then bring it, if in fact you're telling the truth."

The conversation in Surah Al-A'raf moves much faster than the more detailed account found in Surah Ash-Shu'ara. Here, the exchange is brief and direct, capturing the essential confrontation without elaboration.

translation: So Moses threw his staff, and suddenly it was a serpent, manifest
commentary:
فَأَلْقَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِىَ ثُعْبَانٌ مُّبِينٌ

Musa (AS) threw his staff. The word فَإِذَا (fa-idha) means "then immediately, out of nowhere" - emphasizing the instantaneous nature of the miracle. It became a ثُعْبَانٌ مُّبِينٌ (thu'ban mubin), a large serpent that made everything absolutely clear.

The Quran uses three different words for snakes when describing this miracle across various surahs. The word حَيَّة emphasizes the fangs, particularly when they're showing and the snake looks ready to strike. The word ثُعْبَان highlights its massive size - this was no ordinary snake but a python of enormous proportions. Each word choice illuminates a different aspect of what the witnesses experienced. Here, مُّبِينٌ tells us it clarified the issue completely. This wasn't something that could be explained away or dismissed. The sheer reality of what they were witnessing made it absolutely obvious that Musa (AS) wasn't fabricating anything.
