translation: And he drew out his hand; thereupon it was white [with radiance] for the observers
commentary:
وَنَزَعَ يَدَهُۥ فَإِذَا هِىَ بَيْضَآءُ لِلنَّـٰظِرِينَ

He drew out his hand, and again we see فَإِذَا - immediately it turned white بَيْضَآءُ لِلنَّـٰظِرِينَ - for all to see, for the people to stare at. They couldn't take their eyes off it. The linguistic choice here indicates they were transfixed, unable to look away from this radiating whiteness.

translation: Said the eminent among the people of Pharaoh, "Indeed, this is a learned magician
commentary:
قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَسَـٰحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ

The chieftains, the elite who filled Firaun's court, immediately declared: "No doubt about it, this is really a knowledgeable magician." This response should remind us of what was said about the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ: سَاحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ - "a knowledgeable magician." The parallels between these stories of rejection are striking and deliberate.

translation: Who wants to expel you from your land [through magic], so what do you instruct
commentary:
يُرِيدُ أَن يُخْرِجَكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِكُمْ ۖ فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ

"He intends to expel you from your land. So what do you people say?" This verse reveals a strategic pivot. The elites aren't just dismissing the miracle - they're rallying nationalist sentiment. They're making this about homeland security, about protecting the Egyptian way of life from a foreign threat. Notice the brilliance of this tactic: they're speaking to the general Egyptian population, not the Israelites. They're saying, "This foreigner wants to use his magic to destroy your amazing lifestyle, to take away everything you have."

It's a classic authoritarian move: transform a religious and moral challenge into a nationalist cause, making it about us versus them, about protecting borders and preserving a way of life.

translation: They said, "Postpone [the matter of] him and his brother and send among the cities gatherers
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ أَرْجِهْ وَأَخَاهُ وَأَرْسِلْ فِى ٱلْمَدَآئِنِ حَـٰشِرِينَ

The word أَرْجِهْ comes from a root meaning to grip something with your teeth. When you bite down and grind your food, you're holding off on swallowing because you need to be cautious. أَرْجِهْ conveys this same sense: "Hold off on him. Don't get to him yet. Give him time." It's like saying, "Let's finish preparing our plan carefully before we act. Don't rush into this."

"Let his brother go with him" - don't separate them yet, let them both wait. وَأَرْسِلْ فِى ٱلْمَدَآئِنِ حَـٰشِرِينَ - "Send recruiters into all the major cities." The word حَـٰشِرِينَ is particularly telling. In Arabic, this verb is used when you herd animals. If you were simply going to gather magicians through normal channels, you'd use a different word. But حَـٰشِرِينَ implies force: you find the talent, you grab them by the neck, you drag them here. There's no contract negotiation, no offering incentives to switch teams. Just grab them and bring them. It reveals the desperation and aggression of the response.

translation: Who will bring you every learned magician
commentary:
يَأْتُوكَ بِكُلِّ سَـٰحِرٍ عَلِيمٌ

"They will bring you every knowledgeable magician." The empire is mobilizing its full resources, gathering all available expertise to counter this threat.

translation: And the magicians came to Pharaoh. They said, "Indeed for us is a reward if we are the predominant
commentary:
وَجَآءَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ فِرْعَوْنَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ لَنَا لَأَجْرًا إِن كُنَّا نَحْنُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ

The magicians came to Firaun, and notice the stark difference in tone compared to Surah Ash-Shu'ara. There, in the earlier revelation, they appeared somewhat hesitant, asking questions. Here in Surah Al-A'raf, revealed later, they come with aggressive confidence: إِنَّ لَنَا لَأَجْرًا - "No doubt about it, we're going to have compensation if we win."

This isn't a question. They're not asking "Will we be paid?" They're declaring "We are going to get paid." They're telling Firaun, not asking him. The entire texture of their approach is more assertive, more self-assured. This shift reflects how Allah ﷻ presents the same story from different angles, each revelation highlighting different dimensions of the narrative.

translation: He said, "Yes, and, [moreover], you will be among those made near [to me]
commentary:
قَالَ نَعَمْ وَإِنَّكُمْ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ

Firaun's response reveals his desperation. He doesn't say "Yes, if you win, I will pay you." There's no conditional here. He immediately says نَعَمْ (yes) and then adds: وَإِنَّكُمْ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ - "And you will certainly be brought close to my administration."

He's not waiting to see the results. He's giving them the position right now, guaranteeing their place in his inner circle before they've done anything. Why such desperation? Firaun is politically astute. He recognizes that if these magicians succeed, they'll become heroes among the people. He needs to co-opt them immediately for two reasons:

First, if they gain popularity and he doesn't bring them into his administration, they might develop political ambitions of their own. People might start following the magicians instead of him. By giving them cabinet positions, he neutralizes them as potential rivals.

Second, their success reflects on him. If they're the heroes and they're standing right beside him as part of his government, then their glory becomes his glory. It's a calculated PR move: align yourself with the winners, and their shine makes you shine.

The magicians only asked for financial compensation, but Firaun, unprompted, offers them political power. This shows us how insecure his position has become.

translation: They said, "O Moses, either you throw [your staff], or we will be the ones to throw [first]
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِمَّآ أَن تُلْقِىَ وَإِمَّآ أَن نَّكُونَ نَحْنُ ٱلْمُلْقِينَ

The texture of the confrontation continues to shift. In Surah Taha, Musa (AS) speaks first. Here, the magicians speak first, demonstrating even greater confidence: "O Musa, either you throw what you're going to throw, or we'll be the ones to cast first. Whose court is the ball going to be in?"

They're so assured of their abilities that they're giving him the choice, almost as a courtesy to an opponent they're certain they'll defeat.

translation: He said, "Throw," and when they threw, they bewitched the eyes of the people and struck terror into them, and they presented a great [feat of] magic
commentary:
قَالَ أَلْقُوا۟ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَلْقَوْا۟ سَحَرُوٓا۟ أَعْيُنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَٱسْتَرْهَبُوهُمْ وَجَآءُو بِسِحْرٍ عَظِيمٍ

Musa (AS) tells them, "You throw." When they did, سَحَرُوٓا۟ أَعْيُنَ ٱلنَّاسِ - they mystified the eyes of the people, they enchanted people's vision through trickery. This description continues a theme from earlier in the surah about seeing and believing. Even falsehood can appear convincing to the eyes. People can witness something and be completely deceived by what they're seeing.

Allah ﷻ addresses this phenomenon elsewhere in the Quran, saying that even if He sent down physical scrolls from the sky that people could touch with their own hands, they would still say their eyes must be drunk, that they must be hallucinating. The point is profound: visual evidence alone doesn't guarantee conviction when hearts are closed.

وَٱسْتَرْهَبُوهُمْ - they instilled fear in them. The word رَهَب specifically refers to fear with immediate consequences, fear of something that's about to attack you right now. When their ropes and rods began moving around, people became terrified. This wasn't just impressive - it was frightening.

وَجَآءُو بِسِحْرٍ عَظِيمٍ - Allah ﷻ Himself describes what they brought as "tremendous magic." Even the Quran acknowledges this was no amateur performance. Those recruiters found elite practitioners, and they put on quite a show. This wasn't a small thing they did.

translation: And We inspired to Moses, "Throw your staff," and at once it devoured what they were falsifying
commentary:
۞ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنْ أَلْقِ عَصَاكَ ۖ فَإِذَا هِىَ تَلْقَفُ مَا يَأْفِكُونَ

Then We revealed to Musa (AS) to throw his staff. The fact that Allah ﷻ had to reveal this instruction tells us something significant: the magicians had mystified and scared the people so effectively that even Musa (AS) was momentarily frozen, uncertain of what to do. We learn from other passages that even his brother Harun (AS) didn't know how to respond. The impact of their performance was overwhelming.

Allah ﷻ essentially coaches him from ringside: "Don't get frozen by what just happened. Remember what to do. Throw the staff."

فَإِذَا هِىَ تَلْقَفُ - then immediately, out of nowhere, it swallowed. The word تَلْقَفُ is one of several words in the Quran for swallowing, but this particular term means to devour something quickly, in one bite. It's like when you're eating and your brother has his eye on something on your plate, so you move faster and just finish it in one gulp. That urgency, that immediacy is captured in تَلْقَفُ.

Two elements emphasize the speed: فَإِذَا (immediately, out of nowhere) and تَلْقَفُ (swallowed in one swift motion). The moment Musa (AS) threw the staff, it consumed their illusions instantly.

Notice something subtle: earlier, the word ثُعْبَانٌ was used, which is masculine. But here the pronoun is feminine: هِىَ (it). Why? Because the reality is that it's still Musa's staff. The staff عَصَا is feminine in Arabic. So even while manifesting as this massive serpent, linguistically it remains referenced as the staff underneath. Otherwise it would have been "he swallowed" using masculine pronouns. This linguistic precision maintains the connection to its true nature.

مَا يَأْفِكُونَ - whatever lies they had fabricated, whatever deception they had come up with.

translation: So the truth was established, and abolished was what they were doing
commentary:
فَوَقَعَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَبَطَلَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

فَوَقَعَ ٱلْحَقُّ - the truth fell, it dropped. The imagery is powerful: imagine a boulder falling from height, a mountain descending, a meteor striking the earth. Later in the Quran, we read about how the mountain was suspended above the Israelites and they were convinced it would drop and crush them. That's the image here - the truth landing with crushing, undeniable weight.

وَبَطَلَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ - and falsified, negated, cancelled out everything they had been working on. Their entire performance, all their preparation, everything they had practiced and perfected - nullified in an instant.

translation: And Pharaoh and his people were overcome right there and became debased
commentary:
فَغُلِبُوا۟ هُنَالِكَ وَٱنقَلَبُوا۟ صَـٰغِرِينَ

"Then they were dominated then and there, and they were transformed into humiliated ones." Now here's something crucial to notice: you might think this ayah is describing the magicians. But pay attention to what comes next. If this were about the magicians, the pronouns would simply continue. The subject would stay the same.

But the pronouns shift. This verse isn't describing the magicians becoming humiliated - the next ayah will show us the magicians are about to be honored. Who then was humiliated? Firaun and his generals, his chiefs, his entire administrative staff standing there. They're the ones who have been transformed into the humiliated. The entire crowd is witnessing their defeat. They've been publicly exposed and embarrassed before the nation they rule.

translation: And the magicians fell down in prostration [to Allah]
commentary:
وَأُلْقِىَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ سَـٰجِدِينَ

Meanwhile, وَأُلْقِىَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ - the magicians were thrown. The passive voice and the verb أُلْقِىَ (they were cast down) indicates they couldn't even help themselves. It was as if an overwhelming force just pushed them down سَـٰجِدِينَ - into a state of prostration.

Combine the imagery: the truth was this tremendous weight that came down, and it physically forced them into sujood. They didn't decide to prostrate - they were compelled by the sheer reality of what they had witnessed. Their expertise in magic meant they alone fully understood that what Musa (AS) did was no trick. They knew every technique, every method of illusion. What they saw transcended all of that completely.

translation: They said, "We have believed in the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

They declared: "We believe in the Master of all peoples, all nations."

translation: The Lord of Moses and Aaron
commentary:
رَبِّ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ

"The Master of Musa and Harun" - they specify to eliminate any ambiguity. They're not declaring faith in Firaun. They're explicitly identifying whose authority they now recognize.

Interestingly, notice which name comes first: رَبِّ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ - the Master of Musa and Harun. In one place in the Quran, you'll find رَبِّ هَارُونَ وَمُوسَىٰ where Harun's name is mentioned first. That occurs in the surah that highlights Harun's role more than any other. Here in Al-A'raf, have we learned much about Harun (AS) so far? Not yet. But have we heard extensively about Musa (AS)? Yes. So his name is mentioned first.

Even the sequencing of names is influenced by the context and emphasis of each passage. Every word choice in the Quran serves the purpose of that particular revelation.

translation: Said Pharaoh, "You believed in him before I gave you permission. Indeed, this is a conspiracy which you conspired in the city to expel therefrom its people. But you are going to know
commentary:
قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ ءَامَنتُم بِهِۦ قَبْلَ أَنْ ءَاذَنَ لَكُمْ ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَمَكْرٌ مَّكَرْتُمُوهُ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ لِتُخْرِجُوا۟ مِنْهَآ أَهْلَهَا ۖ فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ

*Fir'awn said, "You believed in him before I gave you permission. Indeed, this is a conspiracy you conspired in the city so you could expel from it its people. But you are going to know."*

Fir'awn's immediate reaction reveals the depth of his humiliation. He realizes the magicians haven't just given up—they've actually believed in Musa (AS) as a messenger. The phrase آمَنتُم بِهِ (you believed in him) captures this recognition that they've submitted completely, thrown in the towel, and accepted Musa's message.

In other accounts of this confrontation, Fir'awn first accused Musa (AS) directly, calling him the chief magician who taught them magic. But in Surah Al-A'raf, the narrative shows a more aggressive exchange from the beginning. The magicians themselves were more confrontational, even in their initial dialogue. So when Fir'awn responds, he goes straight after them: إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَمَكْرٌ مَّكَرْتُمُوهُ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ—"This is a plot you guys came up with, you hashed it together in this city."

His accusation is calculated. He claims they conspired to expel the great citizens from the city. But who is listening to this conversation? Everyone. Fir'awn has already been humiliated. He can no longer rely on his chiefs and generals to make him feel important. The only people who can validate him now are the masses. Before this crisis, he tried to make his chieftains feel significant by consulting them about what to do with Musa (AS). But that strategy has failed.

Now he turns to nationalist rhetoric, making it about the people's land. He's essentially saying, "They want to kick you out of your land." This is a patriotic appeal designed to turn public sentiment against the magicians and Musa (AS). The phrase فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (you will soon find out) is a veiled threat meant for the crowd as much as for the magicians—whoever messes with "my people" will see what I do to them.

translation: I will surely cut off your hands and your feet on opposite sides; then I will surely crucify you all
commentary:
لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُم مِّنْ خِلَـٰفٍ ثُمَّ لَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ

*"I will surely cut off your hands and your feet on opposite sides; then I will surely crucify you all."*

Fir'awn escalates to brutal threats. The phrase لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ (I will surely cut off) carries an emphatic oath—he swears he will carry out this punishment. The specification مِّنْ خِلَـٰفٍ (from opposite sides) describes the horrific detail of cutting the right hand with the left foot, or vice versa, a form of mutilation designed to humiliate and torture.

Then comes ثُمَّ لَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ—"then I will crucify all of you together." This is meant to be the ultimate display of power, a public execution that serves as a warning to anyone who dares challenge his authority.

translation: They said, "Indeed, to our Lord we will return
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا مُنقَلِبُونَ

*They said, "Indeed, to our Lord we will return."*

The magicians' response is strikingly calm and profound. They simply said, إِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا مُنقَلِبُونَ—"No doubt about it, we're going to be returning to our real Master anyway."

The casualness of their tone is remarkable given the horrific threats they just heard. The word مُنقَلِبُونَ (we will return) is significant because it inherently acknowledges that death is not the end. You're just being taken away from here to somewhere else. The very structure of the word suggests continuation beyond this life, unlike simply saying "we will die," which might imply finality.

These magicians, who moments before were devoted servants of Fir'awn, now demonstrate complete detachment from this worldly life. They've recognized a reality far more important than Fir'awn's threats.

translation: And you do not resent us except because we believed in the signs of our Lord when they came to us. Our Lord, pour upon us patience and let us die as Muslims [in submission to You]
commentary:
وَمَا تَنقِمُ مِنَّآ إِلَّآ أَنْ ءَامَنَّا بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّنَا لَمَّا جَآءَتْنَا ۚ رَبَّنَآ أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنَا صَبْرًا وَتَوَفَّنَا مُسْلِمِينَ

*"And you do not resent us except because we believed in the signs of our Lord when they came to us. Our Lord, pour upon us patience and let us die as Muslims."*

The magicians continue with powerful clarity: وَمَا تَنقِمُ مِنَّآ إِلَّآ أَنْ ءَامَنَّا بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّنَا—"What revenge are you taking from us? You're not taking revenge on us at all, except for one reason: that we have come to believe in the miraculous signs of our Master when they came to us."

This response reveals a crucial understanding. As experts in their field, these magicians knew the limits of magic. An expert in any discipline—whether physics, chemistry, or neuroscience—knows how far that field can go. These were the top magicians in Egypt, the best of the best. They knew exactly what magic could and couldn't accomplish. When they saw Musa's staff transform and consume their illusions, they immediately recognized this wasn't magic. Why? Because they knew magic couldn't go that far.

The general crowd, however, couldn't tell the difference. For them, this was just better magic versus lesser magic. They weren't mature enough to understand that you have to really know magic to recognize when something transcends it completely. This is why the magicians fell into prostration immediately—they could differentiate between human effort and divine miracle.

In some sense, this applies to the Quran as well. When someone studies Arabic in depth, one of their goals is of course to find guidance. But another goal is to appreciate the difference between the best of human speech and Allah's ﷻ creative speech. As they progress, they start appreciating the miracle of the Quran more and more. When someone truly appreciates this miracle—because they can compare the peak of what humans can do with language to what Allah ﷻ does—what should be their reaction? They should be overwhelmed, thrown into prostration by its beauty. That's the point of appreciating the miracle: it should increase you in your pursuit of guidance and commitment to that guidance.

Appreciating a miracle is like waking up. The magicians suddenly saw what they'd been blind to before. And when you see clearly after being in darkness, the behavior of someone truly awake follows. The first natural response is prostration.

Their supplication رَبَّنَآ أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنَا صَبْرًا (Our Master, pour patience onto us) is significant. This same dua appears twice in the Quran, both times connected to followers of Musa (AS). First with these magicians, and later with the sincere followers of Musa (AS) who came generations after, during the time of Dawud (AS) and Talut, when they faced the army of Jalut. They said, "رَبَّنَآ أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنَا صَبْرًا وَثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا—Our Lord, pour patience upon us and plant our feet firmly." 

Perhaps they remembered how the magicians and Musa (AS) had to face impossible opposition from Fir'awn, and they drew from that historical dua because it was part of their tradition. By the time we get to Dawud (AS) and later Sulayman (AS), this supplication had become part of their inherited legacy. Just as we draw from the supplications of Ibrahim (AS), Zakariyya (AS), and other prophets, they drew from the prayers taught to them by those who came before.

The phrase وَتَوَفَّنَا مُسْلِمِينَ (and give us death as Muslims) uses تَوَفَّنَا, which means "take us away." The word itself suggests that you believe this isn't the end—you're just being taken away from here to somewhere else. It's different from simply saying "let us die," which might not carry that implication. They already understood that this life isn't the only one, and that understanding is captured beautifully in this word choice.

translation: And the eminent among the people of Pharaoh said," Will you leave Moses and his people to cause corruption in the land and abandon you and your gods?" [Pharaoh] said, "We will kill their sons and keep their women alive; and indeed, we are subjugators over them
commentary:
وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِ فِرْعَوْنَ أَتَذَرُ مُوسَىٰ وَقَوْمَهُۥ لِيُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَيَذَرَكَ وَءَالِهَتَكَ ۚ قَالَ سَنُقَتِّلُ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ وَنَسْتَحْىِۦ نِسَآءَهُمْ وَإِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قَـٰهِرُونَ

*And the eminent among the people of Fir'awn said, "Will you leave Musa and his people to cause corruption in the land and abandon you and your gods?" He said, "We will kill their sons and keep their women alive; and indeed, we are subjugators over them."*

After this crisis, there's an emergency meeting among Fir'awn's chiefs and generals—imagine their equivalent of a Pentagon security council. The generals are yelling and screaming: "How did you let it get this far?" They're now confronting Fir'awn himself, challenging his leadership.

Their argument is direct: أَتَذَرُ مُوسَىٰ وَقَوْمَهُۥ—"Are you going to just leave Musa and his group of people?" لِيُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ—"So they can create more corruption in the earth? Haven't they done enough damage already? Let's just execute them and be done with it."

What's particularly interesting is the phrase وَيَذَرَكَ وَءَالِهَتَكَ—"and further abandon you and your gods." The generals recognize that Fir'awn also has gods. This is an intricate detail of actual Egyptian history being acknowledged in the Quran. Yes, Fir'awn claimed to be the lord and god of the earth, but that doesn't mean he didn't believe in false gods himself. In Egyptian history, the ruling class believed they were descendants of the god of the sky—the sun. They worshiped the sun god. So the generals are saying, "He's not just disloyal to you and this country, he's disrespecting your gods too."

Now notice something crucial: Fir'awn still has a soft spot for Musa (AS). He can't bring himself to issue the death warrant, to give the executive order to just be done with it. If he does, it's over. But he also can't say, "No, no, he's my brother, I love him," because then he'll look weak before his generals. And if he appears weak, the generals might decide they need to take care of business—and if they can't get rid of Musa (AS), they might get rid of Fir'awn himself.

So Fir'awn needs to look tough right now, but he can't get himself to say, "Okay, fine, kill Musa." His solution? Ignore Musa (AS) entirely and target everyone else. He completely disregards what the generals said about "Musa and his nation." Instead, he focuses only on the nation: سَنُقَتِّلُ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ وَنَسْتَحْىِۦ نِسَآءَهُمْ—"We will kill all of their sons and let their women live."

This is a form of deep humiliation. It doesn't need to be spelled out what they intended to do with those women. The statement وَإِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قَـٰهِرُونَ—"We are the dominant force over them"—is Fir'awn reasserting his power. It's as if he's saying, "Who do they think they are, talking like that in that crowd? They think they've got some momentum?"

translation: Said Moses to his people, "Seek help through Allah and be patient. Indeed, the earth belongs to Allah. He causes to inherit it whom He wills of His servants. And the [best] outcome is for the righteous
commentary:
قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِ ٱسْتَعِينُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱصْبِرُوٓا۟ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ يُورِثُهَا مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَٱلْعَـٰقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ

*Musa said to his people, "Seek help through Allah and be patient. Indeed, the earth belongs to Allah. He causes to inherit it whom He wills of His servants. And the [best] outcome is for the righteous."*

Meanwhile, Musa (AS) is having an important meeting with his own people. His message is clear and direct: ٱسْتَعِينُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱصْبِرُوٓا۟—"Seek help from Allah ﷻ and remain patient." Tough times are coming.

Then he reminds them of a fundamental reality: إِنَّ ٱلْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ—"The land belongs to Allah ﷻ." يُورِثُهَا مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ—"He will give it as an inheritance to whomever He wants from among His slaves." This word يُورِثُ (He will cause to inherit) is powerful—it suggests that the earth is passed down according to Allah's ﷻ will, not human scheming.

Then comes the promise: وَٱلْعَـٰقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ—"And the eventual outcome, the last laugh, is in favor of those who are truly cautious of Him."

But Musa's followers are understandably terrified. They realize what just happened means serious trouble. Whether Musa (AS) gets killed or not isn't even the main concern—they know one thing for sure: they're about to be wiped out.

translation: They said, "We have been harmed before you came to us and after you have come to us." He said, "Perhaps your Lord will destroy your enemy and grant you succession in the land and see how you will do
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ أُوذِينَا مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَأْتِيَنَا وَمِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جِئْتَنَا ۚ قَالَ عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يُهْلِكَ عَدُوَّكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ

*They said, "We have been harmed before you came to us and after you have come to us." He said, "Perhaps your Lord will destroy your enemy and grant you succession in the land and see how you will do."*

Their complaint is bitter: أُوذِينَا مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَأْتِيَنَا وَمِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جِئْتَنَا—"We were beaten up, tortured, hurt way before you ever came, and even after you came." The implication is clear: "Thanks a lot, friend. You were no help at all."

Notice Musa's response—or rather, his lack of direct response. This is characteristic of his communication style. When people say foolish or hurtful things to him—whether it's Fir'awn or even his own people—he often pretends he didn't hear them. He doesn't dignify the complaint with a direct answer. He just continues with his previous message, moving forward. It's a choice: either address the negativity (and possibly respond harshly) or simply ignore it and continue teaching.

So he says, عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يُهْلِكَ عَدُوَّكُمْ—"It may be—in fact, it's very likely—that your Lord is going to destroy your enemy." وَيَسْتَخْلِفَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ—"And then He will leave you behind on the earth, make you successors." The word يَسْتَخْلِف means to leave someone in charge, to put them in a position of authority and responsibility.

Then comes the crucial test: فَيَنظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ—"And then He will see how you do." In other words, they're about to be killed, and Musa (AS) is telling them: No, Allah ﷻ will very likely destroy your enemy, make you in charge of the earth, and then test you with the authority He gives you.

Did this come true? Absolutely. After the life of Musa (AS), almost the entire earth came under the rule of Sulayman (AS)—a huge, expansive empire. And notice the structure of the prophecy: it's not just about being given authority. It's about what you do with that authority. Allah ﷻ is saying, "I know how you're dealing with weakness. But how will you deal with power?"

This is the question Allah ﷻ poses to any nation that is weakened and then given a position of power. The Muslims were weakened in Makkah, then given power in Madinah and beyond. And today we find ourselves in a position of weakness again. How did we get here? We abused our power. Allah ﷻ tested us when He gave us the resources of the earth, and He literally said to us through this ayah: فَيَنظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ—"Let's see how you do."

If you study contemporary history—the post-colonial era and the formation of many Muslim countries like Pakistan, Algeria, and Egypt being freed from colonial rule—political scientists of that time projected these countries would collapse. They shouldn't survive, they said. The infrastructure wasn't there, corruption was rampant, there were countless problems. But somehow these nations chugged along and survived.

That survival should have been enough for Muslim lands to get their act together, to use that opportunity to build something better. Have we? In many cases, we've gone deeper into corruption and dysfunction. We were given power, we didn't respect it, and as a result, it's been stripped from us again. The lesson of فَيَنظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ remains eternally relevant.

translation: And We certainly seized the people of Pharaoh with years of famine and a deficiency in fruits that perhaps they would be reminded
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنَآ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ بِٱلسِّنِينَ وَنَقْصٍ مِّنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ

*And We certainly seized the people of Fir'awn with years of famine and a deficiency in fruits that perhaps they would be reminded.*

Now the conversation shifts back to Fir'awn's side. Allah ﷻ says, وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنَآ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ بِٱلسِّنِينَ—"We grabbed the followers of Fir'awn with very tough years." The word ٱلسِّنِينَ refers specifically to years of famine and difficulty.

There's a linguistic note here: the difference between سَنَة and عَام in classical Arabic. عَام is a neutral year, while سَنَة is a difficult, tough year. When Allah ﷻ describes the lifespan of Nuh (AS), He says فَلَبِثَ فِيهِمْ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ إِلَّا خَمْسِينَ عَامًا—"He remained among them a thousand years except fifty years." Why the difference? The 950 years were سِنِين (tough years), but the 50 he didn't live were عَام (easier years). This is the precision of Quranic language.

Allah ﷻ started showing them difficult signs: وَنَقْصٍ مِّنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ—"and We caused them to have less in terms of fruits." Drought began, and this was catastrophic for Egypt. All the major ancient Egyptian cities were built along the Nile River. When drought happens and the water levels drop, crops disappear. The very foundation of their civilization—agriculture and trade—starts crumbling.

Why does Allah ﷻ do this? لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ—"So hopefully they will wake up and make a little more effort to remember." This is the purpose of trials: to shake people out of their heedlessness.

translation: But when good came to them, they said, "This is ours [by right]." And if a bad [condition] struck them, they saw an evil omen in Moses and those with him. Unquestionably, their fortune is with Allah, but most of them do not know
commentary:
فَإِذَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْحَسَنَةُ قَالُوا۟ لَنَا هَـٰذِهِۦ ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَطَّيَّرُوا۟ بِمُوسَىٰ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥٓ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّمَا طَـٰٓئِرُهُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

*But when good came to them, they said, "This is ours." And if a bad [condition] struck them, they saw an evil omen in Musa and those with him. Unquestionably, their fortune is with Allah, but most of them do not know.*

Here Allah ﷻ exposes their twisted mentality. فَإِذَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْحَسَنَةُ—"Then whenever the good came to them." Notice the phrasing: it's not الْحَسَنَات (the good things, plural), but rather ٱلْحَسَنَةُ (the good thing, singular with the definite article). This implies it wasn't happening all the time. When one good thing came—some rain, a decent harvest—it became a big deal because good had become such a rare occasion.

Their response? قَالُوا۟ لَنَا هَـٰذِهِۦ—"They said, 'This is ours.' Obviously we're Egypt. That's why good stuff happens to us." They attributed success to themselves, their superiority, their nation, their gods—anything except the true source.

But وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ—"whenever calamity would fall upon them, some bad thing would happen"—their reaction was completely different: يَطَّيَّرُوا۟ بِمُوسَىٰ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥٓ—"they would attribute the curse to Musa and whoever was with him."

The word يَطَّيَّرُوا۟ comes from الطَّيْر, which means bird. In classical Arabic culture, certain birds like vultures and crows were considered symbols of death and bad luck. So يَطَّيَّرُوا۟ means to attribute bad omens, to see someone as a curse, like that bird of bad fortune. They essentially said, "If we believed in bad omens, Musa would be it. He's bad luck."

Allah ﷻ responds definitively: أَلَآ إِنَّمَا طَـٰٓئِرُهُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ—"Tell them: their curse, their fortune, all of their attributions belong with Allah ﷻ alone." Whatever they make up is meaningless. Allah ﷻ controls all outcomes.

And then the final observation: وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ—"However, most of them have no knowledge at all. Most of them don't even realize what they're talking about."

This is the mindset of those who refuse guidance: when things go well, they credit themselves; when things go wrong, they blame the messenger. They never look inward. They never consider that perhaps the trials are meant to wake them up, to turn them back to their Creator. Instead, they remain in willful ignorance, and that ignorance becomes their downfall.

translation: And they said, "No matter what sign you bring us with which to bewitch us, we will not be believers in you
commentary:
**وَقَالُوا۟ مَهْمَا تَأْتِنَا بِهِۦ مِنْ ءَايَةٍ لِّتَسْحَرَنَا بِهَا فَمَا نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ**

The arrogance of Pharaoh's people reaches its peak in this ayah. They declare: "Whatever you bring us of miraculous signs, we will never believe in you." The word مَهْمَا is a hyperbolic expression meaning "whatever" or "no matter what" - emphasizing their absolute rejection. They're essentially saying: "Bring whatever you want, Musa. We don't care what kind of miracle you show us."

Notice how they frame their rejection: لِّتَسْحَرَنَا بِهَا - "to cast a spell on us with it" or "to mystify us with it." They've already decided that whatever Musa (AS) brings is magic, not truth. This is the mindset of someone who has closed their heart before even examining the evidence. They conclude: فَمَا نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ - "We are never going to believe for your sake." The emphasis here is critical - they're saying they won't accept the truth even if they recognize it, simply because it comes from Musa (AS).

This attitude represents the ultimate level of arrogance, where a person refuses guidance not because the evidence is lacking, but because accepting it would require humility.

translation: So We sent upon them the flood and locusts and lice and frogs and blood as distinct signs, but they were arrogant and were a criminal people
commentary:
**فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلطُّوفَانَ وَٱلْجَرَادَ وَٱلْقُمَّلَ وَٱلضَّفَادِعَ وَٱلدَّمَ ءَايَـٰتٍ مُّفَصَّلَـٰتٍ فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا مُّجْرِمِينَ**

After their brazen challenge, Allah ﷻ responds with a series of devastating signs. فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ - "Then We sent upon them" - Allah ﷻ takes up their challenge directly.

The punishments come in sequence: الطُّوفَانَ- storms or floods. The word comes from the root طاف, meaning to circle or go around, which describes how tornadoes and cyclones move. Then came الْجَرَادَ- locusts that infested everything. These insects spread throughout the land, in beds, in food, everywhere. You couldn't escape them.

After the locusts came الْقُمَّلَ - lice that covered everything and everyone. Then الضَّفَادِعَ - frogs appeared everywhere. According to some narrations, they showed up inside food, in pots and pans, in drinking water. People couldn't go to bed without finding frogs there first.

Finally, الدَّمَ - blood. The rivers turned blood red, and wells began producing blood instead of water.

These were ءَايَـٰتٍ مُّفَصَّلَـٰتٍ - signs that were mufassalat. This word carries two meanings: First, they were "well-explained" and "self-evident" - clearly divine interventions that couldn't be explained away. Second, mufassalat also means "separated" or "distinct" - these calamities didn't all happen at once. They came one after another, giving people time to reflect between each trial.

Despite all this, فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ - they remained arrogant. وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا مُّجْرِمِينَ - they had been a criminal nation all along. Their arrogance wasn't new; it was deeply rooted in their character.

translation: And when the punishment descended upon them, they said, "O Moses, invoke for us your Lord by what He has promised you. If you [can] remove the punishment from us, we will surely believe you, and we will send with you the Children of Israel
commentary:
**وَلَمَّا وَقَعَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلرِّجْزُ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَى ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ بِمَا عَهِدَ عِندَكَ ۖ لَئِن كَشَفْتَ عَنَّا ٱلرِّجْزَ لَنُؤْمِنَنَّ لَكَ وَلَنُرْسِلَنَّ مَعَكَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ**

The word الرِّجْزُ is particularly meaningful. In Arabic, rijz originally refers to war poetry - the kind of terrifying chants soldiers would recite to frighten their enemies or civilians. It's poetry designed to leave you psychologically disturbed and unsettled. When used for punishment, it describes a calamity so intense that it becomes mentally overwhelming. People couldn't stop thinking about it; it consumed their thoughts and left them in constant distress.

When this level of torment fell upon them, they finally broke: يَـٰمُوسَى ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ - "O Musa, call upon your Lord for us." Notice they still say "your Lord," not "our Lord." They're acknowledging His power but not submitting to Him.

This is actually the second time in the Quran we see Musa (AS) being asked to make dua for others. The first was in Surah Al-Baqarah when Bani Israel themselves asked him about the cow. But here, the disbelievers are asking. This reveals something important: questioning your messenger in this demanding way is an attitude of disbelief, regardless of who does it.

They continue: بِمَا عَهِدَ عِندَكَ - "by whatever promise He has with you" or "because of whatever agreement He has made with you." Then comes their conditional promise: لَئِن كَشَفْتَ عَنَّا ٱلرِّجْزَ - "If you remove this punishment from us" - using the emphatic لَئِن to stress their sincerity.

لَنُؤْمِنَنَّ لَكَ - "We will definitely believe in you" or more accurately, "we will give in to you." The phrase لَكَ - "for you" - shows this isn't about accepting truth, but about meeting his demands because they have no other choice. They've been brought so low that even Pharaoh's chiefs and generals don't know how to solve this problem except through the help of Allah ﷻ.

وَلَنُرْسِلَنَّ مَعَكَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ - "And we will send the Children of Israel with you." They swear they'll let the Israelites go. The multiple emphatic constructions show how desperately they want relief.

translation: But when We removed the punishment from them until a term which they were to reach, then at once they broke their word
commentary:
**فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمُ ٱلرِّجْزَ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ هُم بَـٰلِغُوهُ إِذَا هُمْ يَنكُثُونَ**

The word كَشَفْنَا is significant. كشف means to remove something covering something else, with the implication of exposing what's underneath. When Allah ﷻ removed their problems, He exposed their true nature - what was really in their hearts.

إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ هُم بَـٰلِغُوهُ - "until a term they would reach." Allah ﷻ gave them relief for a specified time - perhaps a week or two weeks. Musa (AS) made the dua, and the punishment stopped according to the agreed timeframe.

إِذَا هُمْ يَنكُثُونَ - "Then suddenly they broke their promise." The word نكث is the opposite of وفى - fulfilling a promise. It's the most general term for violating agreements. The Quran uses several words for breaking promises, and this is the broadest of them.

As soon as the punishment ended, they immediately denied ever making the agreement. "What are you talking about, Musa? We never promised you anything." This cycle of making promises under pressure and breaking them in comfort reveals their fundamental dishonesty.

translation: So We took retribution from them, and We drowned them in the sea because they denied Our signs and were heedless of them
commentary:
**فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَكَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا غَـٰفِلِينَ**

فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ - "Then We took revenge from them" or "We avenged against them." This isn't petty vengeance; it's divine justice after countless chances and warnings.

فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ - "And We drowned them in the yamm." اليَمّ is one of several words the Quran uses for bodies of water. Some scholars say it refers specifically to large bodies of water like vast rivers, lakes or oceans. Others note that yamm refers to water flowing downstream - water with a current.

بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا - "Because they denied Our signs." This was their final crime - outright rejection and denial of Allah's ﷻ clear signs.

وَكَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا غَـٰفِلِينَ - "And they had been heedless even before that." This is crucial to understand. There are two levels of rejection. The ultimate level is كذّب - actively denying and lying against the signs. But before someone reaches that level, they start with غفلة - heedlessness, not paying attention.

Pharaoh's people had been heedless long before they reached the stage of active denial. That initial heedlessness eventually led them to the point where they could brazenly lie against Allah's ﷻ signs. This teaches us that neglecting spiritual awareness, even when it seems harmless, can lead down a dangerous path.

translation: And We caused the people who had been oppressed to inherit the eastern regions of the land and the western ones, which We had blessed. And the good word of your Lord was fulfilled for the Children of Israel because of what they had patiently endured. And We destroyed [all] that Pharaoh and his people were producing and what they had been building
commentary:
**وَأَوْرَثْنَا ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَانُوا۟ يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشَـٰرِقَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَغَـٰرِبَهَا ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا ۖ وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ عَلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ ۖ وَدَمَّرْنَا مَا كَانَ يَصْنَعُ فِرْعَوْنُ وَقَوْمُهُۥ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْرِشُونَ**

The ayah begins with a beautiful reversal: وَأَوْرَثْنَا ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَانُوا۟ يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ - "And We caused the people who had been oppressed to inherit." The weak became the inheritors. This echoes what Prophet Nuh (AS) said: the earth belongs to Allah ﷻ, and He gives it to whomever He wills from among His servants.

مَشَـٰرِقَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَغَـٰرِبَهَا - "The east of the earth and its west." This refers to the vast empire that would eventually come under the authority of the descendants of the Prophets - the empire of Dawud and Sulayman (AS), which spread across much of the known world.

ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا - "Which We had blessed in it." The center and capital of this blessed land was Al-Quds, Jerusalem, the sacred land. The east and west of that blessed territory were given to Bani Israel.

وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ - "And the beautiful word of your Lord was fulfilled." Which word? The promise that was conveyed through Musa (AS). Here's a subtle but important point: When Musa (AS) spoke to Pharaoh's people, he said "perhaps my Lord will destroy you and make you successors in the land" - using عَسَىٰ (perhaps). He spoke humbly, not making definitive claims about what Allah ﷻ would do.

But now Allah ﷻ says this was "My word" - My promise. When a prophet speaks, even with apparent uncertainty, if it comes to pass, it was divine promise all along. What appears in narrations as the words of Musa (AS) - what we might call hadith of the prophet - Allah ﷻ now claims as His own beautiful promise. This teaches us the status of prophetic speech: it's not separate from revelation, even when it's not part of the revealed book.

عَلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ - "Upon the Children of Israel for what they patiently endured." The promise was fulfilled because of their patience through years of oppression and persecution. Their sabr (patience) earned them this divine reward.

وَدَمَّرْنَا مَا كَانَ يَصْنَعُ فِرْعَوْنُ وَقَوْمُهُۥ - "And We destroyed what Pharaoh and his people had been making." Allah ﷻ completely annihilated their works and constructions.

وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْرِشُونَ - "And what they had been raising high." The word عرش refers to elevated structures - thrones, but also elaborate agricultural systems and impressive architecture. Everything they had built up, all their engineering marvels and farming infrastructure, all the symbols of their power and civilization - Allah ﷻ demolished it completely.

translation: And We took the Children of Israel across the sea; then they came upon a people intent in devotion to [some] idols of theirs. They said, "O Moses, make for us a god just as they have gods." He said, "Indeed, you are a people behaving ignorantly
commentary:
**وَجَـٰوَزْنَا بِبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْبَحْرَ فَأَتَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ يَعْكُفُونَ عَلَىٰٓ أَصْنَامٍ لَّهُمْ ۚ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَى ٱجْعَل لَّنَآ إِلَـٰهًا كَمَا لَهُمْ ءَالِهَةٌ ۚ قَالَ إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ**

Notice how Allah ﷻ phrases this: وَجَـٰوَزْنَا بِبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ - "We caused the Children of Israel to cross." He doesn't say "they crossed" as if they did it themselves. He takes full credit because they couldn't have crossed without His help. This teaches us humility - even our greatest achievements are only possible because Allah ﷻ enables them.

After crossing the sea, they entered the land where their forefather Ibrahim (AS) and Ya'qub (AS) had lived. But things had changed since their ancestors left. The teachings of the prophets had faded, and the people had fallen into various forms of shirk (associating partners with Allah ﷻ) and strange practices.

فَأَتَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ - "They came upon a people" - like encountering an aboriginal tribe or an isolated cult. يَعْكُفُونَ عَلَىٰٓ أَصْنَامٍ لَّهُمْ - "devoted to some idols of theirs." The word عكوف (i'tikaf) is significant. It means to seclude oneself and concentrate deeply, removing all distractions to focus on worship. This is the same word used for the Islamic practice of i'tikaf in the mosque.

This gives us insight into the psychology of shirk. There's a lower level of idol worship where people kiss the idol, wash it, place food before it - treating the physical object as if it has power. But there's also a higher philosophical level of idol worship. The practitioners of this "higher" shirk say: "We don't actually worship these idols. We know they're just stone. They're just representatives, symbols that help us concentrate on the higher power. They help us remember God better."

This rationalization is important to understand because similar thinking exists even among Muslims today. Some people treat the Ka'bah itself as an object of worship - hugging it, trying to take pieces from it, doing sajdah toward it as if the structure itself has power. We've forgotten the example of Umar ibn Al-Khattab (RA), one of the most spiritually elevated companions, who approached the Black Stone and said clearly: "I know you're just a rock. You can't benefit or harm anyone. I'm only kissing you because the Prophet ﷺ did it." He wanted to be absolutely clear that his reverence was for the prophetic practice, not for the stone itself.

This is why the Sahaba warned about decorating mosques excessively. The Prophet ﷺ said that when you start beautifying and decorating mosques, you should know that destruction has already fallen upon you - because you're concentrating on the wrong things.

So when Bani Israel saw these people sitting in deep concentration before their idols, they were impressed. قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَى ٱجْعَل لَّنَآ إِلَـٰهًا كَمَا لَهُمْ ءَالِهَةٌ - "They said, 'O Musa, make for us a god just as they have gods.'" The irony is stark. These people were in the desert with no distractions - the perfect place to concentrate on Allah ﷻ. Yet they looked at idol worshippers and thought: "Look how focused they are! We need something like that to help us concentrate!"

But here's the reality: when someone engages in shirk, Shaytan leaves them alone. He says, "Go ahead, concentrate." He doesn't need to distract them because they're already on the wrong path. But when a Muslim tries to focus on Allah ﷻ, Shaytan works overtime to create distractions. The idol worshipper's "concentration" isn't a blessing - it's evidence that Shaytan has no need to disturb them.

قَالَ إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ - Musa (AS) responds: "You are a people who are ignorant." The word تَجْهَلُونَ is powerful. It doesn't just mean lacking knowledge. Jahl means letting your emotions do the thinking for you, not holding yourself back. The opposite is حِلْم - restraint and wisdom. Musa (AS) is saying: "You're not thinking straight. You're being emotional and impulsive. You're letting your feelings override your intellect."

translation: Indeed, those [worshippers] - destroyed is that in which they are [engaged], and worthless is whatever they were doing
commentary:
**إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مُتَبَّرٌ مَّا هُمْ فِيهِ وَبَـٰطِلٌ مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ**

Musa (AS) explains why their request is so foolish: إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ - "Indeed, these people..." He's referring to the idol worshippers they saw.

مُتَبَّرٌ مَّا هُمْ فِيهِ - "Destroyed is what they are engaged in." The word تَبَّرَ means to be utterly shattered, annihilated, completely dismantled. It's related to تَبَار, which means destruction so complete that nothing remains.

وَبَـٰطِلٌ مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ - "And worthless is what they have been doing." The grammatically standard order would be: "What they are doing is batil (worthless)." But notice how the ayah restructures the sentence, putting the word بَـٰطِلٌ (worthless) at the beginning, and it does this twice.

This grammatical fronting (called taqdim) shows emphasis and urgency. Musa (AS) is so eager to make them understand how utterly pointless this is that he can't even wait to complete the normal sentence structure. He needs them to hear "WORTHLESS" first, "DESTROYED" first, before anything else. It's like someone trying to stop you from touching something harmful - they don't calmly explain, they shout "DON'T!" first.

The emphatic structure communicates: "Destroyed - utterly destroyed - is what they're involved in! And worthless - completely worthless - is everything they're doing!"

translation: He said, "Is it other than Allah I should desire for you as a god while He has preferred you over the worlds
commentary:
**قَالَ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْغِيكُمْ إِلَـٰهًا وَهُوَ فَضَّلَكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ**

Musa (AS) continues his response with a rhetorical question that cuts to the heart of the matter: قَالَ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْغِيكُمْ إِلَـٰهًا - "Should I seek for you a god other than Allah?"

The word أَبْغِي means "should I seek" or "should I search for." There's an element of absurdity in the question itself. He's saying: "Are you seriously asking me to go looking for some other deity when you have Allah ﷻ?"

Then comes the devastating reminder: وَهُوَ فَضَّلَكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - "While He has preferred you over all the worlds" or "given you excellence over all nations." The word فَضَّلَ means to give preference, to grant special favor and distinction.

Which nation has crossed water like you did? Which nation has been rescued the way you were rescued without even having a standing army? Which nation has been given the miracles and signs you've witnessed? You've been given a level of divine favor that no other nation has experienced, and you want to worship something other than the One who gave you all of this?

This question should echo in every believer's heart when we're tempted by the attractions of the world. When we have Allah ﷻ, why would we seek anything else?

translation: And [recall, O Children of Israel], when We saved you from the people of Pharaoh, [who were] afflicting you with the worst torment - killing your sons and keeping your women alive. And in that was a great trial from your Lord
commentary:
**وَإِذْ أَنجَيْنَـٰكُم مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۖ يُقَتِّلُونَ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ وَيَسْتَحْيُونَ نِسَآءَكُمْ ۚ وَفِى ذَٰلِكُم بَلَآءٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ**

Musa (AS) reminds them of their recent history: وَإِذْ أَنجَيْنَـٰكُم - "And remember when We saved you." The phrase is powerful because it combines two speakers. These are the words of Musa (AS) speaking to his people, yet Allah ﷻ is saying "We saved you" - not "He saved you." This interweaving shows how completely the words of the prophet carry divine authority. When Musa (AS) reminds them of Allah's ﷻ favor, he speaks with Allah's ﷻ voice.

مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ - "From the people of Pharaoh." يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ - "Who were afflicting you with the worst torment." The word يَسُومُونَ means to inflict continuously, to subject someone to ongoing humiliation and suffering.

يُقَتِّلُونَ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ - "Slaughtering your sons." وَيَسْتَحْيُونَ نِسَآءَكُمْ - "And letting your women live." This is presented in the present tense - يُقَتِّلُونَ , يَسْتَحْيُونَ - creating a vivid, ongoing picture. It's as if they can still see it happening, still feel the horror of that systematic oppression.

وَفِى ذَٰلِكُم بَلَآءٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ - "And in that was a tremendous trial from your Lord." The word بَلَآءٌ is interesting because it means both a trial and a blessing. They were tested through their suffering, but surviving it became a blessing. The oppression itself was the trial, but being saved from it revealed Allah's ﷻ tremendous favor upon them.

This final reminder serves as the ultimate response to their request for idols: How can you forget so quickly? You were slaves being systematically destroyed, and Allah ﷻ saved you in the most miraculous way. Your sons were being killed, your women were being subjugated, and He delivered you. You crossed the sea, saw your oppressors drowned, and were given freedom. And now, just moments later, you want to worship something other than Him?

This passage speaks to all believers across time. When we forget Allah's ﷻ favors and turn toward the false attractions of this world, we need to be reminded: Look at what He has given you. Look at what He has saved you from. How can you seek anyone or anything besides Him?

translation: And We made an appointment with Moses for thirty nights and perfected them by [the addition of] ten; so the term of his Lord was completed as forty nights. And Moses said to his brother Aaron, "Take my place among my people, do right [by them], and do not follow the way of the corrupters
commentary:
وَوَٰعَدْنَا مُوسَىٰ ثَلَـٰثِينَ لَيْلَةً وَأَتْمَمْنَـٰهَا بِعَشْرٍ فَتَمَّ مِيقَـٰتُ رَبِّهِۦٓ أَرْبَعِينَ لَيْلَةً ۚ وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِأَخِيهِ هَـٰرُونَ ٱخْلُفْنِى فِى قَوْمِى وَأَصْلِحْ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ

Allah ﷻ appointed Musa (AS) for thirty nights, and then completed them with an additional ten, bringing the total appointment with his Master to forty nights. Before departing for this divinely appointed time, Musa (AS) gave clear instructions to his brother Harun (AS): "Stay behind among my people, do what is right, and do not follow the way of those who cause corruption."

The word اخْلُفْنِى means to remain behind as a deputy, to take charge in someone's absence. Musa (AS) wasn't simply asking his brother to babysit—he was appointing him as a leader with responsibility. The instruction وَأَصْلِحْ carries the meaning of "fix what needs fixing" and "maintain what is right." It's a comprehensive command that encompasses both correcting problems and preventing new ones from arising.

What's particularly insightful here is the final warning: وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ—"and do not follow the way of the troublemakers." The word المفسدين doesn't just refer to corrupt people in a passive sense, but to those who actively create problems and stir up trouble. This instruction reveals something important about leadership: a leader must be warned about specific individuals who will try to influence them negatively. It's not enough to say "be good"—sometimes you need to say "watch out for this person, and this person, and that person."

This is wise counsel that any leader leaving behind a deputy should give. Identify the potential sources of corruption and warn against them explicitly. Don't just hope your deputy figures it out on their own.

translation: And when Moses arrived at Our appointed time and his Lord spoke to him, he said, "My Lord, show me [Yourself] that I may look at You." [Allah] said, "You will not see Me, but look at the mountain; if it should remain in place, then you will see Me." But when his Lord appeared to the mountain, He rendered it level, and Moses fell unconscious. And when he awoke, he said, "Exalted are You! I have repented to You, and I am the first of the believers
commentary:
وَلَمَّا جَآءَ مُوسَىٰ لِمِيقَـٰتِنَا وَكَلَّمَهُۥ رَبُّهُۥ قَالَ رَبِّ أَرِنِىٓ أَنظُرْ إِلَيْكَ ۚ قَالَ لَن تَرَىٰنِى وَلَـٰكِنِ ٱنظُرْ إِلَى ٱلْجَبَلِ فَإِنِ ٱسْتَقَرَّ مَكَانَهُۥ فَسَوْفَ تَرَىٰنِى ۚ فَلَمَّا تَجَلَّىٰ رَبُّهُۥ لِلْجَبَلِ جَعَلَهُۥ دَكًّا وَخَرَّ مُوسَىٰ صَعِقًا ۚ فَلَمَّآ أَفَاقَ قَالَ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ وَأَنَا۠ أَوَّلُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

When Musa (AS) came to his appointment with Us and his Master spoke to him, something remarkable happened. Having already experienced the honor of direct conversation with Allah ﷻ multiple times, Musa (AS) had grown in confidence. He thought to himself: if I've been blessed to speak with Him, perhaps I can ask for something greater. So he made a bold request: رَبِّ أَرِنِىٓ أَنظُرْ إِلَيْكَ—"My Master, show me so I can look at You."

Notice the careful language Musa (AS) uses. He doesn't say "show me Yourself" directly. Instead, he says "show me so that I can look at You." The verb أَنظُرْ implies more than a casual glance—it suggests an intense, sustained gaze. Musa (AS) wasn't asking for a quick peek; he wanted to stare, to take in the sight completely, unable to look away.

Allah's ﷻ response was immediate and clear: لَن تَرَىٰنِى—"You will not see Me." The particle لَن indicates a definitive negation. It's not happening. But then Allah ﷻ offers an alternative demonstration: وَلَـٰكِنِ ٱنظُرْ إِلَى ٱلْجَبَلِ—"But look at the mountain." And then comes the condition: فَإِنِ ٱسْتَقَرَّ مَكَانَهُۥ فَسَوْفَ تَرَىٰنِى—"If it remains in its place, then you will see Me."

What happened next demonstrates the impossibility of the condition. فَلَمَّا تَجَلَّىٰ رَبُّهُۥ لِلْجَبَلِ—"When his Master manifested to the mountain." The word تَجَلَّىٰ means to reveal one's full glory, to be fully illuminated, to manifest completely. When Allah ﷻ manifested even a fraction of His glory to the mountain, جَعَلَهُۥ دَكًّا—He made it crushed, obliterated, reduced to dust. The mountain exploded. It couldn't handle even that much exposure to divine glory.

And Musa (AS), who had been staring at the mountain, وَخَرَّ مُوسَىٰ صَعِقًا—fell unconscious, struck down by what he witnessed. When he regained consciousness, his response was immediate repentance: سُبْحَـٰنَكَ تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ—"How perfect You are! I repent to You." He recognized that he had crossed a boundary in his eagerness to know more about Allah ﷻ.

Then he added something profound: وَأَنَا۠ أَوَّلُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ—"And I am the first of the believers." Why is this statement significant here? Because it completes an earlier conversation. Musa (AS) is essentially saying: "I don't need to see You to believe in You. My faith doesn't depend on sight." This is the ultimate submission—believing without seeing, trusting without physical proof.

This ayah provides a powerful answer to those who demand, "Why can't we see Allah ﷻ?" The answer is not just that it's prohibited or that Allah ﷻ chooses not to reveal Himself. The answer is that we couldn't handle it. Even the most sincere believer, even a prophet of the stature of Musa (AS), cannot withstand the direct manifestation of Allah's ﷻ glory in this life. The mountain itself, solid and massive, was reduced to dust. What chance would a human being have?

translation: [Allah] said, "O Moses, I have chosen you over the people with My messages and My words [to you]. So take what I have given you and be among the grateful
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنِّى ٱصْطَفَيْتُكَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ بِرِسَـٰلَـٰتِى وَبِكَلَـٰمِى فَخُذْ مَآ ءَاتَيْتُكَ وَكُن مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ

After this humbling experience, Allah ﷻ reassures Musa (AS) of his special status: "O Musa, I have chosen you over all people with My messages and with My speech." The word ٱصْطَفَيْتُكَ emphasizes divine selection—"I have specially chosen you, without doubt."

Notice the dual distinction: بِرِسَـٰلَـٰتِى وَبِكَلَـٰمِى—"with My messages and with My speech." The plural رِسَـٰلَـٰتِى indicates that Musa (AS) would not receive just one message, but multiple revelations. Allah ﷻ would give him various instructions: what kind of society to establish, what to say to his people, what commands to deliver to Pharaoh. This wasn't a one-time communication but an ongoing conversation.

And then there's the special title: وَبِكَلَـٰمِى—"and with My speech." The word كَلَـٰمِ (kalam) refers to thorough, engaged conversation. This is why Musa (AS) is known as Kalimullah—the one who conversed with Allah ﷻ. It's a unique honor, a special designation that sets him apart.

After affirming this honored status, Allah ﷻ gives a simple instruction: فَخُذْ مَآ ءَاتَيْتُكَ—"So take what I have given you." Stop asking for more. Be content with what you've been blessed with. وَكُن مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ—"And be from those who are deeply grateful."

This is a lesson for all of us. Sometimes we're so focused on what we don't have, on what we wish we could experience, that we fail to appreciate the tremendous blessings we've already been given. Musa (AS) had already been granted the extraordinary honor of speaking with Allah ﷻ directly. That should have been enough. When we find ourselves constantly wanting more, always feeling dissatisfied, we need to pause and recognize the ingratitude in that attitude. Being among الشَّـٰكِرِينَ—the deeply grateful—means recognizing that what we have is already extraordinary.

translation: And We wrote for him on the tablets [something] of all things - instruction and explanation for all things, [saying], "Take them with determination and order your people to take the best of it. I will show you the home of the defiantly disobedient
commentary:
وَكَتَبْنَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْأَلْوَاحِ مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مَّوْعِظَةً وَتَفْصِيلًا لِّكُلِّ شَىْءٍ فَخُذْهَا بِقُوَّةٍ وَأْمُرْ قَوْمَكَ يَأْخُذُوا۟ بِأَحْسَنِهَا ۚ سَأُو۟رِيكُمْ دَارَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ

Allah ﷻ revealed His guidance to Musa (AS) in a unique form: carved into stone tablets. The word كَتَبْنَا literally means "we wrote," but in this context, it refers to engraving, carving into stone. The Arabic language has another word for carving—حَفْر—but here the engraving is called كِتَابَة because the carved inscriptions themselves are also called نَقْش in classical Arabic.

What was written in these tablets? مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ —"of everything." Does this mean Allah ﷻ gave Musa (AS) nuclear codes, advanced architectural engineering, and the secrets of the universe? No. This is language, not science. When we say "everything," we mean everything relevant, everything needed for the purpose at hand. It's like when a husband tells his wife, "I gave you everything," he doesn't mean he's given her every car on the planet. He means he's given her everything she needs, everything important.

Similarly, when Allah ﷻ says He wrote "everything" in the tablets, He means everything Musa (AS) and his people needed for guidance. The ayah clarifies this: مَّوْعِظَةً—"good counsel, heart-penetrating advice," وَتَفْصِيلًا لِّكُلِّ شَىْءٍ—"and a thorough explanation of everything" they needed to live a good life according to divine guidance.

Then comes a crucial instruction: فَخُذْهَا بِقُوَّةٍ—"Take it with strength, with determination." Don't approach this revelation halfheartedly. Grab onto it firmly. وَأْمُرْ قَوْمَكَ يَأْخُذُوا۟ بِأَحْسَنِهَا—"And command your people to take the best of it."

What does it mean to take "the best" of something? Think of a teacher who assigns a one-page essay. One student writes single-spaced, font size 10, filling every bit of that page with quality content. Another student writes triple-spaced, font size 18, doing the bare minimum to technically fulfill the assignment. Both submitted a one-page essay, but one took the instruction and knocked it out of the park while the other just scraped by.

Taking the best of Allah's ﷻ guidance means going beyond the minimum. It means not asking, "What's the least I can do and still be considered Muslim?" but rather, "How can I excel in this? How can I do this better than anyone else?" Every instruction in our religion has grades—you can get an A, B, C, D, or F in how you fulfill it. Allah ﷻ is telling us to aim for the A, to follow His guidance with excellence, not mere compliance.

Then Allah ﷻ makes a promise: سَأُو۟رِيكُمْ دَارَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ—"I will show you the house of the corrupt." In other words, when they hold onto this book and follow it with excellence, they will be ready for bigger challenges. What's that bigger challenge? Confronting the tyrannical nations, the physically imposing enemies that seem unbeatable.

This teaches us something profound about our religion. When you do the best you can with the daily things in your faith—your prayer, your character, your relationships, your honesty—then you become ready for the bigger challenges. There's a lot of work to be done for this ummah, but we can't do it until we're excelling at the foundational things first. You have to master the basics before Allah ﷻ gives you the bigger tasks. The Israelites later refused to fight, saying, "You and your Master go fight; we'll wait here." Why? Because they hadn't followed the best of what Allah ﷻ had given them before, so they didn't have the courage to follow harder instructions afterward.

translation: I will turn away from My signs those who are arrogant upon the earth without right; and if they should see every sign, they will not believe in it. And if they see the way of consciousness, they will not adopt it as a way; but if they see the way of error, they will adopt it as a way. That is because they have denied Our signs and they were heedless of them
commentary:
سَأَصْرِفُ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَكَبَّرُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ كُلَّ ءَايَةٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهَا وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ سَبِيلَ ٱلرُّشْدِ لَا يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلًا وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ سَبِيلَ ٱلْغَىِّ يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَكَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا غَـٰفِلِينَ

After describing what happens to those who follow His guidance with excellence, Allah ﷻ now describes the opposite end of the spectrum. سَأَصْرِفُ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِىَ—"I will turn away from My signs." Who gets turned away? ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَكَبَّرُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ —"Those who seek to be big on the earth without justification, who walk around with arrogance without any right to do so."

This is a severe warning. You'll find people who say, "I'm not convinced about Islam. Prove it to me. Show me the evidence." And you bring them proof after proof, miracle after miracle, and they respond with, "Yeah, but... that's not that impressive. I'm not convinced." What's really happening? It's not an evidence problem—it's an arrogance problem. They want to be impressed. They want Islam to market itself to them like a product, like a fancy shoe commercial.

For such people, Allah ﷻ says: No, it's not that they don't believe. I won't let them believe. I will turn them away from My signs. You think you can bring that attitude of arrogance to Me? You don't deserve guidance. Allah ﷻ describes their condition: وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ كُلَّ ءَايَةٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهَا—"Even if they saw every single miraculous sign, they would not believe in it."

But then notice how the ayah shifts from belief to action. وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ سَبِيلَ ٱلرُّشْدِ لَا يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلًا—"If they see the path of righteousness, they will not take it as a path." The word رُشْد means guidance, the right direction. And conversely, وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ سَبِيلَ ٱلْغَىِّ يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلًا—"If they see the path of deviation, they will take it as a path."

The word غَىّ means to be lost, to take the wrong road thinking it's the right one and keep going until you realize you've ended up in completely the wrong place—like heading for Dallas and ending up in Mexico. Interestingly, غَىّ is also the name of one of the levels of Hellfire, specifically for those who were lost and never turned back.

Here's what's crucial to understand: Allah ﷻ isn't just describing people who won't believe. That's one problem. But He's also describing a problem of action—taking a road or not taking a road is not merely a matter of belief, it's a matter of what you do. So Allah ﷻ is saying that not only will they not believe, but the proof of their disbelief shows up in their actions. When they see the right path, they won't take it. When they see the wrong path, they will take it.

A person might even lie to themselves and think they believe. They might claim to have faith. But where's the proof? It's in the actions. And if someone refuses to examine their own actions, if they refuse to consider that they might be on the wrong path, then that refusal itself is proof of arrogance. And if that's the case, then Allah ﷻ has turned them away from His signs.

This is not just a problem for disbelievers. This is a problem that you and I need to worry about. Just because we're Muslim, just because we're studying Quran, doesn't make us immune to this disease of arrogance. Anyone can have this problem. The son of a scholar can be completely empty inside. You could be going through the motions of prayer without any actual prayer happening. Allah ﷻ can be addressed in worship both with humility and with arrogance. You can have a person praying who has spiritual arrogance corrupting their prayer, who isn't actually connecting with Allah ﷻ at all.

This is why the Quran emphasizes: اُدْعُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ تَضَرُّعًا وَخُفْيَةً—"Call upon your Master, humbling yourself and secretly" (7:55). If we're not actually conversing with Allah ﷻ in our prayer, if we're not humbling ourselves before Him, what's happening? Heedlessness, غَفْلَة.

Imagine you're in the second rak'ah of prayer thinking, "What time does the game start? Oh no, first quarter is already over." Or you're in Salah and you're mentally putting together Arabic sentences for your exam: "Ana... adh-habu... ila... al-madrasa." That's not prayer. That's heedlessness. If you're not connecting with Allah ﷻ, if you're not present with Him, then eventually a really ugly state occurs: you become extremely knowledgeable on the outside while being completely empty on the inside. This warning is coming up soon in this very surah—the example of someone with tremendous knowledge but no spiritual substance, no character, nothing going on inside despite all that learning.

The ayah concludes by explaining why this happens: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا—"That is because they lied against Our signs." But notice where it actually began: وَكَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا غَـٰفِلِينَ—"And they were heedless of them."

So there are two problems mentioned here: arrogance (كِبْر) and heedlessness (غَفْلَة). The ayah begins with arrogance but ends with heedlessness. This shows us the progression—it often starts with heedlessness, with not paying attention, with not being present. That ghaflah eventually develops into full-blown arrogance and rejection. The end stage is lying against Allah's ﷻ signs, but it begins with simply being absent, disconnected, going through the motions without consciousness of what we're doing.

translation: Those who denied Our signs and the meeting of the Hereafter - their deeds have become worthless. Are they recompensed except for what they used to do
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَلِقَآءِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ ۚ هَلْ يُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

Those who denied Our signs and the meeting of the Hereafter—حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ —all their deeds have become worthless, nullified, void. The word حَبِطَ means to become null and void, to lose all value. What's interesting is that the word أَعْمَال is typically used in the Quran for good deeds. Allah ﷻ often says صَالِحَ الأَعْمَال—righteous deeds.

This raises an important question that many people struggle with today, especially Muslim college students: "But he does so many good things! He's a humanitarian. He volunteers. He helps people. Does that mean just because he doesn't believe, all his good deeds are gone?" Or "She's Hindu, but she's such a good person. Are you saying all her goodness doesn't count?"

First of all, we need to stop being obsessed with deciding who's going to Hell and who isn't. That's not our job. We have bigger problems to worry about—mainly ourselves, our own spiritual state, our own relationship with Allah ﷻ. Second, what happens to people's deeds is what Allah ﷻ says, not what I decide. It's not me who has the right to make that judgment. That authority belongs to Allah ﷻ alone.

Think about why people were offended by what the Prophet ﷺ was saying. They said, "What, you think we're going to Hell? Who are you to judge?" And the Prophet's ﷺ response was essentially: "I'm not judging you. This is what Allah ﷻ said. I'm just delivering the message. And it's not just about you—I'm in danger too if I don't tell you what Allah ﷻ says."

Unfortunately, religion has often been used as a tool for projecting self-righteousness: "We're saved, you're doomed." But from the very beginning, the Prophet ﷺ said, "I fear Allah ﷻ, and I call you to fear Him too." He would advise his companions: "I counsel myself and you to have taqwa of Allah ﷻ." Neither of us is automatically safe. We both need to work on saving ourselves. That's the proper attitude of this religion.

When religion becomes about "I'm better than you, you're going to Hell, you should be more like me," that's when people rightfully get turned away from it. And we need to understand why that happens and avoid that self-righteous attitude ourselves.

The ayah ends with a rhetorical question: هَلْ يُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ—"Will they be compensated with anything except what they used to do?" Everyone gets what they worked for. The compensation matches the deeds. That's Allah's ﷻ perfect justice.

translation: And the people of Moses made, after [his departure], from their ornaments a calf - an image having a lowing sound. Did they not see that it could neither speak to them nor guide them to a way? They took it [for worship], and they were wrongdoers
commentary:
وَٱتَّخَذَ قَوْمُ مُوسَىٰ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ مِنْ حُلِيِّهِمْ عِجْلًا جَسَدًا لَّهُۥ خُوَارٌ ۚ أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّهُۥ لَا يُكَلِّمُهُمْ وَلَا يَهْدِيهِمْ سَبِيلًا ۘ ٱتَّخَذُوهُ وَكَانُوا۟ ظَـٰلِمِينَ

After Musa (AS) left for his forty-day appointment with Allah ﷻ, something terrible happened among his people. وَٱتَّخَذَ قَوْمُ مُوسَىٰ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ—"The people of Musa, after he was gone, took..." Notice the phrasing: مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ—after he had left, after they made sure he was gone. They waited. They were scared to do this while he was around.

مِنْ حُلِيِّهِمْ عِجْلًا جَسَدًا لَّهُۥ خُوَارٌ—"From their jewelry, a calf, a body that had a mooing sound." Samiri, the one who led this corruption, collected everyone's gold jewelry and fashioned it into a calf. But not just any statue—he engineered it in such a way that when wind blew through it, it would produce a sound, a lowing noise like a cow makes: خُوَارٌ.

The image of the calf, the bull, the cow in idol worship is fascinating and disturbing. There are studies now showing the use of cow imagery in satanic cults. Many dark magic traditions, including corrupted Kabbalistic practices that developed in deviant Jewish mysticism, incorporate cow symbolism and bull imagery. This isn't new—it seems to be an ancient form of idol worship that predates even the Israelites. On another spectrum, you have the Hindu tradition where the cow is considered sacred, where some beliefs hold that the earth revolves on the horns of a bull. The prevalence of cow worship across different corrupt religious traditions is striking—and may trace back to very ancient roots.

When the people saw this golden calf that seemed to "speak" (really just wind whistling through it), they started falling down before it, worshiping it, doing all manner of foolish things. أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّهُۥ لَا يُكَلِّمُهُمْ وَلَا يَهْدِيهِمْ سَبِيلًا—"Didn't they see that it doesn't actually talk to them and doesn't guide them to any path?"

Think about this rationally for a moment. What did this calf say? Did it give them a sermon? Did it provide guidance? Did it communicate anything meaningful whatsoever? No. It made a mooing sound. That's it. Yet they took it as their god. ٱتَّخَذُوهُ—"They took it," they adopted it as an object of worship. وَكَانُوا۟ ظَـٰلِمِينَ—"And they were wrongdoers," they were doing wrong to themselves.

The word ظَـٰلِمِينَ means those who wrong themselves, who harm themselves. This is the nature of shirk, of associating partners with Allah ﷻ—it is fundamentally self-destructive. You're not hurting Allah ﷻ when you commit shirk; you're destroying yourself.

translation: And when regret overcame them and they saw that they had gone astray, they said, "If our Lord does not have mercy upon us and forgive us, we will surely be among the losers
commentary:
وَلَمَّا سُقِطَ فِىٓ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَرَأَوْا۟ أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ قَالُوا۟ لَئِن لَّمْ يَرْحَمْنَا رَبُّنَا وَيَغْفِرْ لَنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ

وَلَمَّا سُقِطَ فِىٓ أَيْدِيهِمْ—this is a beautiful, evocative Arabic expression that literally means "when it fell into their hands." It's a figure of speech describing that moment of sudden, horrified realization.
