You know that feeling when you're holding something carelessly and it slips from your grasp, and the instant it falls you think, "Oh no, what have I done?" That sick feeling in your stomach when you realize the magnitude of your mistake.

That's what happened to some of these people. They suddenly snapped out of it. They woke up from their spiritual stupor and realized: "What have we been doing? How could we have done this?" Some people came back to their senses. They were no longer in a state of heedlessness (غَفْلَة). They became aware.

وَرَأَوْا۟ أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟—"And they saw that they had truly gone astray, that they had been lost." This is the moment of genuine recognition of one's own error. Not making excuses, not justifying, not rationalizing—just pure acknowledgment: we messed up. We went astray.

And what was their response? Immediate, desperate supplication: قَالُوا۟ لَئِن لَّمْ يَرْحَمْنَا رَبُّنَا وَيَغْفِرْ لَنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ—"They said: If our Master does not have mercy on us and forgive us, we will surely be among the ultimate losers."

Notice the structure of their plea. They don't say, "We're good people, we deserve forgiveness." They don't say, "We didn't mean it, so it shouldn't count." They don't make excuses or minimize what they did. Instead, they throw themselves entirely on Allah's ﷻ mercy: "If You don't show us mercy, if You don't forgive us, we're finished. We're lost. We'll be among those who have lost everything."

This is the door of repentance. This is what tawbah looks like—recognizing your error, feeling genuine remorse, and turning to Allah ﷻ knowing that you have no claim on His forgiveness except His own mercy. You can't demand it. You can't earn it through your own righteousness. You can only hope for it, pray for it, and throw yourself at His door knowing that He is الرَّحْمَـٰن, الرَّحِيم—the Most Merciful, the Especially Merciful.

These ayahs present us with a complete picture: the honor of being chosen for divine communication, the limits of human capacity to experience Allah's ﷻ glory in this life, the importance of taking revelation with excellence rather than bare minimum compliance, the danger of arrogance and heedlessness that turns people away from guidance, the self-destructive nature of shirk, and finally, the ever-open door of repentance for those who snap out of their heedlessness and return to their Master with genuine remorse.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from spiritual arrogance and heedlessness, and make us among those who hold onto His guidance with strength and excellence, and who return to Him quickly.

translation: And when Moses returned to his people, angry and grieved, he said, "How wretched is that by which you have replaced me after [my departure]. Were you impatient over the matter of your Lord?" And he threw down the tablets and seized his brother by [the hair of] his head, pulling him toward him. [Aaron] said, "O son of my mother, indeed the people oppressed me and were about to kill me, so let not the enemies rejoice over me and do not place me among the wrongdoing people
commentary:
وَلَمَّا رَجَعَ مُوسَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ غَضْبَـٰنَ أَسِفًا قَالَ بِئْسَمَا خَلَفْتُمُونِى مِنۢ بَعْدِىٓ ۖ أَعَجِلْتُمْ أَمْرَ رَبِّكُمْ ۖ وَأَلْقَى ٱلْأَلْوَاحَ وَأَخَذَ بِرَأْسِ أَخِيهِ يَجُرُّهُۥٓ إِلَيْهِ ۚ قَالَ ٱبْنَ أُمَّ إِنَّ ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱسْتَضْعَفُونِى وَكَادُوا۟ يَقْتُلُونَنِى فَلَا تُشْمِتْ بِىَ ٱلْأَعْدَآءَ وَلَا تَجْعَلْنِى مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

The ayah opens with a striking description: when Musa (AS) returned to his people, he was already غَضْبَـٰنَ أَسِفًا (angry and deeply upset). The language here is significant. The state of anger and distress wasn't something that developed upon his arrival—it was the condition in which he returned. Allah ﷻ had already informed him of what transpired in his absence, and as he made the journey back, his emotions intensified with each step.

Consider the context: Musa (AS) had been so eager to enhance his relationship with Allah ﷻ that he arrived early for their appointed meeting. He thought this would please his Lord, but instead Allah ﷻ scolded him for leaving his nation without proper supervision. Then came the devastating news that his people had fallen into worship of the calf. The longer the journey back took, the more his thoughts consumed him—like being stuck in traffic with your mind racing through what went wrong.

When he finally arrived, his first words cut to the core: "How horrible is what you've done in my absence! Did you rush to the command of your Master?" This rebuke reveals something profound. The people had been instructed to wait for guidance on how to worship Allah ﷻ properly. Musa (AS) was to return with those very instructions on the tablets. But they couldn't wait. They demanded, "Can we worship Allah like these people with their statue?" The answer was no—Allah ﷻ had appointed a specific time and would provide specific instructions. Yet they rushed ahead anyway.

In his fury, Musa (AS) threw down the tablets. Then, in a moment of raw emotion, he grabbed his brother Harun (AS) by the head and dragged him forward. The intensity of this action shows how deeply the betrayal affected him—his own brother, whom he had left in charge, seemed to have failed in his duty.

Harun's (AS) response is equally revealing. He doesn't say "Ya Akhi" (O my brother). Instead, he desperately calls out ٱبْنَ أُمَّ (son of my mother). This unusual address invokes their shared mother—a woman who had already sacrificed so much for Musa (AS), who had placed him in the river with complete trust in Allah ﷻ. It's as if Harun (AS) is saying, "Remember our mother. Remember our bond. Calm down and listen to me."

The word ٱبْنَ is in the accusative case (mansub), which indicates he's being called to attention—there's urgency here, not the casual "hey" you'd use in normal conversation. Harun (AS) quickly explains: "The people weakened me, they held me back. They almost killed me for trying to stop them."

Then comes his plea: فَلَا تُشْمِتْ بِىَ ٱلْأَعْدَآءَ (Don't let the enemies rejoice over me). The word إِشْمَات refers to that particular kind of happiness people feel when watching someone else fail or fall—the schadenfreude that comes from another's humiliation. Harun (AS) is essentially saying, "This is an internal matter. Don't handle it this way in front of them. Don't give them the satisfaction of watching us fight." Notice he even calls the people who fell into shirk "the enemies"—once they committed shirk, they ceased being simply "our people" and became enemies of the truth.

His final words are critical: "And don't place me with the wrongdoing nation." He's distancing himself completely from their actions, making it clear he had no part in their deviation.

translation: [Moses] said, "My Lord, forgive me and my brother and admit us into Your mercy, for You are the most merciful of the merciful
commentary:
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱغْفِرْ لِى وَلِأَخِى وَأَدْخِلْنَا فِى رَحْمَتِكَ ۖ وَأَنتَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ

Musa's (AS) immediate response after this confrontation is to turn to Allah ﷻ in supplication. What's remarkable about this dua is what it doesn't include. He says, "My Master, forgive me and my brother." But notice—he doesn't ask forgiveness for those who worshiped the calf. Why? Because he knows you cannot ask forgiveness for shirk on behalf of others. That's a line only they themselves can cross by making sincere repentance. There's no point in making dua for something Allah ﷻ has made clear will not be forgiven without personal repentance.

Instead, Musa (AS) asks to be admitted into Allah's mercy, both himself and Harun (AS). The closing phrase is powerful: وَأَنتَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ (And You are the Most Merciful of those who show mercy). He's acknowledging that while humans can show mercy to one another, Allah's mercy infinitely surpasses all of it.

translation: Indeed, those who took the calf [for worship] will obtain anger from their Lord and humiliation in the life of this world, and thus do We recompense the inventors [of falsehood]
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ ٱلْعِجْلَ سَيَنَالُهُمْ غَضَبٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَذِلَّةٌ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُفْتَرِينَ

Now Allah ﷻ directly addresses the consequence for those who took the calf as an object of worship. Two punishments are decreed: anger from their Lord will reach them, and humiliation in this worldly life. The word ذِلَّةٌ refers to a state of being brought low, of being disgraced. This isn't just a personal feeling—it's a social reality that manifests in how they're perceived and treated by others.

The ayah concludes with a universal principle: وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُفْتَرِينَ (And that is how We compensate those who fabricate lies in matters of religion). This extends beyond the Bani Israel. Anyone who invents falsehoods about Allah ﷻ, who adds to or distorts the religion, will face similar consequences. The humiliation in this life becomes a preview of what awaits in the next.

translation: But those who committed misdeeds and then repented after them and believed - indeed your Lord, thereafter, is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ثُمَّ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِهَا وَءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهَا لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

This ayah provides essential balance to the previous warning. Yes, those who worshiped the calf will face consequences—but the door to repentance remains open. Allah ﷻ describes those who did terrible wrongs (السَّيِّـَٔاتِ), then repented after them, and truly believed again.

This passage addresses a common phenomenon we see today, particularly on college campuses. You have students who grew up Muslim but weren't deeply practicing. They encounter the ultra-religious crowd at the MSA who immediately start commanding good and forbidding evil before even saying salam. These newcomers feel judged and unwelcome, so they drift away entirely—joining other student clubs, abandoning Islamic practices, sometimes falling into serious sins.

Then a friend approaches them with genuine concern, not judgment: "Bro, you're Muslim. You shouldn't be doing this." And the response? "It's too late for me. I'm already too far down this road. You don't know what I've done." They allow their past sins to define their future. They've written themselves off.

But consider what Allah ﷻ says here about the worshipers of the calf. You cannot get much worse than shirk—especially shirk committed by people who had been taught tawhid by a prophet, who had witnessed Allah's power with their own eyes multiple times. These weren't ignorant pagans who didn't know any better. These were the companions of a prophet who had seen miracle after miracle, yet they still chose to worship a golden statue. If anyone had the right to say "there's no hope for me," it would be them.

Yet Allah ﷻ declares: even after that level of sin, those who repented and came back to true faith—your Master after all that is غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (Extremely Forgiving, Always Merciful). He doesn't close His doors on people. People close doors on Allah ﷻ, but He never closes His door on them.

translation: And when the anger subsided in Moses, he took up the tablets; and in their inscription was guidance and mercy for those who are fearful of their Lord
commentary:
وَلَمَّا سَكَتَ عَن مُّوسَى ٱلْغَضَبُ أَخَذَ ٱلْأَلْوَاحَ ۖ وَفِى نُسْخَتِهَا هُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِرَبِّهِمْ يَرْهَبُونَ

When the anger finally subsided and left Musa (AS)—when it calmed down and released its grip on him—he picked up the tablets again. The phrasing سَكَتَ عَن مُّوسَى (the anger became silent from Musa) is beautifully poetic. It's as if the anger had been speaking loudly, drowning out everything else, and now it finally went quiet.

He grabbed the tablets because their inscriptions contained هُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ (guidance and mercy) for those who truly have fear and reverence for their Master. This picks up the narrative after a critical event that occurred between the ayahs—an event detailed in Surah Al-Baqarah. Those who had committed the sin of worshiping the calf and refused to repent had to be executed by their own tribespeople. This was the first command. Only after this purification could the nation move forward to seek collective forgiveness.

translation: And Moses chose from his people seventy men for Our appointment. And when the earthquake seized them, he said, "My Lord, if You had willed, You could have destroyed them before and me [as well]. Would You destroy us for what the foolish among us have done? This is not but Your trial by which You send astray whom You will and guide whom You will. You are our Protector, so forgive us and have mercy upon us; and You are the best of forgivers
commentary:
وَٱخْتَارَ مُوسَىٰ قَوْمَهُۥ سَبْعِينَ رَجُلًا لِّمِيقَـٰتِنَا ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ قَالَ رَبِّ لَوْ شِئْتَ أَهْلَكْتَهُم مِّن قَبْلُ وَإِيَّـٰىَ ۖ أَتُهْلِكُنَا بِمَا فَعَلَ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ مِنَّآ ۖ إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا فِتْنَتُكَ تُضِلُّ بِهَا مَن تَشَآءُ وَتَهْدِى مَن تَشَآءُ ۖ أَنتَ وَلِيُّنَا فَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَٱرْحَمْنَا ۖ وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْغَـٰفِرِينَ

After the executions, Musa (AS) selected seventy leaders from among his people for an appointed meeting with Allah ﷻ. This was to be the official collective repentance before Allah ﷻ—not just the execution of criminals, but a formal acknowledgment of what had happened and a seeking of divine forgiveness.

But when they arrived at Mount Tur, something terrifying occurred. A violent earthquake seized them—الرَّجْفَةُ, a shaking so intense that boulders began falling from the mountain. These seventy men had come to do something righteous, to make tawbah, yet they were met with this display of Allah's anger.

Musa (AS) cried out in desperation: "My Master, if You had wanted, You could have destroyed them long ago—and me too!" He's saying, "Why now? Why these particular people who are here trying to make things right?" Then comes his anguished question: أَتُهْلِكُنَا بِمَا فَعَلَ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ مِنَّآ (Are You destroying us because of what the foolish ones among us did?). He calls them السُّفَهَآءُ (the foolish, the idiots, the senseless ones)—strong language, even though those individuals had already been executed. He's still expressing his frustration with them.

Then Musa (AS) says something profound: إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا فِتْنَتُكَ (This is nothing but Your test). Now, this might sound strange—is he teaching Allah ﷻ something? No. He's actually repeating back what Allah ﷻ had already told him in a previous conversation documented in another surah. Allah ﷻ had described this entire episode as a trial, a test. Musa (AS) is acknowledging this reality: You test people, You lead astray through these tests those who deserve it, and You guide through them those who deserve guidance.

His supplication concludes with beautiful submission: أَنتَ وَلِيُّنَا (You are our Protector, our Guardian, our Friend). Based on that relationship, "So forgive us and show us mercy." Notice the ending here: وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْغَـٰفِرِينَ (And You are the best of those who forgive). Why didn't he end with "Most Merciful" like before? Because mercy comes after forgiveness. Right now, at this moment of crisis, they need forgiveness first. The mercy will follow, but forgiveness is the immediate need.

translation: And decree for us in this world [that which is] good and [also] in the Hereafter; indeed, we have turned back to You." [Allah] said, "My punishment - I afflict with it whom I will, but My mercy encompasses all things." So I will decree it [especially] for those who fear Me and give zakah and those who believe in Our verses
commentary:
وَٱكْتُبْ لَنَا فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِنَّا هُدْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ۚ قَالَ عَذَابِىٓ أُصِيبُ بِهِۦ مَنْ أَشَآءُ ۖ وَرَحْمَتِى وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ ۚ فَسَأَكْتُبُهَا لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

Musa (AS) continues his supplication: "Write for us good in this world and in the Hereafter." What does "good in this world" mean in this specific context? Keep in mind where they are and what they're asking for. They came seeking guidance on how to worship Allah ﷻ properly. So the good of this world they're requesting is: don't let us fall away from Your teachings. Keep us on the straight path. Grant us the ability to maintain what we're about to learn. And as a result of that worldly good, guarantee us the goodness of the afterlife as well.

He concludes: إِنَّا هُدْنَآ إِلَيْكَ (We have turned back to You). This is the root from which the word يَهُودِيّ (Jewish) derives—those who turn back to God. It's a beautiful term expressing loyalty and love: we are coming back to You, returning to Your guidance with complete devotion.

Allah's response is striking in its structure. Musa (AS) asked for mercy and forgiveness. Allah ﷻ responds: عَذَابِىٓ أُصِيبُ بِهِۦ مَنْ أَشَآءُ (My punishment—I target with it whoever I want). Then He says something that requires careful attention: وَرَحْمَتِى وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ (And My mercy encompasses everything).

There are two types of mercy here. The general mercy of Allah ﷻ extends to all of creation—Muslim, non-Muslim, human, animal, everything. Every creature benefits from Allah's general mercy: the rain that falls, the air we breathe, the sustenance we receive, the functioning of our bodies. But Musa (AS) was asking for something more specific—the special mercy that leads to eternal salvation.

Allah ﷻ clarifies: فَسَأَكْتُبُهَا (So I will write it)—this special mercy—لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ (for those who have taqwa), وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ (who give zakah), وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (and those who truly believe in Our signs).

Here's something fascinating: a brother once visited a synagogue in Minnesota and was welcomed to look at their religious texts. He picked up a copy of the Torah and found eight instances of the word "maktuban" (written/prescribed) referring to various commandments. He immediately thought of the famous Quranic ayah about fasting being "maktuban" (prescribed) for us as it was for those before us. He approached the rabbi and asked, "Did you know your book tells you over and over to give zakah?" The rabbi simply replied, "Yeah," and that was the end of the conversation.

But Allah ﷻ says here: those who will receive My special mercy are those who believe in My signs. However, the description doesn't end there—and this is what we must pay close attention to.

translation: Those who follow the Messenger, the unlettered prophet, whom they find written in what they have of the Torah and the Gospel, who enjoins upon them what is right and forbids them what is wrong and makes lawful for them the good things and prohibits for them the evil and relieves them of their burden and the shackles which were upon them. So they who have believed in him, honored him, supported him and followed the light which was sent down with him - it is those who will be the successful
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ ٱلرَّسُولَ ٱلنَّبِىَّ ٱلْأُمِّىَّ ٱلَّذِى يَجِدُونَهُۥ مَكْتُوبًا عِندَهُمْ فِى ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَٱلْإِنجِيلِ يَأْمُرُهُم بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَىٰهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيُحِلُّ لَهُمُ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ وَيُحَرِّمُ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْخَبَـٰٓئِثَ وَيَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَٱلْأَغْلَـٰلَ ٱلَّتِى كَانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَعَزَّرُوهُ وَنَصَرُوهُ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلنُّورَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ مَعَهُۥٓ ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ

Remember: this conversation is happening at Mount Tur. When the Bani Israel came to make their collective repentance, Allah ﷻ told them He would give His special mercy to those who have taqwa, who give zakah, and who believe in His signs. But then He added: ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ ٱلرَّسُولَ ٱلنَّبِىَّ ٱلْأُمِّىَّ (Those who will follow the Messenger, the Prophet who is unlettered).

This is remarkable. They were being told right there at the mountain: you must believe in and follow Muhammad ﷺ when he comes. The one ٱلَّذِى يَجِدُونَهُۥ مَكْتُوبًا عِندَهُمْ فِى ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَٱلْإِنجِيلِ (whom they will find written about in their Torah) and in another revelation still to come (the Injil). They were being informed of this from the very beginning.

Think of it like climbing a mountain. The Torah was a milestone on that climb, and the Injil would be another milestone. But these are not the summit—they're points along the way. The ultimate goal, the peak of the mountain, is Muhammad ﷺ, the Messenger of Allah. They were told: you have guidance now to get started, but the final, complete guidance comes through following him.

The description of this prophet is comprehensive. يَأْمُرُهُم بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ (He will command them to everything known to be decent and good), وَيَنْهَىٰهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ (and he will forbid them from all things evil and wrong). وَيُحِلُّ لَهُمُ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ (He will make permissible for them all good and pure things), وَيُحَرِّمُ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْخَبَـٰٓئِثَ (and he will forbid them from all things filthy and harmful).

Then comes something profound: وَيَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَٱلْأَغْلَـٰلَ ٱلَّتِى كَانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ (And he will remove from them their burdens and the chains that were upon them). The word إِصْر refers to heavy loads—like the massive construction materials slaves had to carry on their backs and drag across distances. الْأَغْلَـٰل are chains and bands around the neck. Both are images of an oppressed slave weighed down and shackled.

What are these burdens and chains? Several things. When religion becomes corrupted over time, people add innovations they think are good. But these additions end up making life miserable and creating endless complications. We see this in the Muslim world today—and even in Muslim communities here in the West.

Consider marriage. What have we done? We've created impossible standards about wedding ceremonies, expenditures, venues, catering, hotel star-ratings, guest accommodations, gifts between families, mahr amounts that equal half a country's GDP. We've made it so complicated that people can barely afford to get married according to "proper" religious standards—standards we invented ourselves! Then the Messenger ﷺ comes with pristine Islam and teaches simplicity: two witnesses, a simple agreement, some modest food shared with guests. Done. No complications.

How easy is it for someone to become Muslim in Islam? Extremely easy. You don't need a ceremony, an event, a crowd. You don't have to wait until Friday prayer to make it official in front of a thousand people. But we've added layers: "You should change your name—that's not a Muslim name. We need to have a proper shahada ceremony. Let me find you a good Islamic name." Meanwhile, Umar (RA) kept his name—a Roman name. Salman kept his name—a Persian name. Mark, an Irish brother, goes to the imam: "I want to become Muslim." The imam says, "What's your name?" "Mark." "You need to change that to something Arabic." Mark looks up his name's meaning: it means "warrior" in Celtic Irish. The imam insists he needs a proper "Muslim" name. This is a burden we've added that Islam never required.

Beyond names, there are chains of oppression in how societies function: racism, nationalism, tribalism, greed-driven economics, unfair social laws, unjust family structures. This deen came to remove all these chains and give people a genuinely good life built on justice and mercy.

The ayah concludes with the conditions for success: فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِهِۦ (Those who believe in him), وَعَزَّرُوهُ (and honor him with respect). The word عَزَّرُوهُ is beautiful—it refers to going out of your way to show respect. When a teacher or scholar enters, and people rush to take his bags, offer him a seat, do things for him before he even asks—that's ta'zir. It's proactive respect done out of genuine honor.

This was specifically mentioned to the Bani Israel because they had a serious problem with respecting prophets. They barely showed minimal respect even to Musa (AS), the prophet who did the most for them. So now they're being told: when the final Messenger comes, you must go out of your way to respect him.

وَنَصَرُوهُ (And they will aid him)—they won't just believe in him and respect him. They'll understand that he's on a mission, and they're part of that mission with him. وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلنُّورَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ مَعَهُۥٓ (And they will follow the light—meaning the Quran—that was sent down with him). أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (Those are the truly successful ones).

This is a Makkan surah already dealing with the People of the Book. Multiple groups have been addressed: Jews, Christians, and the mushrikun (polytheists) of Makkah. Everyone around has been mentioned. So what's the logical next statement?

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "O mankind, indeed I am the Messenger of Allah to you all, [from Him] to whom belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth. There is no deity except Him; He gives life and causes death." So believe in Allah and His Messenger, the unlettered prophet, who believes in Allah and His words, and follow him that you may be guided
commentary:
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِنِّى رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ جَمِيعًا ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ ٱلنَّبِىِّ ٱلْأُمِّىِّ ٱلَّذِى يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ وَٱتَّبِعُوهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ

Say: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ (O humanity), إِنِّى رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ جَمِيعًا (I am the Messenger of Allah to all of you together). Not just to the Jews. Not just to the Christians. Not just to the Arabs. To everyone, all together.

The message immediately shifts focus from the messenger to the One who sent him: ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (The One to whom belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth). لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ (No one is worthy of worship and obedience except Him). يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ (He gives life and He gives death).

Why does the focus shift so quickly to Allah ﷻ? Because the real reason people should believe in a messenger is to connect properly with Allah ﷻ. If you truly understood who Allah ﷻ is, accepting His messenger would be natural and easy. People keep forgetting why they're supposed to follow revelation in the first place.

فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ ٱلنَّبِىِّ ٱلْأُمِّىِّ (So believe in Allah and His messenger, the prophet, the unlettered one). When two words are mentioned together—Rasul (messenger) and Nabi (prophet)—the purpose is to highlight the distinction between them. Rasul indicates he comes with a new message, new legislation. Nabi comes from نَبَأ (news)—he has information you don't possess, knowledge you couldn't have accessed on your own. And despite being unlettered—unable to read or write—he brought this miraculous knowledge.

ٱلَّذِى يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ (The one who believes in Allah and His words). وَٱتَّبِعُوهُ (So follow him closely), لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (so that you may be truly committed to guidance).

This is the magnificent call: to all of humanity, from every background and belief system, to accept this final messenger and through him find the path to Allah ﷻ.

translation: And among the people of Moses is a community which guides by truth and by it establishes justice
commentary:
وَمِن قَوْمِ مُوسَىٰٓ أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَبِهِۦ يَعْدِلُونَ

"And among the people of Musa (AS) is a community which guides by truth and by it establishes justice."

Even within the nation of Musa (AS), there existed a group of people who remained steadfast on the truth. The word أُمَّة (ummah) indicates not just a few individuals, but a sizable, significant group. These were people who fulfilled two essential functions: يَهْدُونَ بِٱلْحَقِّ – they guide others by means of the truth, actively calling people to what is right. 

The second quality is equally important: وَبِهِۦ يَعْدِلُونَ – by means of that same truth, they pass verdicts and establish justice. The root ع د ل  can mean both fairness and unfairness depending on context, but here it clearly refers to establishing justice and fairness in their judgments. This ayah establishes that even in times of widespread corruption, there will always be those who hold firm to Allah's ﷻ guidance.

translation: And We divided them into twelve descendant tribes [as distinct] nations. And We inspired to Moses when his people implored him for water, "Strike with your staff the stone," and there gushed forth from it twelve springs. Every people knew its watering place. And We shaded them with clouds and sent down upon them manna and quails, [saying], "Eat from the good things with which We have provided you." And they wronged Us not, but they were [only] wronging themselves
commentary:
وَقَطَّعْنَـٰهُمُ ٱثْنَتَىْ عَشْرَةَ أَسْبَاطًا أُمَمًا ۚ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ إِذِ ٱسْتَسْقَىٰهُ قَوْمُهُۥٓ أَنِ ٱضْرِب بِّعَصَاكَ ٱلْحَجَرَ ۖ فَٱنۢبَجَسَتْ مِنْهُ ٱثْنَتَا عَشْرَةَ عَيْنًا ۖ قَدْ عَلِمَ كُلُّ أُنَاسٍ مَّشْرَبَهُمْ ۚ وَظَلَّلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْغَمَـٰمَ وَأَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَنَّ وَٱلسَّلْوَىٰ ۖ كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ ۚ وَمَا ظَلَمُونَا وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ

"And We divided them into twelve tribes [as distinct] nations. And We inspired to Musa (AS) when his people asked him for water, 'Strike with your staff the stone,' and there gushed forth from it twelve springs. Each people knew its watering place. And We shaded them with clouds and sent down to them manna and quails, [saying], 'Eat from the good things with which We have provided you.' And they wronged Us not, but they were [only] wronging themselves."

Allah ﷻ divided the children of Israel into twelve tribes – أَسْبَاطًا, which refers to large family groups that eventually evolved into أُمَمًا, full nations. Notice the progression in terminology: what began as tribes developed into distinct nations over time.

When the people asked Musa (AS) for water in the desert, Allah ﷻ commanded him to strike the rock with his staff. The word used here is انۢبَجَسَتْ, which implies water gushing out but in measured amounts. This is distinct from the term used in Surah Al-Baqarah, انفَجَرَتْ, which indicates abundant flowing water. The subtle difference in wording contains wisdom we continue to study.

From this rock burst forth twelve springs – one for each tribe. قَدْ عَلِمَ كُلُّ أُنَاسٍ مَّشْرَبَهُمْ – each group of people already knew their designated drinking place. There was no confusion, no争夺, no fighting over resources. Allah ﷻ made the distribution clear and fair.

Beyond water, Allah ﷻ provided shade through clouds – not threatening storm clouds, but الْغَمَـٰمَ, gentle, protective clouds that shielded them from the desert heat. He sent down المَنَّ, a wheat-like seed from which they could make bread, and السَّلْوَىٰ, quail-like birds that didn't fly away when approached, making them easy to catch for food.

The command was simple: كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ – eat from the pure, good things We have provided you. Yet despite these overwhelming blessings, they complained and disobeyed. Allah ﷻ says: وَمَا ظَلَمُونَا – they didn't wrong Us. When people disobey Allah ﷻ, they don't harm Him in any way. وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ – they were only engaged in wronging themselves. The consequences of disobedience fall entirely upon the disobedient.

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], when it was said to them, "Dwell in this city and eat from it wherever you will and say, 'Relieve us of our burdens,' and enter the gate bowing humbly; We will [then] forgive you your sins. We will increase the doers of good [in goodness and reward]
commentary:
وَإِذْ قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱسْكُنُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةَ وَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمْ وَقُولُوا۟ حِطَّةٌ وَٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْبَابَ سُجَّدًا نَّغْفِرْ لَكُمْ خَطِيٓـَٔـٰتِكُمْ ۚ سَنَزِيدُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

"And [recall] when it was said to them, 'Dwell in this city and eat from it wherever you will and say, "Relieve us of our burdens," and enter the gate bowing humbly; We will [then] forgive you your sins. We will increase the doers of good [in goodness].'"

When they were finally brought to the Promised Land, Allah ﷻ gave them straightforward instructions. اسْكُنُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةَ – settle in this town. وَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمْ – eat from it wherever you want, without restriction. The land was theirs to enjoy freely.

They were given three simple commands. First: قُولُوا۟ حِطَّةٌ – say the word "hittah," which means forgiveness, a request for Allah ﷻ to remove their burdens. Second: ادْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْبَابَ سُجَّدًا – enter the gate in a state of humility, with heads bowed low, like someone riding an animal with their head resting on its neck, demonstrating complete submission.

In return, Allah ﷻ promised: نَّغْفِرْ لَكُمْ خَطِيٓـَٔـٰتِكُمْ – We will forgive your mistakes. Despite all their previous errors and complaints, the door to forgiveness remained open. More than that: سَنَزِيدُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ – We will increase those who excel, those who pursue excellence in their faith. 

This sequence is meaningful. Allah ﷻ acknowledges they have accumulated many mistakes, but forgiveness wipes the slate clean. After that fresh start, the path to becoming among the muhsinin – those who excel in their faith – remains completely open. Past sins don't permanently bar you from achieving the highest levels of righteousness.

translation: But those who wronged among them changed [the words] to a statement other than that which had been said to them. So We sent upon them a punishment from the sky for the wrong that they were doing
commentary:
فَبَدَّلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ قَوْلًا غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى قِيلَ لَهُمْ فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِجْزًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَظْلِمُونَ

"But those who wronged among them changed [those words] to a statement other than that which had been said to them. So We sent upon them a punishment from the sky for the wrong that they were doing."

Despite the simplicity of what was asked, فَبَدَّلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ قَوْلًا غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى قِيلَ لَهُمْ – those who did wrong among them replaced that word with something else, something other than what they were told to say. They couldn't even bring themselves to utter a simple word asking for forgiveness.

The consequence was swift: فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِجْزًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ – We sent upon them a punishment, a plague from the sky. The word رِجْزًا implies something disturbing, deeply unsettling. بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَظْلِمُونَ – because of the wrong they were engaged in doing. Their punishment was directly connected to their persistent wrongdoing.

translation: And ask them about the town that was by the sea - when they transgressed in [the matter of] the sabbath - when their fish came to them openly on their sabbath day, and the day they had no sabbath they did not come to them. Thus did We give them trial because they were defiantly disobedient
commentary:
وَسْـَٔلْهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلَّتِى كَانَتْ حَاضِرَةَ ٱلْبَحْرِ إِذْ يَعْدُونَ فِى ٱلسَّبْتِ إِذْ تَأْتِيهِمْ حِيتَانُهُمْ يَوْمَ سَبْتِهِمْ شُرَّعًا وَيَوْمَ لَا يَسْبِتُونَ ۙ لَا تَأْتِيهِمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَبْلُوهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ

"And ask them about the town that was by the sea – when they transgressed in [the matter of] the sabbath – when their fish came to them openly on their sabbath day, and the day they had no sabbath they did not come to them. Thus did We give them trial because they were defiantly disobedient."

Now we come to an incident mentioned briefly in Surah Al-Baqarah but explained in detail here. Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ: وَسْـَٔلْهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ  – ask them about the town. This wasn't just any settlement – ٱلَّتِى كَانَتْ حَاضِرَةَ ٱلْبَحْرِ – it was right on the water's edge, a coastal town, a marine settlement where fishing was the primary livelihood.

The test came regarding the Sabbath. إِذْ يَعْدُونَ فِى ٱلسَّبْتِ – when they would transgress in the matter of the Sabbath. They were commanded not to work on Saturday, to dedicate it to worship. But observe what happened: إِذْ تَأْتِيهِمْ حِيتَانُهُمْ يَوْمَ سَبْتِهِمْ شُرَّعًا – their fish would come to them on their Sabbath day, openly, abundantly. The word شُرَّعًا suggests the fish were practically jumping out of the water, presenting themselves to be caught.

Every Saturday, the water would be teeming with fish, so abundant they seemed to be offering themselves. وَيَوْمَ لَا يَسْبِتُونَ ۙ لَا تَأْتِيهِمْ – but on the days they weren't observing the Sabbath, the days they were permitted to fish, the fish wouldn't come to them. The water would be empty. The catch would be minimal.

كَذَٰلِكَ نَبْلُوهُم – this is how We tested them. Allah ﷻ created a situation that revealed what was truly in their hearts. بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ – because of the corruption they were engaged in. They had shown themselves to be a people prone to disobedience, so Allah ﷻ gave them a test that would expose the depth of that corruption. 

The principle here is profound: when a nation repeatedly demonstrates corruption, Allah ﷻ sometimes gives them opportunities to dig themselves deeper. It's not that He wants them to fail, but rather that He allows their choices to reveal their true character. The test wasn't arbitrary – it was a response to patterns they had already established.

translation: And when a community among them said, "Why do you advise [or warn] a people whom Allah is [about] to destroy or to punish with a severe punishment?" they [the advisors] said, "To be absolved before your Lord and perhaps they may fear Him
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَالَتْ أُمَّةٌ مِّنْهُمْ لِمَ تَعِظُونَ قَوْمًا ۙ ٱللَّهُ مُهْلِكُهُمْ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا ۖ قَالُوا۟ مَعْذِرَةً إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ

"And when a community among them said, 'Why do you advise [or warn] a people whom Allah is [about to] destroy or to punish with a severe punishment?' they [the advisors] said, 'To be absolved before your Lord and perhaps they may fear Him.'"

Within this community facing the Sabbath test, three distinct groups emerged. The first, as we've seen, actively violated the law. Between the lines, we understand there was another group trying to stop them, the righteous minority calling them back to obedience. 

Then there was a third group – the vast middle, the silent majority. They weren't fishing on Saturday themselves, but they also weren't stopping those who did. When they saw the righteous group trying to advise the violators, this middle group challenged them: لِمَ تَعِظُونَ قَوْمًا – why are you bothering to give advice and sermons to people? ٱللَّهُ مُهْلِكُهُمْ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا – Allah ﷻ is going to destroy them anyway, or at minimum punish them with severe punishment. Why waste your breath? They're hopeless. We're better off without them. Let them go. They're acceptable losses.

This conversation reveals something critical about our own times. Don't we see this pattern today? A large population of Muslims openly engaged in serious sins, disconnected from their religion. A smaller population actively trying to reach them, calling them back. And another population of practicing Muslims saying, "Forget them. They're too far gone. They're secular, they're liberal, don't waste your time. Allah ﷻ will replace them with better people anyway."

Look at the response of those who were trying to make a difference. They gave two reasons for their efforts: قَالُوا۟ مَعْذِرَةً إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ – they said, "To be absolved before your Lord." This is the first and primary reason. It's not even for the sake of those who are sinning – it's for our own sake. When Allah ﷻ questions us on the Day of Judgment, when He asks what we did while corruption spread among our people, we need to have an answer. At minimum, we can say: "Ya Allah, I tried. I spoke to them. I didn't give up on them. I kept channels of communication open."

The second reason: وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ – and perhaps they will develop taqwa, perhaps they will become conscious of Allah ﷻ and return to Him. How do you know they won't? Can you see inside their hearts? Yes, they're violating the Sabbath. Yes, they're doing it knowingly. Yes, they've gone far in their corruption. But that doesn't mean the door of repentance is closed. You cannot make that judgment. Only Allah ﷻ knows if someone will eventually turn back to Him. Maybe Allah ﷻ will put power in your words that doesn't come from you but from Him, and maybe through that, they'll change.

translation: And when they forgot that by which they had been reminded, We saved those who had forbidden evil and seized those who wronged, with a wretched punishment, because they were defiantly disobeying
commentary:
فَلَمَّا نَسُوا۟ مَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَنجَيْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلسُّوٓءِ وَأَخَذْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ بِعَذَابٍۭ بَـِٔيسٍۭ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ

"And when they forgot that by which they had been reminded, We saved those who had forbidden evil and seized those who wronged, with a wretched punishment, because they were defiantly disobedient."

The righteous group continued their efforts, but فَلَمَّا نَسُوا۟ مَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ – when they completely forgot what they were repeatedly reminded of. The violators didn't just ignore the advice – they actively rejected it, pushing it completely out of their minds.

Then came the divine response. أَنجَيْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلسُّوٓءِ – We rescued those who used to forbid evil. Notice carefully: three groups were mentioned in the story, but when it comes to rescue, only one is explicitly named – those who actively forbade the wrong.

وَأَخَذْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ بِعَذَابٍۭ بَـِٔيسٍۭ – and We seized those who did wrong with a terrible, wretched punishment. The word بَـِٔيسٍۭ indicates something horrible, truly terrible. بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ – because of the corruption they were engaged in.

But this raises a crucial question: who exactly are "those who did wrong"? The scholars have two opinions. Some say this refers only to those who actually fished on Saturday – the ones who directly violated the command. According to this view, the silent majority isn't mentioned because they weren't punished but also weren't specifically rescued. Their fate simply isn't addressed.

Other scholars argue that "those who did wrong" includes both the active violators and the silent majority. In reality, there were only two parties: those who spoke out against evil, and everyone else. The silent watchers, by their inaction, joined the camp of the wrongdoers. 

This interpretation finds support in a powerful hadith qudsi. Allah ﷻ once commanded that a nation be destroyed, their city turned upside down. The angel said, "But Ya Allah, in this nation is a person who hasn't disobeyed You even for the blink of an eye, not for a split second." Allah ﷻ responded, "Start with him first. His blood didn't boil when I was disobeyed. He wasn't bothered when My religion was mocked, when haram was committed openly. He was content with his personal worship but never spoke up when My boundaries were violated."

This teaches us that silence in the face of widespread wrong is itself a crime. The famous hadith of the Prophet ﷺ instructs us: "Whoever among you sees an evil, let him change it with his hand. If he cannot, then with his tongue. If he cannot, then with his heart, and that is the weakest of faith." The people in this story had the opportunity to speak – another group was already speaking – but they chose silence, not out of fear, but out of apathy and hopelessness about their fellow believers.

translation: So when they were insolent about that which they had been forbidden, We said to them, "Be apes, despised
commentary:
فَلَمَّا عَتَوْا۟ عَن مَّا نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ قُلْنَا لَهُمْ كُونُوا۟ قِرَدَةً خَـٰسِـِٔينَ

"So when they were insolent about that which they had been forbidden, We said to them, 'Be apes, despised.'"

فَلَمَّا عَتَوْا۟ عَن مَّا نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ – when they persisted arrogantly in what they were forbidden from, when they brazenly continued their violation despite repeated warnings. The word عَتَوْا۟ indicates extreme arrogance, a defiant persistence in wrongdoing.

قُلْنَا لَهُمْ كُونُوا۟ قِرَدَةً خَـٰسِـِٔينَ – We said to them, "Be apes, despised." They were physically transformed into monkeys, humiliated and degraded. This was the ultimate consequence of their persistent violation and mockery of Allah's ﷻ clear commands.

translation: And [mention] when your Lord declared that He would surely [continue to] send upon them until the Day of Resurrection those who would afflict them with the worst torment. Indeed, your Lord is swift in penalty; but indeed, He is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
وَإِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكَ لَيَبْعَثَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَن يَسُومُهُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَسَرِيعُ ٱلْعِقَابِ ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

"And [mention] when your Lord declared that He would surely [continue to] send upon them until the Day of Resurrection those who would afflict them with the worst torment. Indeed, your Lord is swift in penalty; but indeed, He is Forgiving and Merciful."

وَإِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكَ – and when your Lord declared, when He made a loud proclamation, an announcement. The word تَأَذَّنَ indicates a public, emphatic declaration.

لَيَبْعَثَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَن يَسُومُهُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ – He will certainly send against them until the Day of Resurrection people who will afflict them with the worst kind of torment, who will humiliate them and blacken their faces. One persecution after another, one trial after another.

This declaration has been interpreted in different ways. Some scholars connect it to other passages in the Quran suggesting this will be the ultimate fate of the Children of Israel at the end of times – that they will face the worst persecution just before the Day of Judgment. Others see this as a description of what has already occurred throughout history: the Spanish Inquisition, the purges across Europe, and in recent times, the horrors of the Holocaust. These scholars view such events as the fulfillment of this divine declaration.

Yet the ayah ends with a powerful reminder: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَسَرِيعُ ٱلْعِقَابِ – indeed, your Lord is swift in imposing penalty. When Allah ﷻ decrees punishment, it comes quickly and decisively.

وَإِنَّهُۥ لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ – but indeed, He is Forgiving and Merciful. Even after describing generations of punishment, Allah ﷻ reminds us of His mercy. The door to His forgiveness never closes. No matter how far a people have strayed, no matter how severe the consequences they face, His mercy remains available to those who turn back to Him in sincere repentance. This final statement serves as both warning and hope – warning of the consequences of persistent disobedience, but hope that Allah's ﷻ mercy always exceeds His wrath for those who seek it.

translation: And We divided them throughout the earth into nations. Of them some were righteous, and of them some were otherwise. And We tested them with good [times] and bad that perhaps they would return [to obedience]
commentary:
**وَقَطَّعْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أُمَمًا ۖ مِّنْهُمُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحُونَ وَمِنْهُمْ دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَبَلَوْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْحَسَنَـٰتِ وَٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ**

Allah ﷻ declares: "We dispersed them in the earth as nations." The word قَطَّعْنَـٰهُمْ means "We cut them apart" or "We divided them." This refers to the Bani Israel after their repeated cycles of rebellion and rejection. They were reestablished in the land, then given rulership, but eventually they rejected Prophet Isa (AS). Seventy years after his time, the Babylonians and Assyrians devastated Jerusalem. A policy was instituted that Jews could not live in Palestine—they were expelled completely. Some ended up in Europe, some in Asia, some in Africa. They spread everywhere, no longer one unified people.

Allah ﷻ says: مِّنْهُمُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحُونَ وَمِنْهُمْ دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ—"Among them are the righteous, and among them others beyond that." This is actually a tactful way of saying some are righteous and some are not. The expression دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ literally means "below that" or "less than that"—indicating those who fall short of righteousness.

This dispersal wasn't random—it was part of divine testing. وَبَلَوْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْحَسَنَـٰتِ وَٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ—"And We tested them with good and bad, so that perhaps they may return." Through prosperity and hardship, ease and difficulty, Allah ﷻ continually gave them opportunities to turn back to Him.

translation: And there followed them successors who inherited the Scripture [while] taking the commodities of this lower life and saying, "It will be forgiven for us." And if an offer like it comes to them, they will [again] take it. Was not the covenant of the Scripture taken from them that they would not say about Allah except the truth, and they studied what was in it? And the home of the Hereafter is better for those who fear Allah, so will you not use reason
commentary:
**فَخَلَفَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ وَرِثُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَأْخُذُونَ عَرَضَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْأَدْنَىٰ وَيَقُولُونَ سَيُغْفَرُ لَنَا وَإِن يَأْتِهِمْ عَرَضٌ مِّثْلُهُۥ يَأْخُذُوهُ ۚ أَلَمْ يُؤْخَذْ عَلَيْهِم مِّيثَـٰقُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَن لَّا يَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ وَدَرَسُوا۟ مَا فِيهِ ۗ وَٱلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ ۗ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ**

"Then a following generation came after them"—فَخَلَفَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ. This was a generation far removed from the righteous ones before them. وَرِثُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ—"They inherited the book." These were religious scholars, knowledgeable people who had the scripture in their hands. But what was their focus? يَأْخُذُونَ عَرَضَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْأَدْنَىٰ—"They held onto the goal of this inferior life."

This is a devastating statement. Though they inherited the book, their hearts and minds were consumed with one agenda: accumulating worldly gain. They turned religion into an industry. They transformed sacred knowledge into a commodity to be bought and sold.

The ayah continues: وَيَقُولُونَ سَيُغْفَرُ لَنَا—"And they said, 'We will be forgiven.'" They convinced themselves that despite their corruption, forgiveness would somehow be granted to them. وَإِن يَأْتِهِمْ عَرَضٌ مِّثْلُهُۥ يَأْخُذُوهُ—"And if they were given another opportunity like it, they would take it." If presented with another chance to compromise the faith for worldly benefit, they wouldn't hesitate to seize it again.

Allah ﷻ then asks: أَلَمْ يُؤْخَذْ عَلَيْهِم مِّيثَـٰقُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَن لَّا يَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ—"Wasn't the contract of the book taken from them? That they wouldn't say anything against Allah except the truth?" This rhetorical question emphasizes their violation of their most sacred covenant.

وَدَرَسُوا۟ مَا فِيهِ—"And they had studied what was in it." This makes their betrayal even worse. They weren't ignorant—they knew what the book contained. They had studied it carefully, yet they chose to ignore its teachings when it conflicted with their material interests.

The industrialization of sacred knowledge is one of the most dangerous corruptions that can afflict a religious community. When scholars begin to view their knowledge as a means to territorial control, when they can be bought with money or silenced by employers, when they pursue agendas rather than truth—this is precisely what this ayah warns against.

This doesn't mean that religious teachers shouldn't be compensated for their work. Throughout Islamic history, scholars and teachers received stipends and support. Musab ibn Umayr (RA), who brought Islam to Madinah, was a salaried instructor. The problem isn't compensation—it's when financial considerations cause someone to hide truth, to avoid uncomfortable topics, or to reinterpret the faith to please a patron or customer.

The protective measure historically was that the financing of religious institutions was independent, allowing scholars to speak truth without fear of losing their positions. When religious leaders become employees dependent on particular individuals or groups, they can be pressured to remain silent about certain issues or to modify their message.

وَٱلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ—"And the final home is better for those who truly protect themselves." This is the antidote to worldly corruption: keeping your focus on the eternal life, not the temporary one. أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ—"Why don't you then use your intellect?" This question is directed at religious scholars specifically, calling them to think deeply about what they're doing and to prioritize the Hereafter over worldly gain.

translation: But those who hold fast to the Book and establish prayer - indeed, We will not allow to be lost the reward of the reformers
commentary:
**وَٱلَّذِينَ يُمَسِّكُونَ بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ إِنَّا لَا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُصْلِحِينَ**

After describing those who betrayed their trust, Allah ﷻ now highlights the faithful: وَٱلَّذِينَ يُمَسِّكُونَ بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ—"Those who hold onto the book." The word يُمَسِّكُونَ conveys intense holding, gripping as tightly as possible. These are people who cling to the book with all their strength, refusing to let it go regardless of what worldly benefits they might lose.

وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ—"And they established the prayer." This is their personal connection with Allah ﷻ. While holding the book represents knowledge and public responsibility, establishing prayer represents personal Taqwa and direct relationship with their Master.

إِنَّا لَا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُصْلِحِينَ—"We do not waste the compensation of those who create good." Notice the specific word choice: ٱلْمُصْلِحِينَ, not just الصالحين. A صالح is someone righteous in themselves, but a مصلح is someone who actively creates good, who reforms society, who brings benefit to others.

This is profound. When scholars truly hold onto the book with knowledge and maintain their personal Taqwa through prayer, they don't just become righteous individuals—they become agents of positive change in society. They create islah, reform and goodness that spreads beyond themselves. And Allah ﷻ promises that the efforts of such people will never be wasted.

translation: And [mention] when We raised the mountain above them as if it was a dark cloud and they were certain that it would fall upon them, [and Allah said], "Take what We have given you with determination and remember what is in it that you might fear Allah
commentary:
**۞ وَإِذْ نَتَقْنَا ٱلْجَبَلَ فَوْقَهُمْ كَأَنَّهُۥ ظُلَّةٌ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُۥ وَاقِعٌۢ بِهِمْ خُذُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكُم بِقُوَّةٍ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ مَا فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ**

The surah now shifts to a dramatic moment in the history of Bani Israel: وَإِذْ نَتَقْنَا ٱلْجَبَلَ فَوْقَهُمْ—"When We held the mountain high above them." The verb نَتَقْنَا means to shake or pull something up. كَأَنَّهُۥ ظُلَّةٌ—"As though it was a shadow."

Imagine the scene: they look up and see what appears to be a dark cloud. But as their eyes adjust, they realize it's not a cloud—it's a mountain suspended above their heads. وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُۥ وَاقِعٌۢ بِهِمْ—"And they were convinced it was going to fall upon them."

In that moment, every distraction disappeared. Every excuse evaporated. Every rationalization ceased. When you have a mountain hovering above your head about to crush you, you have no choice but to pay complete attention. This is Allah's way of ensuring they were fully present, fully conscious, with no excuse for later claiming they didn't understand or weren't paying attention.

At that moment of absolute attention, the command came: خُذُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكُم بِقُوَّةٍ—"Hold onto what We have given you with strength." Take this revelation with all the might you have. Don't be casual about it. Don't treat it lightly. وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ مَا فِيهِ—"And remember what is in it."

لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ—"So that you may protect yourselves." The word تَتَّقُونَ literally means "to shield yourself" or "to protect yourself." If there was ever a time they were thinking about protection, it was at that moment with a mountain suspended over them. Allah ﷻ is saying: This should be your level of consciousness when approaching My book—as if your survival depends on it, because ultimately, it does.

translation: And [mention] when your Lord took from the children of Adam - from their loins - their descendants and made them testify of themselves, [saying to them], "Am I not your Lord?" They said, "Yes, we have testified." [This] - lest you should say on the day of Resurrection, "Indeed, we were of this unaware
commentary:
**وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنۢ بَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ مِن ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ ۛ شَهِدْنَآ ۛ أَن تَقُولُوا۟ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ إِنَّا كُنَّا عَنْ هَـٰذَا غَـٰفِلِينَ**

But the mountain incident wasn't the first time Allah ﷻ made them aware. This ayah takes us even further back: وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنۢ بَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ مِن ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ—"When I took from the sons of Adam, from their backs, their offspring."

This refers to a primordial covenant before any of us came to this earth. From Adam (AS) and through all generations, Allah ﷻ drew forth every human being who would ever exist. وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ—"And He made them testify against themselves."

أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ—"Am I not your Master?" 

قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ ۛ شَهِدْنَآ—"They said: Yes, of course! We testify."

Look at the sequencing in Surah Al-A'raf. Earlier, the People of the Book claimed they had excuses: "No warner came to us, no messenger came to us, no book came to us." But here Allah ﷻ is establishing that before any book, before any messenger, before any warner, there was already a fundamental covenant.

أَن تَقُولُوا۟ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ إِنَّا كُنَّا عَنْ هَـٰذَا غَـٰفِلِينَ—"So you don't say on the Day of Judgment, 'We were completely unaware of this.'" Allah ﷻ is closing every possible excuse. You cannot claim ignorance because this knowledge is embedded deep within your very existence.

This has profound implications. Even if Allah ﷻ had sent no revelation, He already had an agreement with us. Deep in our subconscious, in our fitrah, we already know. We already made this testimony. The revelations that came afterward were reminders—reminders of something we already acknowledged before we even entered this world.

translation: Or [lest] you say, "It was only that our fathers associated [others in worship] with Allah before, and we were but descendants after them. Then would You destroy us for what the falsifiers have done
commentary:
**أَوْ تَقُولُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَشْرَكَ ءَابَآؤُنَا مِن قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا ذُرِّيَّةً مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۖ أَفَتُهْلِكُنَا بِمَا فَعَلَ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ**

The ayah continues addressing potential excuses: أَوْ تَقُولُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَشْرَكَ ءَابَآؤُنَا مِن قَبْلُ—"Or you might say, 'Our ancestors committed shirk before us.'" This is a common human tendency—to blame previous generations for the state we're in.

وَكُنَّا ذُرِّيَّةً مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ—"And we were just offspring after them." We inherited this situation. We're products of our environment. We can't be held responsible for what they started.

أَفَتُهْلِكُنَا بِمَا فَعَلَ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ—"So will You destroy us for what these people who produced falsehood did?" This argument tries to escape personal accountability by pointing to historical circumstances.

But this excuse is demolished by what was mentioned in the previous ayah. When Allah ﷻ took the covenant, He didn't just pull you out from Adam (AS)—He pulled out all your ancestors too. You were all there together. You and your ancestors were equals in that moment, all making the same testimony. You cannot claim it's their fault when you were standing right beside them, making the same promise.

The chronological argument fails. You weren't born into shirk as an innocent victim of your forefathers' mistakes. You were there with them in that primordial gathering, and you made your own choice then.

translation: And thus do We [explain in] detail the verses, and perhaps they will return
commentary:
**وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ**

وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ—"And that is how We explain the ayat in detail." The word نُفَصِّلُ means to separate, to make distinct, to explain clearly. Allah ﷻ has laid out the evidence piece by piece, closing every possible avenue of excuse.

وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ—"So that they may return." Return to what? Return to the original agreement they made. Return to the covenant they stood under when the mountain hovered above them. Return to the testimony they gave before they even came to this earth.

This is the purpose of all this detailed explanation—to bring human beings back to their original promise to Allah ﷻ. In both cases mentioned in these ayat—the mountain hovering above and the primordial covenant—who was being addressed? People who already knew. These weren't ignorant people. They were people who had been given knowledge, who had been made aware, who had made conscious commitments.

The message is clear: knowledge carries responsibility. Those who have been given revelation, who have been taught, who have been reminded—they have no excuse. The covenant has been established, the evidence has been presented, and the path has been made clear.

translation: And recite to them, [O Muhammad], the news of him to whom we gave [knowledge of] Our signs, but he detached himself from them; so Satan pursued him, and he became of the deviators
commentary:
**وَٱتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ءَايَـٰتِنَا فَٱنسَلَخَ مِنْهَا فَأَتْبَعَهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْغَاوِينَ**

"Recite to them the news of the one to whom We gave Our signs, but he slipped away from them, so Shaytan pursued him, and he became of those who went astray."

Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ to narrate a profound cautionary tale about a scholar who possessed divine knowledge but ultimately lost everything. The choice of words here is deliberate and striking. When Allah ﷻ says آتَيْنَـٰهُ (We gave him), He's taking direct credit for this person's knowledge. This wasn't someone who stumbled upon understanding or pieced together wisdom through their own efforts alone. Allah ﷻ specifically chose this individual and bestowed upon him آيَاتِنَا (Our signs) – the strongest form of miraculous knowledge.

You cannot have more credible scholarly credentials than having Allah ﷻ Himself testify that He gave you His signs. This was a person of exceptional understanding, someone who had been granted divine insight into the truth.

Then comes the devastating turn: فَٱنسَلَخَ مِنْهَا (he slipped away from them). The word انسلاخ carries a vivid, almost disturbing imagery. In Arabic, this term describes how a snake sheds its skin – how it literally slithers out, leaving behind what once covered it completely. This scholar wore the outward appearance of knowledge like a garment, but his character slipped right out of it. The knowledge that should have transformed his entire being became nothing more than a shell he discarded.

The moment he abandoned what would have protected him, فَأَتْبَعَهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ (Shaytan followed him closely). Shaytan didn't just observe from a distance – he got right on this person's case immediately, pursuing him relentlessly. Once the protective barrier of divine knowledge was removed through the person's own choice, there was nothing left to keep Shaytan away.

فَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْغَاوِينَ (he became of those who went astray) – he joined the ranks of those who took the wrong path and just kept going, unable or unwilling to turn back. This is the story of someone who had everything needed for salvation but threw it all away. In Islamic tradition, this passage is understood to refer to the story of Bal'am, a scholar who, step by step, compromised his principles until he fell into major sin – even adultery and murder – after starting from a position of righteousness and knowledge.

translation: And if We had willed, we could have elevated him thereby, but he adhered [instead] to the earth and followed his own desire. So his example is like that of the dog: if you chase him, he pants, or if you leave him, he [still] pants. That is the example of the people who denied Our signs. So relate the stories that perhaps they will give thought
commentary:
**وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَرَفَعْنَـٰهُ بِهَا وَلَـٰكِنَّهُۥٓ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ ۚ فَمَثَلُهُۥ كَمَثَلِ ٱلْكَلْبِ إِن تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْهِ يَلْهَثْ أَوْ تَتْرُكْهُ يَلْهَث ۚ ذَّٰلِكَ مَثَلُ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا ۚ فَٱقْصُصِ ٱلْقَصَصَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ**

"Had We willed, We could have elevated him through them, but he clung to the earth and followed his own desires. His example is like that of a dog: if you chase it, it pants, and if you leave it alone, it still pants. Such is the example of those who deny Our signs. So narrate the stories, that perhaps they will reflect."

Allah ﷻ reveals what could have been: وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَرَفَعْنَـٰهُ بِهَا (Had We willed, We would have elevated him by means of them). The divine signs this person received were meant to be a means of elevation. True knowledge of Allah's ayat should lift a person spiritually, bringing them closer to their Creator. This is the purpose of revelation – to raise human beings above their base nature.

But here we must understand the dual nature of our human existence. Allah ﷻ created us with both a body and a soul (ruh). The body has its needs and appetites – food, shelter, comfort, physical desires. The soul has entirely different needs – it hungers for meaning, truth, connection with the divine. When Allah ﷻ took the covenant from humanity asking "Am I not your Lord?" (أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ), that wasn't a contract with our bodies. That was an agreement with our souls, our spiritual essence.

The divine signs Allah ﷻ gives are nourishment for the soul. They're meant to elevate that ruh inside us. But our bodies also have desires, temptations, cravings that pull in the opposite direction. There's a constant battle between feeding the legitimate needs of the soul and satisfying the wants of the physical self.

This scholar had been given the knowledge to feed both aspects of his being in a legitimate, balanced way. But وَلَـٰكِنَّهُۥٓ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ (he clung to the earth) – he made a choice. He decided to pursue only what his body wanted, what his physical nature craved. The soul inside him, that creature of light that thrives on revelation, began to be starved and suffocated.

Here's what happens when you neglect your spiritual nature: the more you violate the rights of your soul and chase worldly things – especially those things Allah ﷻ has forbidden – the more that light inside you gets covered in layers of darkness. Imagine a bright lamp being wrapped in cloth after cloth until no light can escape. That's what happens to the heart, to the ruh. It's still there, but it's buried, completely surrounded by darkness. You lose access to that inner compass. This is the meaning of spiritual death – the heart becomes sealed. This is called being among the walking dead.

The body is made from earth, from dust, so naturally all its appetites relate to this earthly existence. Its food is here, its temptations are here, its desires don't reach beyond this physical realm. When someone stops feeding their soul with revelation and remembrance of Allah ﷻ, all that remains active are these earthly cravings. The soul gets choked, and for all practical purposes, you lose access to it entirely.

Then comes one of the Quran's most striking metaphors: فَمَثَلُهُۥ كَمَثَلِ ٱلْكَلْبِ إِن تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْهِ يَلْهَثْ أَوْ تَتْرُكْهُ يَلْهَث (His example is like that of a dog: if you load it with burdens, it pants, and if you leave it alone, it still pants). This knowledgeable person – someone who perhaps gave lectures, wrote books, had students and followers – is now compared to a dog with its tongue hanging out, panting uncontrollably.

The dog can't pull its tongue back in. Whether you burden it or leave it be, it's still panting, still drooling, still unable to find satisfaction. This is the state of someone who becomes enslaved to their appetites. They become addicted – to wealth, to status, to pleasure, to whatever worldly thing has taken the place of their spiritual fulfillment. No amount satisfies them because the appetite they're trying to feed is insatiable when pursued for its own sake.

Allah ﷻ has reduced this scholar to the lowest description imaginable, and there's deep wisdom in why the example specifically mentions a dog. Animals at least never had something precious that they lost. But a human being? You were given something extraordinary – a soul capable of recognizing divine truth, a heart that could connect with its Creator. To reduce yourself to the level of an animal after possessing such gifts is far worse than simply being an animal to begin with.

ذَّٰلِكَ مَثَلُ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا (Such is the example of those who deny Our signs) – this isn't just about one person. By giving an example (مَثَل), Allah ﷻ is indicating this pattern will repeat. This is a warning for all who come after. Watch out for this danger in yourselves and in your communities.

فَٱقْصُصِ ٱلْقَصَصَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (So narrate the stories, that perhaps they will reflect). The command to tell this story in detail serves a purpose: deep contemplation. This isn't just history or entertainment. Ask yourself: could this happen to me? Am I feeding my soul or just my body? Am I using knowledge to elevate myself or to serve my ego and desires?

translation: How evil an example [is that of] the people who denied Our signs and used to wrong themselves
commentary:
**سَآءَ مَثَلًا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَأَنفُسَهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَظْلِمُونَ**

"How evil is the example of those who deny Our signs, and it is themselves they wrong."

سَآءَ مَثَلًا (What a horrible example) – Allah ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا emphasizes just how terrible this state is. Those who lie against or deny Allah's miraculous signs have become the worst example humanity can offer.

But notice the profound justice in the next statement: وَأَنفُسَهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَظْلِمُونَ (it is themselves they wrong). They didn't harm Allah ﷻ. They didn't diminish His dominion or authority. They didn't hurt anyone but themselves. When someone rejects divine guidance, the damage is entirely self-inflicted. They rob themselves of elevation, of peace, of purpose, of the very thing their souls desperately need.

translation: Whoever Allah guides - he is the [rightly] guided; and whoever He sends astray - it is those who are the losers
commentary:
**مَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَهُوَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِى ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ**

"Whoever Allah guides – he is the truly guided. And whoever He sends astray – it is they who are the losers."

This ayah establishes a fundamental truth that should both humble and motivate us: مَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَهُوَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِى (Whoever Allah guides, then he is the one truly guided). It's not about how much you know. It's not about your status or credentials. It's not even about your track record up to this point. What matters is Allah's guidance in this moment and the next.

Your past doesn't determine your future. If you've been righteous for a hundred days, the hundred and first day is not guaranteed. If you've struggled and failed for a hundred days, the hundred and first day is not predetermined by those failures. Each moment is a fresh opportunity for guidance or a test of your commitment.

وَمَن يُضْلِلْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (And whoever He sends astray – they are the ultimate losers). When someone loses Allah's guidance, they've lost everything that truly matters, even if the whole world appears to be at their feet.

translation: And We have certainly created for Hell many of the jinn and mankind. They have hearts with which they do not understand, they have eyes with which they do not see, and they have ears with which they do not hear. Those are like livestock; rather, they are more astray. It is they who are the heedless
commentary:
**وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنَا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ ۖ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ بِهَا وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لَّا يُبْصِرُونَ بِهَا وَلَهُمْ ءَاذَانٌ لَّا يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَآ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰفِلُونَ**

"And We have certainly created for Hell many of the jinn and mankind. They have hearts with which they do not understand, they have eyes with which they do not see, and they have ears with which they do not hear. Those are like cattle; rather, they are more astray. It is they who are the heedless."

وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنَا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ (We have created for Hell many of the jinn and mankind) – this is a sobering declaration. A huge multitude from both the jinn species and human species end up destined for the Fire, not because they lacked the tools for guidance, but because they refused to use them.

لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ بِهَا (They have hearts, but they don't understand with them). They possess the organ, the faculty of spiritual comprehension, but it's dead. That inner reality that should connect them to divine truth has been killed through neglect and disobedience. All they can process is what their rational minds perceive of the physical world. The deeper dimensions of existence remain completely closed to them.

Think of it this way: Allah ﷻ gave Prophet Adam (AS) two types of knowledge. In Surah Al-Baqarah, we read that Allah taught him the names of all things – this represents earthly knowledge, the sciences, technology, history, all the understanding we acquire about the physical world. Then separately, Allah ﷻ says guidance will come from Him – that's revealed knowledge, spiritual wisdom that cannot be derived from studying the material world alone.

Imagine having two eyes to see reality. One eye perceives the physical world and one eye perceives the spiritual world. When both eyes are open and working together, you see clearly and completely. But when one eye is closed – when you only use your intellectual faculties and ignore revelation – you see a distorted, incomplete picture. You might see a cloud in the sky, but you don't see the mercy of Allah ﷻ in it. You might see rain, but you don't recognize divine provision. Everything becomes flat, stripped of its deeper meaning.

This is the tragedy described here. These people have hearts – the faculty for spiritual understanding – but those hearts are dead, so they're left with only their minds to navigate existence.

وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لَّا يُبْصِرُونَ بِهَا (They have eyes, but they don't see with them). They can observe physical reality perfectly well, but they're completely blind to spiritual realities. They can't see the signs of Allah ﷻ in creation. They can't perceive the unseen realm that's more real and more permanent than anything visible.

وَلَهُمْ ءَاذَانٌ لَّا يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَآ (They have ears, but they don't hear with them). When divine revelation is recited, when truth is spoken, when wisdom is shared, it doesn't penetrate. They might process the sounds, but the meaning doesn't reach their hearts. They can't hear what would actually benefit them.

Allah ﷻ has given us hearts, eyes and ears specifically to recognize dangers we cannot physically see – the danger of Shaytan's whispers, the danger of Hellfire, the danger of angelic records documenting our deeds, the danger of standing before our Creator in judgment. He's given us the eyes of faith (بصيرة) to see these realities. When someone doesn't respect or develop these spiritual faculties, they become completely unaware of dangers surrounding them. They don't react because they don't perceive the threat, even when it's right there.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ (They are like cattle). Specifically, Allah ﷻ mentions cattle – animals like cows, goats and sheep – because of their dulled reflexes and awareness. These animals can't run fast when threatened. A sheep might not even realize a wolf is behind it until it's too late. They lack the sharpness and alertness of other creatures.

But then comes an even more devastating comparison: بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ (rather, they are even more astray). How can a human being be worse than an animal? Because at least an animal never had something precious that it lost. An animal was never given the capacity for divine knowledge, the ability to connect with its Creator, the gift of consciousness that can perceive both the seen and unseen. When a human being is given all of these extraordinary gifts and still reduces themselves to a state of heedlessness, they've fallen below even the animal realm.

Someone might spend hours upon hours absorbed in entertainment or distractions, completely unaware as time passes, as opportunities for good slip away, as their life ticks by. The sun rises and sets, major events happen in the world, prayers come and go – and they remain in a state of complete oblivion. This is modern ghafla (heedlessness), and it's exactly what this ayah warns against.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰفِلُونَ (It is they who are the truly heedless). They've been given every tool for awareness and yet remain utterly oblivious to what matters most.

Now, recall something profound from earlier in this Surah. When Allah ﷻ shook Mount Sinai over the Children of Israel, even animals would have been terrified by a mountain hovering overhead. That physical fear doesn't necessarily indicate spiritual awareness – it's just the body's natural response to danger. But Allah ﷻ used that bodily fear to try to wake up their souls. Once the immediate physical threat was removed, the spiritual awareness was supposed to remain – they were meant to remember and internalize what was in the Torah they'd been given. But many returned to heedlessness the moment the mountain was lowered. That's the disease of ghafla.

translation: And to Allah belong the best names, so invoke Him by them. And leave [the company of] those who practice deviation concerning His names. They will be recompensed for what they have been doing
commentary:
**وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ فَٱدْعُوهُ بِهَا ۖ وَذَرُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِىٓ أَسْمَـٰٓئِهِۦ ۚ سَيُجْزَوْنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ**

"And to Allah belong the Most Beautiful Names, so call upon Him by them. And leave those who deviate regarding His Names. They will be recompensed for what they have been doing."

After describing the depths of spiritual death and heedlessness, Allah ﷻ provides the remedy: وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ فَٱدْعُوهُ بِهَا (To Allah belong the Most Beautiful Names, so call upon Him by them). On one hand, you have people described with the worst characteristics – like dogs, like cattle, even worse. On the other hand, you have the cure: learn and use Allah's Most Beautiful Names.

This is how you awaken from heedlessness. Get to know your Creator through His names.
