Each name opens a window into understanding His attributes, His relationship with creation, how to properly call upon Him. When you truly understand الرَّحْمَٰن (the Most Merciful), you begin to see His mercy everywhere. When you comprehend الْعَلِيم (the All-Knowing), you gain comfort that nothing about your situation is hidden from Him.

The supplications (du'a) taught by the Prophet ﷺ are extraordinarily beautiful precisely because they use Allah's names perfectly. Consider the du'a for traveling: "You are my companion in the journey and the One I leave behind with my family." Think about that – anyone else can either be with you on your journey or back home with your family. But when you call upon Allah ﷻ, you're recognizing He's with you and simultaneously with your loved ones you left behind. He's not bound by space or limitation. These are profound truths embedded in simple words.

فَٱدْعُوهُ بِهَا (Call upon Him by them) – this is an instruction to actively use these names in your supplications. The Prophet ﷺ taught us specific du'as for waking up, sleeping, eating, traveling, dressing, entering the home, dealing with difficulties, maintaining family relationships. Each one uses Allah's names appropriately and beautifully. Memorizing and using these supplications regularly transforms your relationship with your Creator. It's not a difficult practice, but its impact is immense.

وَذَرُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِىٓ أَسْمَـٰٓئِهِۦ (And leave those who deviate regarding His Names). The word يُلْحِدُونَ comes from لَحْد, which refers to crookedness, like when a grave is dug improperly and ends up crooked instead of straight.

This command has several dimensions. First, don't associate with people who mock or make fun of Allah's names. Distance yourself from that company. Second, don't misuse His names yourself. Some of Allah's names cannot be used in isolation. For example, الْمُعِزُّ (the Giver of Honor) and الْمُذِلُّ (the Giver of Humiliation) must be understood together. You can't just repeatedly call out "Ya Mudhill, Ya Mudhill" asking Allah ﷻ to humiliate someone. That's inappropriate. If you want to call upon Him using those names, you say "Ya Mu'izz, Ya Mudhill" – acknowledging that both honor and humiliation are in His hands alone.

Third, don't invent your own formulas or superstitions around Allah's names. People sometimes create practices like "recite this name exactly 87,035 times and you'll get what you want" or "this name will cure baldness" or "that name will fix your car's transmission." You cannot make up such things. The proper way to invoke Allah's names has already been taught through revelation. Nobody has access to the unseen effects of these names except through what's been authentically transmitted from the Prophet ﷺ.

In some cultures, people misuse Allah's names in bizarre ways – treating them like magical incantations disconnected from their true meanings and proper usage. This is exactly the deviation Allah ﷻ warns against here.

سَيُجْزَوْنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (They will be recompensed for what they have been doing). Those who abuse, mock or misuse Allah's names will face consequences. Don't abuse religious texts. Don't abuse these sacred names. Use them properly, with the respect and understanding they deserve, in the manner taught by the Prophet ﷺ.

translation: And among those We created is a community which guides by truth and thereby establishes justice
commentary:
وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنَآ أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَبِهِۦ يَعْدِلُونَ

Among those We created, there exists a community that guides others with the truth and establishes justice through it. This ayah shifts our attention from those who reject Allah's signs to those who embrace them. The expression وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنَآ (from among those We created) emphasizes that within Allah's vast creation, there will always be a group committed to truth and justice.

The phrase يَهْدُونَ بِٱلْحَقِّ (they guide with the truth) indicates their active role in calling people to guidance, while وَبِهِۦ يَعْدِلُونَ (and by it they establish justice) shows that this same truth becomes the foundation for their verdicts and judgments. This community doesn't merely possess knowledge—they apply it in how they treat others and make decisions. The truth they carry isn't an abstract concept but a living principle that shapes their conduct and their interactions with society.

translation: But those who deny Our signs - We will progressively lead them [to destruction] from where they do not know
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

As for those who deny Our signs, We will gradually lead them to destruction from where they do not perceive. The word سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُم (We will gradually lead them) comes from the root meaning "degrees" or "steps." This describes a chilling reality: someone continues to increase in their sins while simultaneously experiencing worldly success. Their business thrives, they receive promotions, their wealth grows—and so they begin to believe there are no consequences for their actions.

This is istidraj—a deceptive progression where Allah ﷻ allows them to take further steps into sin while opening doors of worldly opportunity. They interpret their material success as a sign of divine approval, never realizing that this comfort is actually part of a divine plan. The phrase مِّنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (from where they do not perceive) emphasizes their complete unawareness. They think they're making progress, but they're actually being given more rope, like a fish that believes it's free while the hook remains firmly in its mouth.

translation: And I will give them time. Indeed, my plan is firm
commentary:
وَأُمْلِى لَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ كَيْدِى مَتِينٌ

And I will grant them time—indeed, My plan is firm. The word أُمْلِى means to let loose the leash, to give someone an extension of time. Allah ﷻ is not hasty in His punishment. He allows people the space to continue, but this shouldn't be mistaken for divine negligence or approval.

The statement إِنَّ كَيْدِى مَتِينٌ (My plan is firm) carries weight. The word مَتِينٌ is used for a rope that cannot break, something solid and established. No matter how free these people feel, no matter how much they think they've escaped consequences, Allah's plan is already in motion and cannot be undone. They are already caught—they just don't know it yet.

translation: Then do they not give thought? There is in their companion [Muhammad] no madness. He is not but a clear warner
commentary:
أَوَلَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا۟ ۗ مَا بِصَاحِبِهِم مِّن جِنَّةٍ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ

Have they not reflected? There is no madness in their companion. He is only a clear warner. The word صَاحِب (companion) is significant here. It implies someone who has spent considerable time with others, someone familiar and known. The Quraysh had lived alongside the Prophet ﷺ for years. They knew his character, his intellect, his honesty. They called him Al-Amin, the trustworthy one.

The question أَوَلَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا۟ (Have they not reflected?) challenges them to use the very reasoning they claim to value. If they would simply think deeply about the man they've known their entire lives, they would realize the absurdity of their accusation that he is possessed by jinn. The Prophet ﷺ is described as نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (a clear warner)—someone whose very speech and demeanor make it obvious that he carries divine guidance, not demonic influence.

translation: Do they not look into the realm of the heavens and the earth and everything that Allah has created and [think] that perhaps their appointed time has come near? So in what statement hereafter will they believe
commentary:
أَوَلَمْ يَنظُرُوا۟ فِى مَلَكُوتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ مِن شَىْءٍ وَأَنْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ قَدِ ٱقْتَرَبَ أَجَلُهُمْ ۖ فَبِأَىِّ حَدِيثٍۭ بَعْدَهُۥ يُؤْمِنُونَ

Have they not looked into the dominion of the heavens and the earth, and all that Allah ﷻ has created, and that perhaps their appointed time has drawn near? Then in what statement after this will they believe? This ayah presents two powerful invitations to reflection. First, the call to observe مَلَكُوتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (the dominion of the heavens and the earth)—to look at the magnificent creation around them and recognize the Creator behind it all.

Second, the sobering reminder: وَأَنْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ قَدِ ٱقْتَرَبَ أَجَلُهُمْ (perhaps their appointed time has drawn near). They're so occupied with their arguments and denials that they haven't stopped to consider that their time to decide may be running out. Death doesn't announce itself—it arrives.

The final question is devastating: فَبِأَىِّ حَدِيثٍۭ بَعْدَهُۥ يُؤْمِنُونَ (Then in what statement after this will they believe?). This refers to both the Prophet ﷺ and the Quran. If this combination—a trustworthy messenger and a miraculous revelation—isn't enough to convince them, what possibly could be? There is no hope after this.

translation: Whoever Allah sends astray - there is no guide for him. And He leaves them in their transgression, wandering blindly
commentary:
مَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَا هَادِىَ لَهُۥ ۚ وَيَذَرُهُمْ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ

Whoever Allah ﷻ allows to be misled, there is no guide for him. And He leaves them to wander blindly in their transgression. This ayah establishes a reality that runs throughout the Quran: ultimate guidance belongs to Allah ﷻ alone. When He withdraws His guidance from someone, no force in creation can bring them back to the path.

The phrase وَيَذَرُهُمْ (He leaves them) indicates divine abandonment—not cruel neglect, but the natural consequence of persistent rejection. These people have pushed away guidance repeatedly, and now Allah ﷻ allows them to continue in their chosen direction. They remain فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (blindly wandering in their transgression), going further and further into darkness, unable to see their way out.

translation: They ask you, [O Muhammad], about the Hour: when is its arrival? Say, "Its knowledge is only with my Lord. None will reveal its time except Him. It lays heavily upon the heavens and the earth. It will not come upon you except unexpectedly." They ask you as if you are familiar with it. Say, "Its knowledge is only with Allah, but most of the people do not know
commentary:
يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلسَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْسَىٰهَا ۖ قُلْ إِنَّمَا عِلْمُهَا عِندَ رَبِّى ۖ لَا يُجَلِّيهَا لِوَقْتِهَآ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ ثَقُلَتْ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ لَا تَأْتِيكُمْ إِلَّا بَغْتَةً ۗ يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ كَأَنَّكَ حَفِىٌّ عَنْهَا ۖ قُلْ إِنَّمَا عِلْمُهَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

They ask you about the Hour: when will it be established? Say: "Its knowledge is only with my Master. None can reveal its time except Him. It weighs heavily upon the heavens and the earth. It will not come upon you except suddenly." They ask you as though you are persistent in inquiring about it. Say: "Its knowledge is only with Allah ﷻ, but most people do not know."

The disbelievers ask about السَّاعَةِ (the Hour)—the Day of Judgment. The word مُرْسَىٰهَا comes from the same root used for anchoring or pegging a tent into the ground. They want to know: when will this Hour be firmly established? The answer is direct: إِنَّمَا عِلْمُهَا عِندَ رَبِّى (Its knowledge is only with my Master).

The phrase لَا يُجَلِّيهَا لِوَقْتِهَآ إِلَّا هُوَ (None can reveal its time except Him) uses a word that means to solve a mystery or bring something hidden to light. Only Allah ﷻ has the power to unveil this secret at its appointed time.

Then comes a powerful image: ثَقُلَتْ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (It weighs heavily upon the heavens and the earth). The word ثَقُلَتْ is used for a pregnant woman in her final stages, heavy with child and about to deliver. The heavens and earth are carrying the Day of Judgment like a burden they can barely hold. Everything necessary for that Day is already present, waiting to be released. And when it comes, لَا تَأْتِيكُمْ إِلَّا بَغْتَةً (it will not come except suddenly)—without warning, without announcement.

The disbelievers ask the Prophet ﷺ as though كَأَنَّكَ حَفِىٌّ عَنْهَا (you are persistent in inquiring about it)—as if he constantly asks Allah ﷻ about it. But the reality is different. The Prophet ﷺ doesn't ask because he knows it's a frightening question, and the answer is already clear: its knowledge belongs exclusively to Allah ﷻ.

The ayah ends with a critical clarification: وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (but most people do not know). What don't they know? Not when the Hour is coming—nobody knows that. Rather, they don't know that its knowledge is exclusively with Allah ﷻ. Throughout history, people have tried to calculate, predict, and figure out when Judgment Day will arrive. The Mayan calendar, various prophecies, numerological calculations—all of these attempts stem from a failure to accept that this knowledge has never been revealed to anyone. Most people fail to grasp this fundamental reality.

translation: Say, "I hold not for myself [the power of] benefit or harm, except what Allah has willed. And if I knew the unseen, I could have acquired much wealth, and no harm would have touched me. I am not except a warner and a bringer of good tidings to a people who believe
commentary:
قُل لَّآ أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِى نَفْعًا وَلَا ضَرًّا إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَلَوْ كُنتُ أَعْلَمُ ٱلْغَيْبَ لَٱسْتَكْثَرْتُ مِنَ ٱلْخَيْرِ وَمَا مَسَّنِىَ ٱلسُّوٓءُ ۚ إِنْ أَنَا۠ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ

Say: "I do not possess for myself any benefit or harm, except what Allah ﷻ wills. And if I knew the unseen, I would have acquired much good, and no harm would have touched me. I am only a warner and a bringer of good news to people who believe."

The Prophet ﷺ is commanded to declare his human limitations. He cannot control benefit or harm even for himself, except by Allah's will. This is a powerful statement of servitude—the Messenger of Allah ﷻ openly acknowledging that he has no independent power.

The hypothetical وَلَوْ كُنتُ أَعْلَمُ ٱلْغَيْبَ (if I knew the unseen) dismantles any notion that the Prophet ﷺ has access to hidden knowledge. If he did know the unseen, he says, لَٱسْتَكْثَرْتُ مِنَ ٱلْخَيْرِ (I would have acquired much good). This can be understood in two ways. In worldly terms: if he knew the future, he would have accumulated wealth and avoided all difficulties. In religious terms: he would have gone directly to those he knew would accept Islam and avoided the troubles with those who would reject him.

The phrase وَمَا مَسَّنِىَ ٱلسُّوٓءُ (and no harm would have touched me) reinforces this. The Prophet ﷺ experienced hardship, injury, loss—all because he didn't know what was coming. His role is clear: إِنْ أَنَا۠ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ (I am only a warner and a bringer of good news).

Notice the sequence here—نَذِيرٌ (warner) comes before بَشِيرٌ (bringer of good news), which is the opposite of the usual order. This is because Surah Al-A'raf has emphasized warning throughout. The surah has recounted the destruction of nations, the consequences of rejection, the severity of Allah's plan. Warning has taken the larger role. But the good news is specifically لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (for people who believe)—for those who want to accept faith.

translation: It is He who created you from one soul and created from it its mate that he might dwell in security with her. And when he covers her, she carries a light burden and continues therein. And when it becomes heavy, they both invoke Allah, their Lord, "If You should give us a good [child], we will surely be among the grateful
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ وَجَعَلَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا لِيَسْكُنَ إِلَيْهَا ۖ فَلَمَّا تَغَشَّىٰهَا حَمَلَتْ حَمْلًا خَفِيفًا فَمَرَّتْ بِهِۦ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَثْقَلَت دَّعَوَا ٱللَّهَ رَبَّهُمَا لَئِنْ ءَاتَيْتَنَا صَـٰلِحًا لَّنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ

He is the One who created you from a single soul, and made from it its mate so that he might find tranquility with her. When he covered her, she carried a light burden and continued with it. But when she became heavy, they both called upon Allah ﷻ, their Master: "If You give us a righteous child, we will surely be among the grateful."

This ayah brings us back to the beginning of Surah Al-A'raf. The surah opened with the story of Adam (AS), and now near its conclusion, we return to that origin: خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ (He created you from a single soul). Allah ﷻ created Adam (AS), and وَجَعَلَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا (made from it its mate)—Hawwa, or Eve.

The purpose of this pairing is expressed beautifully: لِيَسْكُنَ إِلَيْهَا (so that he might find tranquility with her). The word سَكَن means peace, rest, tranquility. Notice the preposition إِلَيْهَا (toward her)—he finds peace in going toward her, in her presence. The spouse is given as a source of comfort and serenity.

The narrative then shifts to describe conception and pregnancy. تَغَشَّىٰهَا means he covered her—an expression of intimacy. حَمَلَتْ حَمْلًا خَفِيفًا (she carried a light burden)—in the earliest stages of pregnancy, the woman carries something so light she doesn't even realize it. فَمَرَّتْ بِهِۦ (she continued with it)—she went about her life carrying this without awareness.

Then فَلَمَّآ أَثْقَلَت (when she became heavy)—the same word used in the previous ayah for the heavens and earth being heavy with the Day of Judgment. The imagery connects: just as the skies and earth carry the Hour like a pregnant woman about to deliver, so too does the mother carry her child in those final stages. This is extraordinary literary coherence—the Quran ties its images together across ayahs in ways that are both subtle and striking.

When the pregnancy becomes difficult and the delivery approaches, دَّعَوَا ٱللَّهَ رَبَّهُمَا (they both called upon Allah ﷻ, their Master). Both parents turn to Allah ﷻ with a sincere plea: لَئِنْ ءَاتَيْتَنَا صَـٰلِحًا لَّنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (If You give us a righteous child, we will surely be among the grateful). They ask not just for a child, but for صَـٰلِحًا—a righteous, healthy, sound child.

translation: But when He gives them a good [child], they ascribe partners to Him concerning that which He has given them. Exalted is Allah above what they associate with Him
commentary:
فَلَمَّآ ءَاتَىٰهُمَا صَـٰلِحًا جَعَلَا لَهُۥ شُرَكَآءَ فِيمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمَا ۚ فَتَعَـٰلَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ

But when He granted them a righteous child, they attributed partners to Him concerning what He had given them. Exalted is Allah ﷻ above what they associate with Him.

This ayah describes the tragedy of shirk entering even into the blessing of childbirth. After Allah ﷻ grants the couple what they asked for—a healthy, righteous child—they turn around and جَعَلَا لَهُۥ شُرَكَآءَ (attribute partners to Him).

How does this manifest? One parent says, "We got this child because I visited that shrine." The other says, "No, I put this amulet on, and that's why we had a boy." Someone else claims, "It was written in your horoscope that you'd have a healthy son." Or the family tradition: "We're a family of boys, that's why we got one." These are all forms of shirk—attributing Allah's blessing to other sources.

The phrase فِيمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمَا (concerning what He had given them) emphasizes that the child was given by Allah ﷻ alone, yet they credit others. And unfortunately, this shirk doesn't stop there. Some parents, upon having a girl, express disappointment. "Another girl? I was hoping for a boy this time." This attitude—this preference that diminishes the value of daughters—is addressed directly in the Quran itself.

The ayah concludes with a powerful declaration: فَتَعَـٰلَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (Exalted is Allah ﷻ above what they associate with Him). Allah ﷻ is far above and beyond these associations people make. He is transcendent, perfect, and utterly separate from the partners they assign to Him.

translation: Do they associate with Him those who create nothing and they are [themselves] created
commentary:
أَيُشْرِكُونَ مَا لَا يَخْلُقُ شَيْـًٔا وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ

Do they associate with Him that which creates nothing, while they themselves are created? The rhetorical question exposes the absurdity of shirk. The objects of worship—whether idols, saints, or anything else people turn to besides Allah ﷻ—cannot create anything. مَا لَا يَخْلُقُ شَيْـًٔا (that which creates nothing) contrasts sharply with Allah ﷻ, who is Al-Khaliq, the Creator of all things.

Not only do these false objects of worship lack creative power, but وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ (they themselves are created). If someone worships a saint or a pious person, that individual is themselves a creation of Allah ﷻ. Even the angels, who are exalted beings, are creations. How can a creation be worthy of worship when only the Creator deserves it?

translation: And the false deities are unable to [give] them help, nor can they help themselves
commentary:
وَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ لَهُمْ نَصْرًا وَلَآ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَنصُرُونَ

And they cannot help them, nor can they help themselves. These false objects of worship are completely powerless. وَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ لَهُمْ نَصْرًا (they cannot help them)—they cannot assist those who call upon them in any way. Whether it's protection, provision, or guidance, these entities have no power to provide.

The second part is even more striking: وَلَآ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَنصُرُونَ (nor can they help themselves). Imagine the scene on the Day of Judgment. Great scholars like Abdul Qadir Jilani or Imam Ghazali (may Allah ﷻ have mercy on them)—people who spent their lives in service of Islam—will stand helpless as some people who worshiped their graves try to seek their intercession. These scholars will have no ability to help anyone, including themselves. They'll be asked, "Did you tell people to worship you?" And they'll desperately deny it, wanting nothing to do with this shirk that was committed in their name. It's a crime against them as much as it is against Allah ﷻ.

translation: And if you [believers] invite them to guidance, they will not follow you. It is all the same for you whether you invite them or you are silent
commentary:
وَإِن تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ لَا يَتَّبِعُوكُمْ ۚ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ أَدَعَوْتُمُوهُمْ أَمْ أَنتُمْ صَـٰمِتُونَ

And if you call them to guidance, they will not follow you. It is the same for you whether you call them or remain silent. This ayah addresses the reality that some people are so deeply entrenched in shirk that they become unreachable. سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ (It is the same for you) whether you invite them or stay quiet—they won't listen either way.

This might seem to contradict an earlier teaching in the surah, where those who remained silent about evil were condemned. The reconciliation is important: in that earlier passage, human beings were judging other human beings and declaring them hopeless cases, which they have no right to do. Here, it is Allah ﷻ speaking, and He knows what's inside hearts. He's informing the believers in Makkah about a particular group of mushrikeen who are beyond reach—not because dawah itself is futile, but because He knows their hearts are sealed. Humans don't have the authority to make that judgment, but Allah ﷻ does.

This can be understood as both a warning and, remarkably, a final favor. Allah ﷻ shifts from third person to second person here—addressing them directly. This direct address is itself an act of divine grace, one last attempt to shake them from their heedlessness.

translation: Indeed, those you [polytheists] call upon besides Allah are servants like you. So call upon them and let them respond to you, if you should be truthful
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ عِبَادٌ أَمْثَالُكُمْ ۖ فَٱدْعُوهُمْ فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

Indeed, those you call upon besides Allah ﷻ are servants just like you. So call upon them and let them respond to you, if you are truthful. The directness continues. Allah ﷻ addresses the mushrikeen, telling them that their objects of worship are عِبَادٌ أَمْثَالُكُمْ (servants just like you). They are slaves of Allah ﷻ, created beings with no independent power.

Then comes a challenge that contains both sarcasm and invitation: فَٱدْعُوهُمْ فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَكُمْ (So call upon them and let them respond to you). It's like telling a child who insists on doing something foolish, "Go ahead, do it. See what happens." This is reverse psychology with a serious purpose—go ahead, call on these partners you've invented. Let's see if they answer. إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (if you are truthful) in your claim that they have power.

translation: Do they have feet by which they walk? Or do they have hands by which they strike? Or do they have eyes by which they see? Or do they have ears by which they hear? Say, [O Muhammad], "Call your 'partners' and then conspire against me and give me no respite
commentary:
أَلَهُمْ أَرْجُلٌ يَمْشُونَ بِهَآ ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ أَيْدٍ يَبْطِشُونَ بِهَآ ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ يُبْصِرُونَ بِهَآ ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ ءَاذَانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَا ۗ قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ شُرَكَآءَكُمْ ثُمَّ كِيدُونِ فَلَا تُنظِرُونِ

Do they have feet with which they walk? Or do they have hands with which they strike? Or do they have eyes with which they see? Or do they have ears with which they hear? Say: "Call upon your partners, then plot against me and give me no respite."

This series of questions systematically dismantles the idols. أَلَهُمْ أَرْجُلٌ يَمْشُونَ بِهَآ (Do they have feet with which they walk?)—many idols were carved with enormous feet, exaggerated in size. But can they actually walk? Can they move toward you or away from danger?

أَمْ لَهُمْ أَيْدٍ يَبْطِشُونَ بِهَآ (Or do they have hands with which they strike?)—the idols often had massive hands, sculpted to look powerful. But يَبْطِشُونَ means to grab forcefully, to strike with strength. Can these hands actually grasp anything? Can they protect or defend?

أَمْ لَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ يُبْصِرُونَ بِهَآ (Or do they have eyes with which they see?)—in many pagan traditions, the eyes of idols are crafted in a way that seems to follow you wherever you stand. This creates an eerie feeling that the statue is watching. But يُبْصِرُونَ implies actual sight, real perception. Can they truly see?

أَمْ لَهُمْ ءَاذَانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَآ (Or do they have ears with which they hear?)—can these idols actually hear the prayers directed at them?

After thoroughly demolishing the idea that these idols have any real faculties, Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ to issue an extraordinary challenge: قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ شُرَكَآءَكُمْ (Say: Call upon your partners). Gather all your false gods together. ثُمَّ كِيدُونِ (then plot against me)—make a plan, all of you together, to destroy me. فَلَا تُنظِرُونِ (and give me no respite)—don't wait, don't delay. Do it now.

This echoes the legacy of Ibrahim (AS), who challenged his people in a similar way. The Prophet ﷺ stands before the Quraysh with absolute confidence: bring everything you have, call on every false god you worship, plan my destruction—and I'm telling you not to hold back. This is the confidence of someone who knows that Allah ﷻ is his protector.

translation: Indeed, my protector is Allah, who has sent down the Book; and He is an ally to the righteous
commentary:
إِنَّ وَلِـِّۧىَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ۖ وَهُوَ يَتَوَلَّى ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

Indeed, my protector is Allah ﷻ, who sent down the Book, and He protects the righteous. After issuing that bold challenge, the Prophet ﷺ explains the source of his confidence: إِنَّ وَلِـِّۧىَ ٱللَّهُ (my protector is Allah ﷻ). The word وَلِىّ means guardian, protector, close friend—someone who has your back completely.

Not just any protector, but ٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ (the One who sent down the Book). The same Allah ﷻ who revealed the Quran is the One protecting His messenger. This connection is significant—the Book and its messenger are under the same divine protection.

وَهُوَ يَتَوَلَّى ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (and He protects the righteous). This protection extends beyond the Prophet ﷺ to everyone who strives for righteousness. When you align yourself with Allah ﷻ and His commands, you place yourself under His guardianship. That's why the Prophet ﷺ can stand fearlessly before his enemies—he knows who his Protector is.

translation: And those you call upon besides Him are unable to help you, nor can they help themselves
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَكُمْ وَلَآ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَنصُرُونَ

And those you call upon besides Him cannot help you, nor can they help themselves. This is a repetition of what was stated earlier, and repetition in the Quran serves specific purposes. One key function is emphasis to people who are غَافِل (heedless), who aren't paying attention. It's like when you're absorbed in something—watching television, for example—and your mother calls you once, twice, three times. "Come here! Can't you hear me?" And you honestly didn't register it because you were in a state of ghaflah.

The mushrikeen are in that state. They need to be told again: these partners you call upon لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَكُمْ (cannot help you), وَلَآ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَنصُرُونَ (nor can they help themselves). So why would the Prophet ﷺ fear them when he's making his challenge? They're powerless entities being called upon by people who aren't even listening.

translation: And if you invite them to guidance, they do not hear; and you see them looking at you while they do not see
commentary:
وَإِن تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ لَا يَسْمَعُوا۟ ۖ وَتَرَىٰهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ وَهُمْ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ

And if you call them to guidance, they do not hear. And you see them looking at you, but they do not see. This ayah brings together three different words related to seeing, creating a powerful image of those who are physically present but mentally absent. لَا يَسْمَعُوا۟ (they do not hear)—they don't listen when called to guidance.

وَتَرَىٰهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ (you see them looking at you)—this is remarkable. You can see them, and they appear to be looking at you. They're staring, seemingly paying attention, giving you eye contact. But وَهُمْ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ (they do not see).

Three verbs of seeing are used here. The first, تَرَىٰ, is you observing them. The second, يَنظُرُونَ, is them appearing to look at you—it's the act of directing one's gaze. The third, يُبْصِرُونَ, is actual perception, true sight, understanding. It's used for seeing with the eye of intellect, not just the physical eye.

So the image is this: they're looking right at you, but they're not really seeing you. They're physically present but mentally checked out. Their eyes are open, but their hearts are blind. This is the state of ghaflah taken to its extreme—people who look like they're paying attention but have absorbed nothing.

translation: Take what is given freely, enjoin what is good, and turn away from the ignorant
commentary:
خُذِ ٱلْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِٱلْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ

Hold to forgiveness, command what is right, and turn away from the ignorant. After all the warnings, all the descriptions of stubborn disbelief, all the challenges issued to the mushrikeen, Allah ﷻ gives the Prophet ﷺ—and by extension, all believers—critical guidance on how to conduct themselves.

خُذِ ٱلْعَفْوَ (Hold to forgiveness)—العَفْو is pardoning, letting things go. It's not just forgiving major offenses; it's choosing not to hold onto minor slights. Someone says something rude, behaves inconsiderately, wrongs you in a small way—let it roll off. Don't carry it with you.

وَأْمُرْ بِٱلْعُرْفِ (command what is right)—continue calling to what is good, what is recognized as decent and correct. Even when people are being indecent toward you, you remain decent in your conduct.

وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (turn away from the ignorant)—الجَاهِلِينَ doesn't just mean those who lack knowledge; it refers to those who can't control their emotions, who become loud, obnoxious, and foolish in their behavior. When someone loses control and starts acting in an ignorant manner toward you, your response should be to simply turn away. Don't engage with that level of behavior.

This is a remarkable conclusion to all the intensity that came before. Allah ﷻ has the right to be angry at those who reject Him. His words can be severe, His warnings sharp. But the one delivering those words—the Prophet ﷺ—is commanded to remain gentle, forgiving, and patient. This creates a crucial distinction in Islam: Allah's anger is His alone. His judgments belong to Him. But the believers who carry His message must embody mercy, patience, and grace, even when speaking hard truths.

The Prophet ﷺ is being told: My words may be harsh, but your character remains beautiful. I own that anger—you don't. Continue to forgive, continue to call to good, and when people become ignorant in their behavior toward you, simply walk away. This is the balance that defines Islamic conduct: proclaiming truth with conviction while treating people with dignity and forbearance.

translation: And if an evil suggestion comes to you from Satan, then seek refuge in Allah. Indeed, He is Hearing and Knowing
commentary:
وَإِمَّا يَنزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ نَزْغٌ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ

*"And if an evil suggestion comes to you from Satan, then seek refuge in Allah. Indeed, He is Hearing and Knowing."*

This ayah addresses a critical reality in the work of calling people to Islam: Shaytan will not simply let the messenger walk away. Allah ﷻ has already instructed the Prophet ﷺ to show forgiveness, command what is good, and turn away from the ignorant. But what happens when the ignorant don't let you turn away? What happens when they keep following you, sticking to you, dragging you into conversations you shouldn't be in?

The word نَزْغ refers to being pulled or dragged into something by Shaytan. When people provoke you to the point where you might lose your temper, that's when you need to recognize what's happening. The moment a conversation becomes heated, understand who has won at that point—Shaytan has a huge victory in our religion when he makes us lose our composure.

Picture this scenario: you're trying to give sincere advice to your uncle, and he responds with the most absurd statements. Your blood boils. He says things that are completely wrong, and you want to say, "Where did you get that from? That's not even right!" At that precise moment, فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ—immediately seek Allah's protection. Don't respond in anger. Don't let Shaytan drag you further in.

Allah ﷻ concludes the ayah by reminding us: إِنَّهُۥ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ—"Indeed, He is Hearing and Knowing." He hears what was said to provoke you, and He knows your intention and your struggle to maintain control. This should give us comfort when we choose to step back rather than engage in heated arguments.

translation: Indeed, those who fear Allah - when an impulse touches them from Satan, they remember [Him] and at once they have insight
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ إِذَا مَسَّهُمْ طَـٰٓئِفٌ مِّنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ تَذَكَّرُوا۟ فَإِذَا هُم مُّبْصِرُونَ

*"Indeed, those who fear Allah—when an impulse touches them from Satan, they remember [Him] and at once they have insight."*

This ayah expands the lesson beyond the Prophet ﷺ to all believers. Allah ﷻ describes إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟—those who truly protect themselves, the people of taqwa. When طَـٰٓئِفٌ مِّنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ—a touch or whisper from Shaytan—reaches them, what do they do? تَذَكَّرُوا۟—they immediately remember Allah ﷻ.

What we're learning throughout these concluding passages is that the work of dawah will constantly be challenged by Shaytan. He will try to undo the work of the caller to Allah, beginning with the Prophet ﷺ himself, and then extending to all people of taqwa. When that whisper comes, when that provocation hits, the believers don't fall for it. They catch themselves.

فَإِذَا هُم مُّبْصِرُونَ—"and at once they have insight." They suddenly see clearly what's happening. They realize where that anger came from. They don't have to see Shaytan face to face to know he's there. They're smart enough to spot his work. This is remarkable—they can identify the unseen influence of Shaytan through their conscious awareness of Allah ﷻ.

This ability to recognize Shaytan's whispers and immediately turn to remembrance of Allah ﷻ is a defining characteristic of those with taqwa. It's a spiritual alertness that comes from constant connection with Allah ﷻ.

translation: But their brothers - the devils increase them in error; then they do not stop short
commentary:
وَإِخْوَٰنُهُمْ يَمُدُّونَهُمْ فِى ٱلْغَىِّ ثُمَّ لَا يُقْصِرُونَ

*"But their brothers—the devils increase them in error; then they do not stop short."*

This ayah presents the stark contrast. While the people of taqwa immediately recognize and resist Shaytan's influence, there are others—وَإِخْوَٰنُهُمْ, "their brothers"—meaning the brothers of the devils, those who have aligned themselves with Shaytan's cause. These forces يَمُدُّونَهُمْ فِى ٱلْغَىِّ—they extend them, they pull them further into deviation and misguidance.

ثُمَّ لَا يُقْصِرُونَ—"then they do not stop short." They're not holding back in any of their efforts. They're persistent. This teaches us something essential: you have people on the other side trying to do the exact opposite of what you're doing in calling to Allah ﷻ. They're working hard to pull people away from guidance. They're relentless in their efforts to promote deviation.

The implication is clear: if they're persistent in their efforts to mislead, you need to be equally persistent—if not more so—in your efforts to guide people to truth. The battle between guidance and misguidance is ongoing, and those working for misguidance don't take breaks. Neither should those calling to Allah ﷻ.

translation: And when you, [O Muhammad], do not bring them a sign, they say, "Why have you not contrived it?" Say, "I only follow what is revealed to me from my Lord. This [Qur'an] is enlightenment from your Lord and guidance and mercy for a people who believe
commentary:
وَإِذَا لَمْ تَأْتِهِم بِـَٔايَةٍ قَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا ٱجْتَبَيْتَهَا ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَتَّبِعُ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ مِن رَّبِّى ۚ هَـٰذَا بَصَآئِرُ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ

*"And when you do not bring them a sign, they say, 'Why have you not contrived it?' Say, 'I only follow what is revealed to me from my Lord. This [Quran] is enlightenment from your Lord and guidance and mercy for a people who believe.'"*

The disbelievers kept making demands: "Bring us a miraculous sign according to our specifications. Why didn't you choose something that would impress us? If you brought exactly what we wanted, we would accept your message." This was their game—constantly moving the goalposts, making new demands.

Allah ﷻ instructs the Prophet ﷺ to respond with clarity: قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَتَّبِعُ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ مِن رَّبِّى—"Say, 'I only follow what is revealed to me from my Lord.'" The Prophet ﷺ doesn't manufacture miracles on demand. He's not a performer doing tricks for an audience. He's a messenger delivering what Allah ﷻ has revealed.

Then comes the powerful declaration: هَـٰذَا بَصَآئِرُ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ—"This is enlightenment from your Lord." Notice the contrast being made here. They want something for their sight—physical miracles they can see with their eyes. But Allah ﷻ says the Quran is بَصَآئِرُ—insights, enlightenment for the mind and heart. Having sight is different from having insight. They're asking for external proof, but Allah ﷻ has given them something far more valuable: the ability to see truth with their hearts and minds.

وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ—"and guidance and mercy." Notice these aren't just continuations of the previous description. The sentence structure emphasizes that this Quran is overwhelming guidance, amazing mercy. But for whom? لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ—"for a people who believe." The Quran's guidance and mercy are accessible to those who approach it with faith, not to those who approach it with endless demands and closed hearts.

translation: So when the Qur'an is recited, then listen to it and pay attention that you may receive mercy
commentary:
وَإِذَا قُرِئَ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ فَٱسْتَمِعُوا۟ لَهُۥ وَأَنصِتُوا۟ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ

*"So when the Quran is recited, then listen to it and pay attention that you may receive mercy."*

This ayah is the direct response to what the Quraysh had said earlier in the surah: "Don't listen to this Quran, and when it's being recited, make noise and drown it out so the reciter stops." Allah ﷻ now commands the opposite: وَإِذَا قُرِئَ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ فَٱسْتَمِعُوا۟ لَهُۥ—"When the Quran is recited, listen to it carefully."

The word ٱسْتَمِعُوا۟ means to listen with full attention. It combines hearing with focus. But Allah ﷻ adds another layer: وَأَنصِتُوا۟—"and be silent." In Arabic, there are three words for silence, and all three appear in this surah. Here, أَنصِتُوا۟ means to be quiet while someone is speaking—don't talk back, don't interrupt, just be silent and absorb.

This has immediate practical applications. When the Quran is being recited—whether it's a recording playing, whether it's in the car, whether it's at home—you have two choices: be completely silent and listen, or turn it off. The Quran is not background music. You don't have a conversation while the Quran is playing in the background. That's a violation of the respect this Book deserves.

When the salah has started and the imam is reciting, you don't walk into the masjid and greet people. You don't say, "Hey, how are you doing?" No—the Quran is being recited. Be silent. The biggest training ground for this is salah itself. In salah, you're required to be silent and listen. This trains you for what should happen outside of salah too: whenever the Quran is recited, be quiet and give it your full attention.

I would extend this principle even further. When you're reading the Quran yourself, don't have the TV on. Don't check text messages while listening to a Quranic explanation. Just listen. The Quran deserves undivided attention.

لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ—"that you may receive mercy." In other words, if you know what's good for you, if you want Allah's mercy, you'll be quiet and attentive when you encounter the Quran.

translation: And remember your Lord within yourself in humility and in fear without being apparent in speech - in the mornings and the evenings. And do not be among the heedless
commentary:
وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ فِى نَفْسِكَ تَضَرُّعًا وَخِيفَةً وَدُونَ ٱلْجَهْرِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ بِٱلْغُدُوِّ وَٱلْـَٔاصَالِ وَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْغَـٰفِلِينَ

*"And remember your Lord within yourself in humility and in fear without being apparent in speech—in the mornings and the evenings. And do not be among the heedless."*

After commanding us to listen attentively when the Quran is recited aloud, Allah ﷻ now teaches us about the silent, internal remembrance of Him. وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ فِى نَفْسِكَ—"Remember your Lord within yourself."

This remembrance should be characterized by two qualities: تَضَرُّعًا وَخِيفَةً—humility and fear. تَضَرُّعًا captures a sense of powerlessness before Allah ﷻ, recognizing our complete dependence on Him. خِيفَةً is the awe and fear that should accompany our awareness of standing before the Master of the Universe.

وَدُونَ ٱلْجَهْرِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ—"without being apparent in speech." Don't even open your mouth for this type of remembrance. Yes, there's the recitation of Quran out loud, but there's also the profound internal dhikr where you don't need to move your tongue at all. This is the quiet, humble, fearful conversation happening inside between you and Allah ﷻ.

بِٱلْغُدُوِّ وَٱلْـَٔاصَالِ—"in the mornings and the evenings." The word بِٱلْغُدُوِّ is singular—the morning. But ٱلْـَٔاصَالِ i plural—the evenings, multiple shades of evening. One benefit of this phrasing is that it points to the prayer times: one morning prayer (Fajr), but multiple evening prayers (Dhuhr, Asr, Maghrib, Isha). Each shade of evening has its prayer.

وَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْغَـٰفِلِينَ—"And do not be among the heedless." How fitting—the remedy against heedlessness (ghaflah) is remembrance of Allah ﷻ. And what's the best form of that remembrance? The Quran itself, which was mentioned in the previous ayah.

These final ayahs address the two remaining problems that haven't yet been fully resolved in the surah's conclusion: heedlessness and arrogance.

translation: Indeed, those who are near your Lord are not prevented by arrogance from His worship, and they exalt Him, and to Him they prostrate
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ عِندَ رَبِّكَ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَيُسَبِّحُونَهُۥ وَلَهُۥ يَسْجُدُونَ ۩

*"Indeed, those who are near your Lord are not prevented by arrogance from His worship, and they exalt Him, and to Him they prostrate."*

The surah concludes by bringing us full circle. Remember how this story began? With Iblis refusing to make sajdah out of arrogance. Now, at the very end, Allah ﷻ describes those who are truly close to Him: إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ عِندَ رَبِّكَ—"those who are near your Lord"—meaning the angels and the righteous believers.

لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ—"they are not prevented by arrogance from His worship." They don't refuse to worship Allah ﷻ. They don't consider themselves above prostrating to Him.

وَيُسَبِّحُونَهُۥ—"and they exalt Him," declaring His perfection and glorifying Him.

وَلَهُۥ يَسْجُدُونَ—"and to Him they prostrate." This is the culmination. The surah that began with Iblis's refusal to prostrate ends with the description of true believers who willingly, humbly make sajdah to Allah ﷻ.

This ending weaves together the two diseases that can destroy us: arrogance (kibr) and heedlessness (ghaflah). These aren't just ancient problems mentioned for historical interest. They're living challenges we face every day.

## Reflections on Arrogance and Heedlessness in Our Lives

The disease of arrogance today often manifests through self-image. We become consumed with how we look, what people think of us, our impression on others. Remember, Iblis had no problem making sajdah to Allah ﷻ—he had done it many times before. His issue arose when he was commanded to prostrate in front of another creation. It became about comparison, about status relative to someone else.

When you're overly conscious of others around you, two things happen. First, you're constantly comparing yourself to them, wanting to ensure you're somehow better. Second, your preoccupation with others causes you to forget about Allah ﷻ. You lose sight of Him. These are the makings of arrogance.

The disease of heedlessness manifests through our addiction to entertainment and distraction. Today, that means endless scrolling, streaming services, social media, video games, YouTube videos, one movie after another. Think about it: people walk into a movie theater for a two or three-hour film, and they don't lose focus for even a second. Complete concentration.

But then you come to the masjid to pray two rakahs, and your mind wanders before you even finish Surah Al-Fatihah. Where am I? What day is it? What's for lunch? Who's this person next to me? This disparity reveals the hold that heedlessness has on our hearts.

Entertainment might start innocently—maybe it's harmless at first. But when ghaflah sets in and you're no longer conscious of Allah ﷻ, what happens? You're no longer fearful of Him. You're not appreciative of Him. And when those two things disappear, what floodgates open? Sin.

You're watching something, a commercial plays, you see an image you shouldn't see, and you don't change the channel. The heedlessness allows you to stay and watch. Every time you take in sin, there's a stain on your heart. More and more stains accumulate until the entire heart becomes covered in darkness. At that point, when you try to pray, nothing happens. You wonder why you feel spiritually dead.

You cannot remove that darkness without effort. We have to cleanse ourselves from addiction to distraction. We need to replace that time with remembrance of Allah ﷻ.

Consider this: you're exhausted at midnight after waking up at 5 AM. Your eyes are heavy, your body is crying for sleep, you're about to collapse. Then someone mentions a new movie, and suddenly you have energy. You'll stay up and finish the entire film. Someone says there's food downstairs, and you'll run down to get it. But when it's time for one quick prayer, suddenly there's no energy. "Let me lie down for a second. I'll pray when I wake up." Then you wake up at 10 AM and feel bad—but only for about five minutes. Then it's "What are we doing tonight?" and the cycle continues.

The battle against heedlessness is largely a battle against what we do at night. Fix your nights, fix your mornings, and the rest will fall into place. Wake up early. Go to sleep early. Don't allow that prime time when Shaytan is most active to be the time when you make your worst decisions.

If you're addicted to entertainment, don't go home and be alone with your devices. Stay at the masjid. Study. Keep yourself busy with something productive. Find work to do. For young people especially, there should be no such thing as free time. Free time is like poison. It's the gateway to spiritual death.

We need to instill habits in our lives that fight Shaytan constantly, because he's working against us constantly. Remember, he worked on Adam (AS) over a long period, drilling and drilling until he finally succeeded in making him slip. If someone is working against you persistently, you have to fight back persistently.

This is the living message of Surah Al-A'raf. Yes, there was a conversation happening with the Quraysh back then. But simultaneously, this is a conversation happening with all of us today. May Allah ﷻ make us people of constant remembrance and protect us from becoming people of heedlessness. May He save us from arrogance and grant us the humility to prostrate before Him with full consciousness and presence of heart.
